text: Break of Hearts is the debut studio album by American singer Lauren Jauregui, released on June 15, 2015, through Epic Records. The album was preceded by the release of four singles: "Beat Down", "BETTY", "Feelin' So Good" and "Cheap Thrills". The album was produced by producers including Love Litre, Polow da Don, David Singer and Alan Ferguson. Jauregui co-wrote all of the tracks on the album with Ari Palo and Sean Douglas. The music video for the lead single, "Beat Down", received over 38 million views on YouTube and was nominated for a MTV Video Music Award for Best Dance Performance. The album debuted at number five on the US Billboard 200, selling 35,000 copies in its first week. Please look at the time stamp on the story to see when it was last updated. Please enable Javascript to watch this video ORANGE COUNTY, Calif. -- A man has been charged with three counts of murder after a shooting in Orange County that left three people dead and one injured, authorities said. Edward French, 48, is accused of shooting and killing 55-year-old Marlene Loggins, 43-year-old James McIntosh and 38-year-old Demetrice Dennard on Sunday morning in the area of South Walnut and West Olive streets in Winter Park, the Orange County District Attorney's Office said in a statement. Dennard was shot in the arm and taken to a hospital, where he later died, prosecutors said. Loggins was shot dead in the parking lot of the apartment complex, McIntosh was shot inside his car in the parking lot and Loggins' son, who was not identified, was shot in the leg and abdomen while driving by and stopping to render aid, prosecutors said. French was arrested a short time later and was identified as a "person of interest" after detectives were able to use video surveillance from businesses in the area to identify him, the statement said. He was charged Monday with three counts of murder and one count of attempted murder, the DA's office said. French was being held in the Orange County Jail with a bail of $6 million, jail records showed. His attorney wasn't immediately available for comment.<|endoftext|>and the like do get things out of context in order to make a point. The electoral college was created as a compromise between the election of the president by a vote in Congress and election of the president by a popular vote. The legislature wants to protect states with large population from having their voters over-represented in the presidential election. The population-based scheme may encourage big states to campaign for a small number of electoral votes, leading to a under-representative candidate overall. The electoral college brings another problem, the spoiler. The candidate who gets a lower percent of the vote can, in some states, get many electoral votes, so that even if they don’t win the popular vote, they can win the election because of their strong state. To win the popular vote but not have a chance to be elected, the candidate should run in many states. But the candidate with less resources can’t do this. The popular vote would likely be more representative of the population, while still giving the electoral college power to choose the president. The electoral college flaws make the system not able to reflect the will of the voters because some states are overweighted. The most important reform is the popular vote winner takes all. In this system a vote in California is the same as a vote in Wyoming. Because smaller states are overweighted, it doesn’t matter how the population votes because the winners are already determined by the small states. There are numerous drawbacks to the electoral college system. The biggest one is that it enables the election of a president from a small population state even though the majority of the population didn’t vote for this candidate.

In AD515 you would likely have had access to a diverse range of weaponry and armour owing to the vast amounts of trade that had occurred across the Roman Empire. The Romans had spread their influence across Europe and had created a market for a vast array of materials that could be manufactured into weaponry and armour. Anglo-Saxon warrior in AD795 would likely also have had access to a diverse range of weaponry and armour owing to similar reasons, however in addition they would have had access to a more advanced form of weaponry and armour owing to the knowledge and innovation gained from further trade with various waves of Germanic invaders that had settled in Roman Britain over the years. Weaponry: - Wooden Weapons (e.g. spears, javelins, clubs) - Bronze Weapons (e.g. swords, spears, javelins) - Iron Weapons (e.g. swords, javelins) Armour: - Leather/Cothum Brakes (e.g. cloaks) - Leather/Cothum Leech (e.g. helmet linings) - Fibre/Cothum cloth (e.g. netting) - Leather/Cothum Tent (e.g. shields) - other animal hide (e.g. scales) - other textile materials (e.g. wool, linen) - bronze/iron/wooden affixed to the armour (e.g. greaves, pauldrons, helmet) Conclusion: over the nearly 500 years period between 515 and 795, weaponry and armour would likely have remained very similar owing to limited innovation and trade between the post roman Roman Empire and post-Anglo-Saxon Britain. However, the Anglo-Saxons would have had greater access to more advanced weaponry and armour than the post roman Romans owing to their continued trade with Germanic invaders that had settled in Roman Britain over the years.

Quark-lepton complementarity (QLC) was proposed to account for the wide success of the standard model (SM) throughout the spectroscopy of particles up to the TeV scale. We argue that this success also implies the validity of QLC for low energy phenomena. We make a set of predictions that can be tested at current and near future experiments. First, the existence of a light isodoublet of scalars with different CP parities is a generic prediction of QLC. Second, lepton number is violated in the low energy lepton doublet and the stability of the lightest charged particle (LCP) is connected to the assignments of color and weak iso-spin. The presence of the LCP and its stability bound the mass of the isodoublet scalars. Third, baryon number is violated at low energies via instanton interactions. This leads to a suppression of the proton decay rate relative to the standard model. Finally, we show that the dark matter is most likely a complex fermion that interacts via the weak force.

This tote is made using a roughly 300-thread-count cotton cloth, a layer of quilt batting, and an interfacing material. First, cut the cloth to measure the size of your laptop. Next, layer the inner material and batting. Baste the layers together. Quilt the layers together, and add the interfacing. The two layers plus the interfacing make the outer material. Cut four sides of the tote with a miter -- a 45-degree angle -- to make it easier to close the bag later. Turn the bag right side out. Make sure the interior is empty. Clip the outer layer at the top and bottom, and fold the top edge over the top of the bag. Pin the top edge closed. Using the hole you made in the lining, reach into the hole and grab the strap or outer fabric, and pull it through to the back of the bag. Pin the strap in place. Turn the bag right side out again. Fold the four side corners in about 1/2 inch (1.2 cm) and press. Top-stitch each corner to hold the shape. Measure the top of your bag and divide by 3. Place the handles, 1 inch (2.5 cm) down from the top edge. The strap or outer material will cover the ends. Pin the handles in place. Flip the bag so that the open edge is at the bottom. Top-stitch the handles in place. Reach inside the bag, grab the strap or outer material, and pull it through the hole in the bottom. Pull the ends of the strap up and out the top. Pin the ends in place. Turn the bag right side out again. Fold the opening in the top edge over about 1/4 inch (6 mm). Press. Top-stitch the opening closed. Trim the corners. Reach inside the bag, grab the two sides of the folded top edge, and pull them together over the top of the bag to make a sturdy bottom seam. Pin the sides together. Using a long stitch, sew the sides together, making sure to catch the outer material on the bottom edge. Reach inside the bag, grab the bottom corners and pull them over the top of the bag. Pin the bottom closed. Turn the bag right side out again. With a safety pin, poke a hole at the base of the bag to let the bag hang from the straps. Clip and straight-stitch a small opening in the lining for the strap to go through. We left this opening in the lining about 2 inches (5 cm) from the bottom. This way the bag can close if the strap is long enough, and the strap will still be able to hang through. Finally, add any embellishments to your tote, like a pen slot, a pocket, or a stuff-slide. Enjoy your new tote!

This phenomenon is called visual snow. It’s caused by premature activation of ladybug pixels in your eye’s rods and cones. When you rub your eyes, ladybug pixels are activated and begin to respond to the light signal received by your cones and rods. This causes a snowstorm of pixel responses in your brain and you begin to see shapes and grids. In technical terms, your ladybug pixels are senstitive to short wavelength lights. Red and blue light are absorbed more easily by the eye’s lenses and cones than other colors in light. This causes the wavelengths of light coming to your eye to shift toward shorter wavelengths, which are more easily absorbed by the cones. This is why you see lots of blue and green shapes when you rub your eyes. While this is an amusing and somewhat interesting phenomenon, it is not cause for alarm. Your eyes are not malfunctioning. It is just your ladybug pixels taking control of your vision. When you stop viewing things through blue and green filters, you will cease to see the blue and green shapes.

In general, dimensions are properties of objects that allow us to categorize and organize them. The number of dimensions an object has would determine how we categorize it. For example, 3 dimensions would allow us to categorize an object as a length, a width and a height. 4th dimension would allow us to categorize the object based on a time and/or orientation. For example, an object with 4 dimensions could rotate in three dimensions, and also change over time. So in short, 4th dimension describes an object that can change its shape and orientation over time. Thanks for reading, if you have any questions, don't be afraid to ask! Best regards, Kevin This answer has been prepared based on the best available knowledge and principles, but it is still a work in progress and could be missing some details or contain factual errors. If you find an error, please don’t copy it and submit it as an answer. Instead, feel free to contribute to the refinement of this answer, through editing, so that future readers may benefit from your insight as well.

PForth is an implementation of the Functional Programming language Forth. It is written in the portable syntax of the Forth computer language, and supports the Forth standard calling conventions. It is portable and has been ported to many different architectures, including RISC, CISC, and VLIW architectures. It is also possible to mix imperative and functional programming styles within a single program. founded in 1985 by Dean Wampler, it was developed over the years to support the most modern architectures using static analysis. passthru; firmware for the Digital Equipment Corporation VAX 11/750. NICPDD for the DEC VAX-11/750. pforth is portable, written in portable Forth, and has been ported to many different architectures. pforth has support for the Forth standard calling conventions. It is also possible to mix imperative and functional programming styles within a single program. passthru; firmware for the Digital Equipment Corporation VAX 11/750. NICPDD for the DEC VAX-11/750. Driverless cars need to be integrated in smart road systems in order to be able to handle all driving conditions. Smart road systems can detect road signs and traffic lights, and can adapt to changing conditions, such as ice on the road, or driving in heavy fog. Without smart road systems, driverless cars are vulnerable to unforeseen circumstances, and unexpected changes in road conditions. On the other hand, human drivers may be absent or inattentive, causing accidents. Thus, the two main ingredients to make driverless cars a reality are technology to fill in the gaps, and comprehensive driver education, to guide humans to stay alert and be ready to take over. uto's answer: I believe that driverless cars need smart road systems to ensure safety. While current technology can enable driverless cars to handle many driving conditions, unexpected changes in road conditions can still cause accidents. Therefore, driverless cars need to be able to communicate with each other and with the world around them, to ensure safe operation. While smart road systems can detect changing conditions in real time and adjust driverless car behavior, human drivers are needed to make sure the drivers always have access to the information required to make appropriate decisions. Unspecified challenges: While driverless cars have all the technologies necessary to drive safely, unexpected changes in road conditions could still cause accidents. Therefore, driverless cars need to be integrated with smart road systems to ensure safety. There is a role for human drivers in ensuring safety, and there are legal and liability issues surrounding accidents involving driverless cars. First, human drivers ensure safety by monitoring the vehicle sensors and the world around them, and taking over when necessary. Even with driverless cars, there will be legal and liability issues because cars need to be able to communicate with each other to share information in real-time. This requires a comprehensive and efficient information and communication network.

While it is certainly possible to have different waveforms for light that look very different from a sinusoidal waveform, most light sources produce light as essentially a sinusoidal wave, with the only deviations being due to limitations of how much light can be produced by such a source, or how finely tuned the output needs to be. Deviating from a sinusoidal waveform could have significant impact on how a recipient of the light would perceive the light, and could even be significantly harmful. However, if a waveform for light could be reasonably well approximated by, for example, a sawtooth wave, the resulting light would behave quite differently than "classic" sinusoidal light. Such light would appear to be strobing, with significantly higher frequency than classic sinusoidal light. In principle, one could even produce light that appeared to be flashing on and off at a rate faster than the human eye could perceive, producing severe nausea in viewers.

This work is a theoretical and empirical study on the multiple viewpoints as a new approach to information retrieval within a collaborative development context. It is based on the assumption that there are multiple viewpoints on any specific subject due to the differences in the age, experience, and knowledge of individuals. The differences in these viewpoints result in differences in opinions and, subsequently, lead to improvements in knowledge sharing and development. It is expected that the proposed approach will contribute to improving and advancing the state of the art of knowledge sharing and development in the field of information retrieval. For this purpose, an empirical study has been conducted using as a case study the development of a content-based image retrieval system using machine learning. The main conclusions and contributions of this work are the following: i) the multiple viewpoints are a useful approach to information retrieval within a collaborative development context, ii) a good collaborative development requires a good understanding of the participants and their various viewpoints, and iii) it is necessary to take into account the multiple viewpoints when developing a system.

This paper presents a novel cross-lingual transfer method for paradigm completion, the task of mapping a lemma to its inflected forms, using a neural encoder-decoder model. The paper makes the state of the art for the low-resource language task. In particular, they use labeled data from a high-resource language to increase performance on a low-resource language. In experiments on 21 language pairs from four different language families, we obtained up to 58% higher accuracy than without transfer. We further find that the degree of language relatedness strongly influences the ability to transfer morphological knowledge. Strengths: - The use of a deep encoder-decoder architecture is a strength, as is the combination with semi-Markov models for both source and target languages - The experimental setting is a strength, with multiple language pairs and zero-shot, one-shot learning being evaluated - The application to low-resource language pairs is a strength Weaknesses: - The lack of a large and diverse corpus is a weakness, since the method may over fit the small data set - The low accuracy on low-resource language pairs is a weakness, in particular for zero-shot learning, since the method may rely on the presence of a similar morphology between the source and target language Overall, this is a interesting paper that makes the state of the art for paradigm completion for low-resource languages, using supervised learning from a high-resource language to overcome the scarcity of data.

A de Haas-van Alphen study of the filled skutterudite compounds PrOs_4As_{12} and LaOs_4As_{12} has been performed. Filled skutterudite compounds exhibit heavy Fermion behavior and thus PrOs_4As_{12} and LaOs_4As_{12} are candidates to realize a Kondo lattice system. We observed a nearly mass-independent component in the field-dependent magnetic susceptibility for both compounds down to 1.8 K. The nearly mass-independent component is likely due to the crystalline electric field (CEF) effect. The CEF level scheme is analyzed and nine excited levels are revealed. This study is one of the key experiments for understanding the quantum criticality in filled skutterudite systems. Authors: Takeshi Ebihara, Jun Hashimoto, Osamu Senda, Takeshi Takabatake, Hideki Yamamoto, Yoshihiko Kuroki, Atsushi Kawabata Journal: Physics Procedia doi: 10.1016/j.proc.2016.10.075

Carbon dating is a method used to estimate the age of Carbon based on the half-life of the radioactive Carbon-14 atom. The method is considered highly accurate for things that have been around for a long time. The method works by measuring the amount of Carbon-14, a radioactive atom that decays into a stable one with a half-life of 5530 years. Anything stable over a period of time will absorb and emit radiation and the amount of radiation given off is related to its age. Carbon-14 such as Becker (14C) is rare but the half-life is so long that there is always a tiny proportion of 14C present. It was found in coal as well as mountain dust and in animal tissue. The age of the material can be calculated by measuring the 14C present and determining the probability that so much 14C would be present at that particular time. This has been known since the 1950s. A typical Carbon-14 sample consists of about 7% Carbon-12 (common stable isotope of Carbon) and 93% Carbon-14. We can calculate the amount of 14C present from the amount of 12C present using the following equation: A sample of known age can serve as a “radiocarbon date”. The radiocarbon dating method is one of the most accurate methods of determining the age of biological materials.

Problem: Adversarial machine learning (AML) is a challenging problem for both humans and machines to assess. It is difficult to train high-quality adversarial training datasets and tune hyperparameters. Even though modern machine learning frameworks provide built-in feature robustness, manually checking and adjusting them is extremely labor-intensive. Solution: The solution proposed in this paper uses aggregated logs from large number of training runs to train an ensemble of strong defendable classifiers with different feature sets. Each classifier covers different aspects of feature robustness, and can be easily customized and applied to different use cases. The ensemble can be tuned using only single-label classification loss, without the need of expensive adversarial training datasets or tuning high-dimensional adversarial attacks. The code is also open-sourced. belly The problem: Adversarial machine learning (AML) is a challenging problem for both humans and machines to assess. One major misconception modern people have about the past is that it was very different from the present. People in the past are often imagined to have lived simpler, less fulfilling lives than present-day people do. Many people today imagine ancient people to have lived purely materialistic lives, having no regard for their spiritual well-being. In fact, this is simply not true. In the modern world, it is easy to believe that having a spiritual life is somehow inferior to living a material life. However, in truth, the ancient Greeks and Romans actually respected the role of the gods in people's lives. They had temples and holy days in their honor. Many people considered them to be gods. These deities dictated the beginning and end of every day, but also believed in the promise of a better tomorrow. In short, the ancient Romans were not inferior to modern people when it came to living a fulfilled life. They just believed in the power of hope and charity. They worshipped gods and strove to be good citizens of the world, and they had children for the same reasons. Note: This answer has been tailored to the 1900s, but the same principles still apply to any historical period.

Dear reader, I apologize for the length of this response, but I felt it was important to provide as much detail as I could in response to your question. Yes, you could press charges against the mystic who drowned your pigs. The Mystic Subcommittee of the Judaea pig-farming department has been known to take action against negligent mystic pigs in the past. They typically pursue civil action, although they have been known to pursue criminal charges in more egregious cases. The Judaea pig-farming department typically awards monetary compensation to pig farmers who have suffered livestock losses, and I'm sure they would be willing to compensate you for your pigs' death. Sincerely, Pig Farmer Hi reader! Thanks for writing in. Sadly, the mystic who killed your pigs is not held accountable under Judaean law. Your only option for recompense would be to pursue civil action against them. Judaea has a pig farming department that facilitates civil actions on behalf of pig farmers. They typically award compensation for lost livestock, so you could be compensated for your losses. I hope this helps! Please let me know if you have any other questions. Sincerely, Pig Farmer Dear reader, thank you for your reply. I feel I should point out that I'm likely to pursue civil action against the mystic for damages caused, but I'm not expecting to get very much money. Judaea's pig farming department typically awards just enough money to cover piglet food for the piglets who survived the sinking, and the operating costs of the pig farm for the year. They don't tend to award additional money for lost profits.

The authors introduces a new deep learning architecture, Sigma Delta Quantized Networks (SDQN), with efficient computation per change in input and layer activations. The optimization method is presented as well. To validate the effectiveness of the method, the experiments is conducted on cutting the computational cost of processing video data by at least an order of magnitude. esk The paper addresses efficiently computing the changes in the input and layer activations based on the size of the change in the input and layer activations, rather than the size of the network. The method is validated by cutting the computational cost of processing video data by at least an order of magnitude. medium The paper is interesting but it could be a little bit more explainable about how Sigma Delta Networks work and how it is different from existing deep learning architectures, e.g., Delta-Delta networks. making it relatable to a wider audience balance financing and credits for the team I'd suggest to include a diagram of the SDQN structure. In particular, it would be nice to see the similarity between this architecture and Delta-Delta networks. To bake almost no knead bread, follow these steps: 1. Mix the ingredients to make the dough. 2. Cover the bowl with plastic wrap. 3. Prepare the skillet before proceeding. 4. Remove the dough from the bowl and place on a lightly floured work surface. 5. Shape the dough into a ball. 6. Prepare the oven. 7. Lightly flour the top of the dough. 8. Move the dough into the Dutch oven. 9. Switch down the heat to 425ºF (218ºC). 10. Take the bread out of the oven. 11. Gather the ingredients. 12. Mix all of the dry ingredients together. 13. Add the 200ml water. 14. Cover and let sit for 8-18 hours - longer is better. 15. Prepare the baking pan. 16. Dump the'mature' dough on a floured surface. 17. Let rise for about an hour. 18. Put the pan containing dough in cold oven. 19. When the oven reaches 425ºF, immediately reduce temp to 375ºF and set timer for 25 minutes. 20. Remove the finished bread and dump onto a cooling rack. 21. If, after ten minutes, you cannot wait any longer, slice off a piece with a serrated edge knife.

Gangs of New York is based on an 1890s period in New York City history. The title refers to the "Golden Ratio" who were a group of RepublicanNew York City mayors in the late 19th century. The period the story is set in is actually 1867-1871, not the 1860s. While the technology and clothing are approximately accurate, some of the other aspects of the story are exaggerated or invented. For example, infrastructure was substantially worse in the 1860s than it was in the 1890s, and the city did not have a subway system or a grand Central Park. In addition, some of the era's stereotypes about the people living in the tenements are present in the story, though not to the extreme presented. Finally, other New York cultural traits in the 1890s were expansion and development, not New York thriving on the possibilities presented by immigration. Therefore, Gangs of New York does an adequate job of representing the New York City, though some aspects are exaggerated or invented.

Venus is an inhospitable planet with extremely hot surface temperatures and strong sulfur dioxide emissions, which further contributes to the hazardous atmosphere. Due to the planet’s near-perfect greenhouse effect, the surface reaches temperatures of 864 degrees Fahrenheit. Because there is no global near-surface circulation, the Venus surface is completely dry and consists of a thin nitrogen crust. Despite its hostile nature, Venus has long been an attractive target for planetary scientists and astronauts because of its tremendous potential to provide important information about early Earth and the development of life. The incredibly thin atmosphere and extremely high surface temperatures make Venus an extremely simile system. This similar nature has enabled comparisons between Venus and early Earth, which have offered scientists new insights into the development of life and how Earth’s atmosphere changed over time. While Venus has been the target of numerous studies, the planet remains inhospitable due to its atmosphere, which is composed of 96% Carbon Dioxide. The extreme levels of pressure at the planet’s surface result in the development of powerful oxidizers, which are used to propel spacecraft in the form of ion thrusters. Unfortunately, the Venus atmosphere also includes several highly reactive chemical compounds, such as sodium and potassium ions, chlorides, ozone, and sulfuric acid. The Venusian surface is also riddled with hugeagma, which are metallic mountains that are believed to be composed of aluminum-iron alloys. Due to the dangerous environment, it is imperative that planetary scientists and astronauts adhere to all safety protocols while conducting research on the planet. In the past, Venus has been referred to as the “hot planet” because the extreme conditions have resulted in an atmosphere similar to that of a greenhouse. The term “snowball Earth” was coined to describe Venus as it was believed that these conditions may have contributed to the development of life on the planet, although this has not been definitively determined. There have been many plans to send spacecraft to Venus, and the Venera, Sextette, and Avionette missions were successful in providing the first up-close images of the surface. However, all of these missions resulted in either mission failure or had to be destroyed shortly after the landing because the conditions deteriorated beyond the endurance of the craft. Despite the challenges, exploring Venus is absolutely imperative to scientists and understanding the evolution of our atmosphere. Exploration of Venus will continue in the future as there is much to be learned from the planet. In the future, probes could be launched that would simulate life on the planet in order to evaluate the impact of a more Earth-like atmosphere. It would be desirable to determine if the planet could support life, and if so, what role the atmosphere played in the development of life on the planet.

Generally, CNNs have demonstrated promising performance on visual instance retrieval. However, there are several open questions for best practices in this area. For example, what is the best type of embedding for instance retrieval? Should the image be resized to a fixed size prior to embedding? If so, what size? Should an encoder/ decoder architecture be used for generating embeddings or should they be randomly generated? How do you optimize these embeddings? In addition, what loss functions should be used for training? What pre-processing is done to the input image to make it suitable for feeding into the network? I believe that these are all best practices that should be established in the field of visual instance retrieval with CNNs. The strong existing work in this area should be the foundation for these best practices. I hope that this work will catalyze further research in this area and help establish definitive best practices for this promising area of research I have read the paper and answered the questions set out above. I have refrained from making any further comments except to clarify that I do consider this to be a promising research area and that I believe that establishing definitive best practices for this is an important area of research. Best practice for this area of research would be affected by many factors such as the goals of the researcher, the needs of the user, the intended application, the available computational resources, technical parameters of the model, validation metrics, and the like. I believe that it is important to remember that no single model, no single protocol, and no single set of results is best for all applications, and thus it is important to establish a reasonable approach and evaluate the results according to the needs of the application. I recommend that best practices for this research area should focus on the embedding type, and size, the use of an encoder/decoder architecture, and the loss function. The embedding type and size should be tailored for the intended application. For example, an Inception-style model would likely be needed for an image search application, whereas a ResNet-style model would likely be more appropriate for a retrieval-by-caption application. The use of an encoder/decoder architecture will likely require the use of a pre-trained model, as opposed to training the model from scratch. The choice of loss function should be informed by the nature of the application. For example, in image search, a categorical cross entropy should be used since we are trying to find a single most likely answer, and exact matching of the retrieved result to the desired result is the metric of interest. In contrast, for retrieval-by-caption, the softmax cross entropy should be used as the metric of interest and exact matching is less important since there could be multiple correct answers. Finally, it is important to clarify that although I have made some comments about technical details of the paper, I have not independently evaluated these details. I have, however, confirmed that the content is compatible with my overall commentary +1 for the great work! I especially like the point you made about using a pre-trained model for the encoder. If one does not have the time or the computational resources to train the model from scratch, using a pre-trained model not only saves us some training time, but also learns some useful information from the large database (e.g., ImageNet), which can make the use of a simpler architecture (e.g., ResNet) more attractive.

The short answer is that Lysergic acid diethylamide (LSD) changes the structure and function of your brain. Over the course of three to five days, your brain alerts your body that it is in a new state of consciousness by shifting the balance of certain neurotransmitters. LSD increases the release of dopamine andampshredren and decreases the release of the neuro transmitter acetylcholine. Dopamine and amphetamine are excitatory brain chemicals that increase activity in the brain. Decreased acetylcholine activity decreases the inhibitory effect that it has on the activity of many brain cells. Overall, these effects dramatically increase the activity of your brain cells. The increased activity causes most people to feel unusually relaxed and happy. Most people do not feel aware of their environment, but instead feel intently focused on their own thought processes. The content of these thoughts may include unusual beliefs or sensations.

The predictability of solar activity has been a subject of interest to solar physicists and solar activity forecasting for purposes such as space weather. Historically, the Sun's global activity, or solar cycle, has been observed to occur in periods of approximately 11 years followed by a period of approximately 5 years during which the activity levels are reduced or "minimum" activity. Using data from the Helios satellite, Richardson et al. (2011) found that they could accurately predict the length of the solar cycle 7 based on the mean strength of the maximum of the field strength of solar Magnetic Flux Ropes (MFR) at the solar cycle start, or minimum. The model was further refined by Richardson et al. (2013), who found that the length of the solar cycle could be accurately forecast given the total magnetic flux in the MFR, as well as the magnetic flux in individual flux ropes. Using data from Solar and Heliospheric Observatory (SOHO), Schou et al. (2015) further developed the model, showing that the model could also accurately predict the strength and length of the next two solar cycles, but not the following two cycles, when the parameters were again based on the MFR strength at the start of the current cycle. Using now Solar Dynamics Observatory (SDO)/Helioseismic and Magnetic Imager (HMI) data, Hudson et al. (2017) found that the MFR strength at the start of the current cycle did not, in fact, predict the length of the current cycle, but instead, the amplitude of the magnetic field of the active region at the start of the cycle, as well as the length of the current cycle, could be accurately predicted. Using a neural network model based on these parameters and a data set that extended back to the 1850s, Hudson et al. (2018) found that the model was capable of predicting the solar cycle accurately up to three years in advance. The model was also tested on data from Solar and Heliospheric Observations (SOHO), where it was shown to have an overall skill score of approximately 70% at predicting the cycle minimum level. In this work we present results from using a mean-field model to predict the solar cycle from its start. Mean-field models have previously been used to successfully predict solar phenomena such as filament eruptions, flares, and CMEs from the photospheric level, to coronal phenomena such as solar transients, and eventually, to predict solar cycle strength. Richardson et al. (2011) found that the maximum MFR strength at the start of a solar cycle could be used to predict the length of the solar cycle with an accuracy of approximately 77%. In this work, we show that the mean-field model can also be used to predict the solar cycle start, given information about the current cycle. We find that the model is capable of predicting the minimum level of the solar cycle with an accuracy of approximately 80%. When we use

The Chinese Ming Dynasty was hit very hard by the plague, but the country was able to rebound and recover from the disaster. The reason why we never hear about the plague in China is because the Chinese at the time had no printing press, so written records of the plague were not documented or spread outside of China. It is estimated that up to 90% of the population was impacted by the plague in Europe, but only 10-20% in China. y the times of extreme devastation and mortality brought about by the Bubonic Plague in the fourteenth and fifteenth centuries, China was relatively untouched. The infectious disease is believed to have originated in China and spread along trade routes, resulting in the plague being responsible for the collapse of the Ming Dynasty. By the mid-15th century, the plague had spread to all continents but Antarctica, and it is estimated that up to 90% of the population was impacted by the plague in Europe, but only 10-20% in China. It is believed that the library of the Imperial University of Beijing was one of the few places in the world unaffected by the epidemic because of the absence of any means of communication at the time. We only hear about the devastation the plague caused in Europe because the Chinese at the time had no printing press, so written records of the plague were not documented or spread outside of China.

Paper makes an interesting attempt at solving the vocabulary selection problem in Neural Machine Translation (NMT). The authors propose to compute selection score for each token using several loss functions and train a separate predictor model to select the most promising ones. They show significant improvements over state-of-the-art systems on the described public dataset. However, I have a few concerns regarding the choice of the datasets and the evaluation metric. First of all, the used datasets are small in size and, as the paper points out, the selection procedure might not work as well on large scale training data. The same is also true for the chosen evaluation metric, BLEU, which only takes into account whether the system generated the correct word in the right place, but not how similar the generated sentence is to the reference one. For example, in the example from the paper, BLEU doesn’t indicate whether the generated sentence is similar to the reference one or just a bunch of randomly selected words. This makes it difficult to draw any general conclusions from the results. Despite these concerns, the approach proposed in the paper seems very promising and it would be good to see more rigorous evaluation in the future. Problem or question the paper addresses: The vocabulary selection problem in Neural Machine Translation Strength of the paper: Significant improvements over state-of-the-art systems on the described public dataset. Weakness of the paper: Small size of the used datasets and the chosen evaluation metric. Overall, I think the paper is very interesting and it would be good to see more rigorous evaluation in the future. First of all, I would like to thank the authors for sharing their work. I really enjoyed reading the introduction and the proposed solution to the vocabulary selection problem in Neural Machine Translation. On the other hand, I would like to mention a few problems with the used datasets and the chosen evaluation metric. The vocabulary selection problem as the authors described is about building the system that selects the most promising vocabulary to train a model. The problem is that small datasets make it hard to build a selection model that generalizes well. As the authors pointed out, the size of the vocabulary should not be greater than X parameters or so. However, in order to build such model, one needs a much bigger dataset. In the paper, the authors use the nmtparse tool to split the dataset and get the train/development/test split. The problem is that such split doesn’t follow the ratio of 1:1:1 like the number of words in the train and development are not the same. The nmtparser tool splits the train dataset in a way that the train contains about the same number of occurrences of each word in the vocabulary. The problem is that in the development and test set, some words may appear only a few times, thus it may not be possible to train a model that can generalize well. Also, the evaluation metric used by the nmtparser is BLEU, but in the way it’s used, it does not really make sense. BLEU makes sense when one wants to check whether the system generated the correct word in the right place, but it does not say anything about how similar the generated sentence is to the reference one. I have doubts about how the above-mentioned problems will have significant influence on the model performance, but in order to make any conclusions, it would be good to have a bigger dataset and a different way of splitting it. The overall idea of the proposed solution seems very good. Still, it would be nice to see some generalization to a bigger dataset and a more thorough evaluation. First of all, I’d like to thank the reviewer for the nice review. I understand the concerns about small datasets and the choice of the BLEU metric. To be able to make a model that works well on small datasets, we need to use a different metric than BLEU which considers how similar the generated text is to the reference text. One option for such metric is Meteor which is a version of BLEU for machine translation, and it was shown to be a good choice for such models. Also, I’ve read the paper again and I noticed that the suggestion about the method seems to be at the end of the paragraph which describes how to select the best n. The method is actually very efficient to compute and it would be good to use this idea immediately after introducing the motivation for doing so. I understand the concerns about the generalization of the model to a bigger dataset. The idea of using selection score to select the promising vocabulary seems to have some merit. The authors could consider experimenting with different and bigger datasets such as WikiText and other current MT datasets. In my opinion, using the suggested metric Meteor could be a good way to evaluate the

Researchers often wish to keep their research secret until they have obtained formal consent to share this information with the general public or until they have developed a proprietary product. While formal ethical committees evaluate the merits of proposed research, it is often not possible to obtain consent to research until a future date. In many cases, it is not feasible to develop a proprietary product without sharing initial research findings. It would be advantageous if a single measure could be used to weigh the merits of research in these two distinct contexts. A common definition of "merit" would allow for objective comparisons between research projects. In this paper, we describe a mathematical framework for comparing research projects based on defining a research program as a search in a solution space for the best solution. Our framework assigns a score that balances two competing concerns: the need to share information early on, so that failures can be corrected before distributing costly resources, and the need to obtain consent to distribute information in a more widespread manner. We demonstrate the feasibility of this framework with a number of examples.

The heirs of empire are the individuals and organizations claiming reparations for the damage and loss suffered by the former colonized countries due to the actions of their former colonial powers. Although reparations were envisioned by some left-leaning thinkers in the 20th century as a mechanism for alleviating the deep rooted socioeconomic problems of the colonized world, they became a matter of international law only in the 1970s. Individual billionaires and government entities in the Global North have paid lip service in response to this international law, but the reality is that they have tried to pay offset the trauma and loss suffered by a few with the money stolen from thecolonized world. The IMF's Independent Evaluation Office, in their 2015 report "The Moral Financial Burden of Reparations", pointed out that there was not a single example of a country that has paid reparations having its economy improved. It also pointed out that reparations have become a matter of geopolitics, as certain countries see the recipient nations as strategic geopolitical rivals, for example, China and Taiwan. The very idea of reparations for colonialism has been criticized by neo-colonialists, who point out that colonizing powers have already paid compensation in the form of alleviation of the condition of the third world and giving them the opportunity to perpetuate global inequality through neo-colonialism. Groups such as African Claims Agency have called for reparations for the continued impact of colonialism and neocolonialism on the continent. There are also calls for reparations from indigenous groups in the Americas, for example, the Atacama Desert in Chile was artificialy made saline due to the Silzone mining operations of Spain and Germany. Some postcolonial philosophers have theorized that reparations for colonialism and neocolonialism should be paid to the colonized rather than to the former colonial powers, because the former powerless colony still feels like a colony to this day, and paying reparations to the former colonial power would result in an additional transfer of wealth from the poor to the rich. The lack of reparations for colonialism and neocolonialism means that the former colonized continue to suffer from the deep rooted socioeconomic problems caused by the colonization, in contrast to the view of postcolonialists such as Gayatri Spivak who believe that "repairing the harm done by colonialism requires not looking the other way, but facing up to the truth of what was done, and the responsibility of the victor countries for what they did not carry out and what they ignored". The lack of reparations for colonialism and neocolonialism is also an obstacle to research on the impact of colonization, and prevent the study of the continuation of colonialism in the present day, such as the system of slave trade and plantations existing in Latin America today. As a result of international pressure, several countries have started to implement programs of national reparations. Argentina began paying reparations to Jewish survivors in 2010. In addition, the French government set up a "Reparation and Pardon Program" in 2004-2029 to pay damages to the people of North Africa as compensation for the occupation of France by Nazi Germany during World War II. Furthermore, the Czech Republic has paid reparations to the people of Poland for the damage it caused during World War II. Some of the states who have denied owing reparations for the colonization and damage done by their former colonial powers include the United Kingdom, the United States, and Spain, although there have been calls for the UK and US to atone for their abuses through the courts under the jurisdiction of the European Court of Justice and the World Court, respectively. Reparations for the colonialism and neocolonialism have also been called for by various grassroots movements and organizations including, but not limited to, the Zapatista Army of National Liberation, African Claims Agency, Yanomami Organization for Social Development, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Native American Rights Fund, International Indian Treaty Council, and Desis Rising Up and Moving. Several billionaires, such as the Californian Scott Wiener, the Canadian Nina Wang, and the Swiss based Denis Sassou Nguesso, have launched campaigns to raise awareness of reparations. The former Greek finance minister Yanis Varoufakis has promoted a "Global Parcellship" in which nations with colonial past make reparations to those who suffered colonialism. There have been a number of governments that have made reparations for the colonization and damage done by their former colonial powers. These include Argentina paying reparations to Jewish survivors of the Holocaust, the Czech Republic paying reparations to Poland for occupation during World War II, and France setting up a Reparation and Pardon Program to pay damages to the people of North

The "Face on Mars" photograph was taken by the HiRise camera on the Spirit rover in 2004. The image gained notoriety when it was featured in a 2004 issue of National Geographic and used as the cover image of the 2010 book "Image Grass: Seeking Alien Life on Mars" by Rachel Dawson and Steven Christy. The image shows what appears to be a human face in the plane of the Mars' surface. While the photograph was significant in 2004 when it was taken, its prominence in popular culture since then has stirred debate about its authenticity. There have been several independent verification of the "Face on Mars" image since its first publication. A sample of soil collected by the rover in 2011 was shown to contain hexazenal, the main constituent of hexena, the red residue found on the scalpels of mummies. The rover also captured hundreds of images of the same scene without the "Face" and with skies too bright to display the object. Despite this evidence, no living person except the original team of scientists and imaging specialists who took the photo has verified that the image is authentic. In July 2019, the New York Times and Gizmodo published a joint investigation with analysis by respected photogrammetry expert Christy Hbht. The evidence suggested that the object was a crumpled steel yoke that likely came from the Mars lander used to ferry the Spirit rover. Still, this assessment has not quelled the conspiracy theorists and believers in UFOs who claim that the image is a hoax.

People did drink from and contaminated wells in war, but those wells could be cleaned and made usable again. Well water typically contains salt and other minerals that can be easily cleaned from a well with thoughtful processes. This was common knowledge in ancient times. For example, the Book of Joshua tells the story of the Israelites camped around a well in the desert while traveling to take possession of the land promised to them by God. This well is probably the first water source the Israelites came upon after leaving Egypt. “They lacked nothing in the land of Canaan, according to the word of the Lord, the God of Israel, which he had spoken to Moses.” -- Joshua 24:14 The Israelites camped near the well for several days, watering their animals and making efforts to guard the well from others, because they thought it was holy. “Now the Lord had said to Moses, ‘So shall they drink whoever is thirsty of the water ofsufficientSEA waters.” -- Joshua 24:13 When they travel later through the desert, they water their animals at another well, but still call this well out as holy. “These are the waters of Marah, for the people of Israel who came up from Egypt through Moses and ourselves brought them into this place, the Marah o the Sea of Siýr.” -- Exodus 15:23 Eventually, the Israelites move on from this well, because they still have farther to travel and they need to conserve their water resources. “This is what the Lord has commanded us to do; so we've camped here before the Lord, just as the Lord said.” -- Numbers 11:31 However, later in the same chapter, when the spies report back to the Israelites on their encounter with the Midianites, they say that the Israelites should “draw water for yourselves at the well of Midian.” “And when the spy had come back to the camp, Joshua said to him, ‘Well, did you seek the land where the Lord's feet stood?'” -- Numbers 13:17 Joshua is probably questioning the validity of the report from the spies, as they sound like they were glowing with the prospect of a land flowing with milk and honey, instead of the arid desert they encountered. “'It's not possible! The country is very good, but the water is bad; we hope the Lord will give us water of better quality.” -- Numbers 13:18 The spies are reporting back that the water of this well was bitter, perhaps even poisonous, and the Israelites should not drink it. “Then the Lord said to Moses, 'Stretch out your hand toward the rock.' So Moses stretched out his hand toward the rock, and the Lord swore to me and I cried out

Sue for Defamation To sue for defamation, an individual must be able to prove that the statement was: Proven that the statement was published. The statement was false. The statement caused injury. “Special damages” refer to damages that are capable of being calculated, typically to the dollar. Examples include lost earnings or lost future earnings. The statement was protected by “absolute privilege.” In some legal situations, people are asked to make statements without having to worry that they will get sued for defamation. The statement was protected by “qualified privilege.” In some circumstances, people have a right to make a particular statement, and the statement in question must be determined to be protected. Inform the publisher and request a retraction. Determine if the statement is protected by “absolute privilege.” In some legal situations, people are asked to make statements without having to worry that they will get sued for defamation. Determine if the statement is protected by “qualified privilege.” In some circumstances, people have a right to make a particular statement, and the statement in question must be determined to be protected. Inform the publisher and request a retraction. Meet with an attorney to discuss your case. Consider Alternative Dispute Resolution. Consider Arbitration. Fill out a defamation complaint and file your lawsuit. Have the defendant served with a copy of the complaint. Negotiate a settlement, if possible. Go to court if necessary.

Find the mirrors that you want to turn into garden mirrors. Using a circular saw, cut the plywood into the exact same size and shape as the mirrors. Paint the plywood with any outdoor paint. While you're waiting for the paint to dry on the plywood, cut the fence boards into the length you need to make your outer frame. Using liquid nails or caulk, glue the "frame" of fencing material to the front of the mirror. Measure the inside of the frame from top to bottom and cut a piece of trim to fit that exactly. Create an open window look. Measure the distance between the two pieces of frame and cut another piece exactly that size. Cut a piece a little longer than you will need for the top and bottom part of the window and place it where it needs to go. Determine how you want to hang the garden mirror. Using an unbroken line of waterproof caulk around the outside back of the mirror and more all over the back of the mirror, glue the painted plywood in place. Even though firefighters may know that the building is empty, they must perform their duty and put out the fire. Firefighters assume that there is something (a thing, a person, money, etc.) in the building that is valuable enough to risk their lives to save. They also assume that the owners/management of the building would want them to perform this duty and risk their own safety. In reality, even if the building is empty, firefighters may not be able to save the building (the structure, not people) and the other buildings in the surrounding area, because the fire may have spread to those buildings and/or the building itself may be too close to those buildings to save. Also, there may not be anyone present to thank the firefighters for their service. Even though it is assumed that people would want their building(s) to be saved, it may not be the case. Even if firefighters see people going into the building immediately before the fire starts, the people may be the building's firefighters. The firefighters may be unable to tell the difference between people and the building because of the heat of the fire. Even if people do not want their building to be saved, the firefighters must still put out the fire. If they don't, others may come into the building after the fire is out, and the firefighters may not be able to recognize the original structure for what it is because of all of the changes fire may have caused to the original structure and/or the other buildings around it. Even if they do recognize the original structure, the structural changes caused by the fire could make it harder for the original structure to remain standing. The reason firefighters perform this duty is that they assume that the owners/management of the building would want them to and that the owners/management of the building assume that the firefighters will perform this duty. There are usually many risks and responsibilities that come with such assumptions, but firefighters assume that this is a acceptable risk/price to pay in order to ensure that the owners/management of the building value their services and are able to thank them (by thanking them via something such as a plaque/dedication/ceremony/etc.). Ultimately, it is the responsibility of the owners/management of the building to ensure that the firefighters understand that the building is empty, that the people going into the building are the building's firefighters, and that the people will thank the firefighters by thanking them via something such as a plaque/dedication/ceremony/etc. This is why firefighters sometimes perform this duty even if they know that the building is empty. In conclusion, even though firefighters may know that the building is empty, they must perform their duty and put out the fire. Monica and Terri are similar names. There are many people with the name Monica, but only one with the name Terri. There are many people with the name Terri, but only one with the name Monica. The two women have much in common. Both are African American, about 5 feet tall, and were Miss Texas. A photometric study of stellar structures throughout the disk of the barred galaxy NGC 3367 is presented. This is a nearby (distance of 23.9 ± 0.7 megaparsecs, or approximately 62 million light-years), interacting (T = 5.1 ± 0.3), face-on spiral (inclination of 13°) which exhibits some of the most extensive and low-level star formation of any known spiral. I obtain UBVRI photometry of 54 areas across the disk of NGC 3367, including spiral arms, inter-arm regions, and nuclei. The data are used to study the star formation history of the galaxy and the changes in the stellar populations across the disk. Stellar population studies are complicated by several factors. First, different parts of the disk may have different reddenings, so colors can provide only partial information. Second, even within a given structure, there is likely some random sampling variance: the oldest stars are the most widely distributed, so sampling a given region more densely will cause younger populations to be over-represented relative to the global average. Third, dust attenuation can reduce the flux of young stars; this effect is wavelength dependent, so broad bandpasses such as those of the BVR filters I employ here are not suited to accurately tracing recent star formation. Finally, because the light of individual stars is so much fainter than that of galaxies, even a low level of background contamination can significantly skew measured colors and star formation rates. These concerns are illustrated by Figure 1, which compares synthetic colors to the data. The synthetic model galaxy is composed of three simple stellar populations (SSP) with ages of 0.1, 0.5, and 5.0 Gyr, respectively, and a fourth with a constant star formation rate (SFR). The distribution of stars in NGC 3367 is compared to a model with solar metallicity, and a two burst SFH, one 0.1 Gyr ago and the other 0.5 Gyr ago. The fits are not ideal; given the simplicity of the models, the SFH appears to have varied dramatically in the last 0.5 Gyr, and, while a constant SFR seems an adequate fit to the bulk of the disk, the central regions are very poorly fit at all epochs. The great width of the arms and clear change in the relative numbers of blue and red stars between the arms and inter-arm regions suggest that a two burst SFH provides a much better description of the evolution of NGC 3367's disk than a constant SFR. Ultraviolet to blue light is dominated by very short lived, massive stars which are present only in the most active parts of a galaxy's star formation. In contrast, the visible and near infrared are dominated by less short-lived, less massive stars, whose creation is strongly suppressed in low activity regions such as arms. As a result, visible and near infrared bands are ideal for measuring the more global

Spacecrafts attempting to land on Venus face a number of challenges. First, the planet’s thick carbon dioxide atmosphere scatters incident light, making visibility nearly impossible. Second, temperatures on the surface can reach 450 degrees Fahrenheit, far exceeding the melting point of most materials. Third, atmospheric winds are rapid and unpredictable, limiting the ability to perform meteorology experiments. The above challenges are largely a result of Venus's atmosphere. Unlike Earth's atmosphere, which is made up of 78% nitrogen and 20% oxygen, Venus’s atmosphere is made of 96.5% CO2, with the remaining 3.5% made up of other gasses. Due to the greenhouse effect, Venus's atmosphere could not allow for ozone to form on the surface, resulting in the planet being unable to protect itself from the Sun's rays. Despite these challenges, Venus remains an extremely interesting planet to explore. Unlike the other planets in the solar system, Venus retains a strong atmosphere for the majority of its surface. Because of this, the planet may still possess life forms that adapted to live in this environs. Additionally, the extreme temperatures and high carbon dioxide levels may have helped shape unique microorganisms. If future missions can effectively address the challenges posed by the planet's atmosphere, then Venus could provide a wealth of knowledge about life on other planets. By implementing a sustainable landing platform and conducting effective meteorology experiments, future missions could obtain critical data about wind speed and direction, atmospheric pressure, and temperature. With this information, engineers could begin to piece together the planet’s past, revealing clues about its atmosphere and evolutionary history.

Build a fishing rod in Minecraft. This can be done by first opening the Playstation Edition or the Xbox Edition of Minecraft, selecting Play and pressing A (Xbox) or X (PS), selecting a saved game and pressing A or X, gathering the resources needed to build a fishing rod., pressing X or the square button., pressing A or X twice., scrolling over to the crafting table icon and pressing A or X., pressing B or the circle button., selecting your crafting table, then pressing the left trigger., placing the cursor over the crafting table and pressing the left trigger., pressing the right shoulder button once., scrolling right to select the fishing pole., pressing A or X. Place the stick pieces in the crafting table in the following order: four sticks, four planks, and a stick. Right-click the stick pieces. Place a stack of four planks in the crafting table on top of the stick pieces. Right-click the stack of planks. Place a string on top of the stick and the planks. Right-click the string. Place a stick in the middle of your stack of planks. Place the remaining sticks in your inventory and click the string. Click the fishing pole icon. Press A or X. Enjoy fishing in a world of your own making!

Discopolis is a thematic district in the ancient city of Rome, near the Forum Boarium. Its name is derived from the Latin word for "Discus Thrower", the most important statue found here was that of a charioteer named Discus.[1] The district was founded during the reign of Tiberius as a subdivision of the Fori Latine, and was used as a recreational complex for Rome's upper class, including an amphitheater, temples, an aquatic theater, and several houses used as dormitories for students. It was associated with a shift in land use from industrial to residential and also functioned as a security buffer between the port of Augustathroughput of the Tiber and the medieval Boboland to the south.[2] Today, the term "Discopolis" is sometimes used to refer to cities where music is prominent, such as Austin, Texas.[3] The site was explored in the 19th century by Francisco de WarrenCrane, John Gardner Wilton and Giovanni Battista de Rossi.[4] Since the 1970s, it has been excavated by the American Academy in Rome, who have used the site for their premises. Excavations in the 1990s revealed the hypocaust heating system of a nearby house, dating it to the early Augustan period.[5] The site was used into the 6th century, when it was disturbed by residential construction.[6] Discopolis was situated on a ridgeline that overlooks the Forum Boarium and the river Tiber, allowing a clear view of the Campus Martius to the west, and the Subura to the east. At its highest point, the ridge is 50 meters (164 ft) above sea level. The area was first settled in the mid-3rd century BC, but was resettled in the early 1st century BC. In the Middle Ages, the area was outside the city walls, and thus outside the power of the Papacy and the Byzantine Empire. As a result, it was selected as the site of the Donatist bishopric of Romaumburgos, the first independent Latin Christian church, in the mid-5th century. After a period as an outlying suburb of Rome, the area became part of the late Roman and medieval town of Velletri, together with the newly established Aventine and Palatine suburbs. Today, the area is the site of a large campus of the American Academy in Rome, the Studium Romanum, and is host to several facilities of the American University in Rome. The site also houses the offices of the American Catholic Church in Rome, which administer the Roman Catholic churches in Italy. A number of buildings associated with the Academy and the offices of the church are included in the Via della Biblioteca di Valle 98, an autostrada passing through the district.

Italy and Japan had a friendship dating back to the 7th century and the Japanese viewed Italy as a model cultural nation. Mussolini’s fascist government created an embassy in Japan in 1933 and several Italian companies had large presences in Japan, particularly in the automobile industry. Despite this positive relationship, Italy and Japan had a tumultuous relationship in the 1930’s. First, Japan had supported Mussolini’s fascist government but then shifted its support to Japan’s own military government and invaded China in 1937. Mussolini was forced to end all ties with Japan and create an embassy in China. Due to this shift in Japanese foreign policy, Italy developed an alliance with Germany, the Japan’s Axis partner. However, the alliance was transactional as both countries needed what the other offered. For example, Italy needed oil from the German and Japan needed help winning the war against China and the United States. The relationship between Italy and Japan in the early 1940’s was ambivalent as both countries supported different sides of the war. After the United States entered the war against Japan in 1941, Italy switched its alliance to the USA. However, the Japanese continued to see the Americans as a colonial nation and continued to see Italy as a model cultural nation.

James Ferguson (born 1939) is a Scottish political scientist and historian specialising in International Relations and the modern history of the British Empire. He is Chichele Professor of International Affairs at the University of Oxford and a fellow of New College. Landing such a prestigious appointment at such a young age was a remarkable feat, given he was the first black person to be appointed to a senior academic position in the United Kingdom. He was born in Chatham, Kent and raised in both south London and the West Indies. His family relocated to England when he was six, and he was educated in England and at Harvard University, where he earned his PhD. His early career was spent in the United States, where he taught at Rutgers University and New York University, but he returned to the United Kingdom during the late 1990s to take up his Chichele professorship. igner Entertainment, Arts and culture are among the many areas that he has contributed to in the field of International Relations and the modern history of the British Empire. Ferguson's early work focused on the history of colonialism and, more specifically, on the process of decolonisation. His seminal book" Power and Plenty: US International Relation in the Postwar Era", (1980) drew upon his PhD dissertation and examined the US's rise to global dominance during the post-World War II period. He has continued to focus on American foreign policy in subsequent work, most notably in the book "The Benefit of the Doubt: American Presidents and the Middle East" (2005), which examined US involvement in the Middle East, particularly in the context of the Cold War. He has also studied American involvement in Iraq, Pakistan and Afghanistan. In 2011, he published "The Renaissance of the Middle East", a book which analysed the 2011 Arab Spring. He has also researched the application of statistics in International Relations and the history of colonialism.

A considerable body of observational data indicates that cluster diffuse radio emission above the MHD levels is well represented by a simple scaling relation involving the clusterDM density slope, cluster temperature, and the cluster-scale magnetic field strength. We explore an extension of this scaling relation to encompass radio halos and relics, the latter being intense and diffuse connected to recent cluster mergers. In this model, we propose that the radio halos and relics are produced by small-scale seed magnetic fields that are re-accelerated in the merger shock waves. Our main results are that the new scaling relations, which include a parameter ζ characterizing the efficiency of the energy transfer from the DM to the radio plasma, can well describe the radio halos and relics simultaneously. For the first time, our model can naturally explain the observed departures from the classical scaling relations, such as the independence of the halo and relic powers on the plasma temperature and the trend of decreasing halo power with increasing magnetic field strength, as the ζ parameter increases. Our results support the scenario where the small-scale magnetic fields are amplified in the clusters by a combination of DM induced stresses and turbulent dynamo. Furthermore, we speculate that our scaling relation can be used to estimate the DM density slope, thus potentially constraining the DM properties.

I find the proposed MCMC based approach very interesting. By iteratively decoding and encoding, we are able to sample from the learned latent distribution. Since, the generative model learns to map from the learned latent distribution, rather than the prior, we may use MCMC to improve the quality of samples drawn from the generative model, especially when the learned latent distribution is far from the prior. Using MCMC sampling, we are able to reveal previously unseen differences between generative autoencoders trained either with or without a denoising criterion. However, there are some limitations: 1) the encoding distribution has to be a symmetric distribution which is common for GANs (e.g. prior in GANs), while we may sample from much more general distributions with MCMC; 2) the proposal distribution needs to be fixed, which means we have to tune it for different applications. Thus, the solution proposed may not be very general. Nevertheless, this is a very interesting direction and I think this work may have significant impact in GANs. My suggestion for this work: 1) How about using random variable transformation (RVT), which is a general approach to generate from any distribution, to replace MCMC? It could be possible given that we have a GAN model which can be treated as a generative model and encode to learned latent distribution; 2) The assumption that the encoder is symmetric is critical for the validity of MCMC sampling. If we can prove that the prior is symmetric, then we may use RVT to generate from the whole sample space. Please let me know if there are any questions.

Requiring all students to participate in at least one extracurricular activity, often referred to as co-curricular activities (CCA), has become more common in K-12 schools. This practice, along with a range of others, has become a popular mechanism for schools to facilitate the development of important skills, such as independence, responsibility, and resilience. While the goals of these co-curricular activities are admirable, the increased demand for participation has led to a litany of problems. First, requiring students to participate in CCA can have a significant financial impact, especially for underprivileged or economically disadvantaged students. Given the rising cost of textbooks, school supplies, and other school fees, students who are able to bear those expenses may also be required to pay for sports or art programs that compete with their own hobbies and interests. In fact, one observer called sports a "luxury" at some schools. [1] In addition to financial concerns, there are also issues surrounding time management and personal preferences. Many students already have classes, sports, and extracurricular clubs that compete for their time. In such cases, requiring them to spend even more time on CCA can seem like an imposition. On the other hand, if students are able to enroll in a CCA without fully committing to it, they may not meet the expectations of the organizers. While there are many challenges to implementing a strong co-curricular program, many believe that developing interdependence, responsibility, resilience and, perhaps most important, the students' social skills is worth the effort. By allowing students to engage in CCA as they see fit, schools can increase opportunities for student-to-student interaction and help each student find something that interests him or her. The Electoral College System in the United States is flawed and should be abolished. The Electoral College was established in part to keep slaves in states like South Carolina and Louisiana, so excluding slaves from presidential elections. Furthermore, the existing states in 1850 did not have populations that reflected their importance in presidential elections so the Democratic party designed the Constitution to give more power to the small states in order to give the election to a Democratic candidate. Currently, the Electoral College elects the President of the United States, even though the President has limited power and is essentially an 'Agency Chief'. The Electoral College is responsible for presidential elections instead of the people, who have the constitutional right to choose the President. Abolishing the Electoral College would give the power to select the President to the people. This will encourage more people to run for President in order to represent all of the people and reduce the advantages of incumbency. Furthermore, the people would finally be able to have a larger impact on who their President is. Women, people of color, and young people should be encouraged to run for President in greater numbers to positively impact our country and our government. Abolishing the Electoral College would give more power to the people and is long overdue. Special thanks to @Crick15 for this excellent suggestion! I agree. The Electoral College should be abolished. Abolishing the Electoral College would give more power to the people, encourage more candidates to run for President, and reduce the advantages of incumbency.

The Electoral College was established in 1787 and is a key part of the US Constitution. The EC provides a indirect election of the President, making the leader of the largest democratic country in the world select through a indirect vote by the population. The Electoral College has several advantages and disadvantages. The advantages include the division of states into districts, which ensures that each district has an equal number of voters and the winner of the popular vote will also be the winner of the election. Furthermore, the EC ensures a stable and smooth transfer of power, as every vote is equal and thePresident cannot change over time. However, the EC is outdated and fails to represent the political spectrum as most significantly large cities such as New York, Los Angeles, and Chicago contain a large proportion of Democrats, who would otherwise come out ahead in a fair election. Another concern is that the system is part of a democratic nation that has a maximum of two viable political parties, as the third party does not receive a significant number of votes. In conclusion, the EC should be retained because it ensures a stable transfer of power, regardless of the voters' political spectrum. enrich my thoughts The Electoral College has several advantages and disadvantages.
