Knots only work in three dimensions because three is the lowest number of dimensions in which objects exist. Knots are topological features and are not dependent on the number of dimensions in which they are formed. Therefore, regardless of the number of dimensions in which you formed a knot, the knot would remain topological and have the same properties. The question does not make sense because it is referring to a 3-d "rope" and a 4-d "rope" in the same statement. A rope is a linear structural object. You cannot twist one rope into a knot and have another rope turn into the same knot. If the first rope were twisted into a knot, then that would be a knot formed from the first rope. A rope that is capable of turning into a knot is not a rope but a cable. A rope and a cable have different fundamental properties and functions. A rope is long and flexible. A cable is thin and stiff. Even if you were to twist a rope into a knot and insert that knot into a 4-d cable, the knot would easily slip out because the cable is too stiff to deform into the shape of the knot. Even if you were to twist a cable into a knot and insert that knot into a 3-d rope, the rope would not become a 3-d knot. A rope and a cable have different dimensions and functions. They do not share the same dimensions and functions. Therefore, the question does not make sense and it is possible to form a knot in 4-space. Knots are topological features and do not depend on the number of dimensions in which they are formed.

There are two general paradigms for summer projects in professional settings: student-designed and teacher-designed. In student-designed projects, teams of students develop solutions for real-world challenges. In teacher-designed projects, professionals or experts develop solutions or design challenges to be solved by the students. One benefit of student-designed projects is that students learn to apply a process for solving a complex, real-world problem. However, the amount of content covered in a typical design project is very limited, and students are not exposed to the actual challenges faced by professionals in their fields. Additionally, because students are not likely to be offered a fulltime job within their chosen field after the completion of the design project, there is not a substantial motivation for them to dedicate the time and energy necessary to create a high-quality product. In contrast, expert-designed projects give students the opportunity to learn more about the relevant content and exposure to complex problems. On the other hand, expert-designed projects better simulate the actual challenges faced by professionals. For example, the expert may not be aware of the best way to teach a particular concept to a student, so letting a subject matter expert solve this design challenge ensures that the solution will be better tailored to the needs of future employees. Finally, the expert is likely already familiar with the potential consequences of a solution, so they can ensure that the solution is viable, feasible, and appropriate for the targeted audience. Overall, it is challenging to find an ideal approach that balances the pros and cons of each approach. In practice, projects are a combination of both methods. Teams of students and subject matter experts work together to develop the best solution.

This page should help enable RAID or AHCI without reinstalling Windows, especially for AMD SB85 and SB8XX chipset-based RAID controllers. Firstly, make sure you have a current backup of your important data. It's always good to have multiple backups, in case one fails. Secondly, backup your Windows registry. This is where settings for Windows, applications and hardware are stored. It's possible that some of these settings may be invalid/broken after a clean install of a new operating system. It's recommended to use a tool like Revo uninstaller to make sure no files and fragments are left behind. Boot your system into Linux. This can be done by either pressing the Shift key immediately after powering on the machine, or by using an installation disc or live USB. If you have any installation discs, try them in order starting with the earliest release date (eg: 2007, 2008, 2009, 2010) until you find one that works. The exact name of the disc does not matter as long as it starts with "Linux". If you don't have a installation disc, there are many free Linux live USB creators available online. Once inside Linux, view chipset information with the command: lspci -vvnn | less The result might look like this: [email protected]:~$ lspci -vvnn | less 00:14.2 PCI bridge: Intel Corporation 88x PCI-root devison (rev e8) Subsystem: Hewlett-Packard HP 88w837y -- 01:00.0 Ethernet controller: Intel Corporation 82571EB 10/100 Network Connection Subsystem: Hewlett-Packard HP 82571EB -- 01:02.0 Ethernet controller: Intel Corporation 82571EB 10/100 Network Connection Subsystem: Hewlett-Packard HP 82571EB -- 01:04.0 Ethernet controller: Realtek Semiconductor Co., Ltd. RTL-10Gbit/s Combo Network Connection Subsystem: Hewlett-Packard HP RTL8111G -- 01:08.0 IDE interface: Intel Corporation 82371AB IDE (ICH4/ICH4-M) -- 01:08.2 SATA controller: Intel Corporation 82801HM/HEM (SATA)'. Find the entry with the word "SATA" and "RAID" in it, and note the following information: Vendor ID: a 4 digit number, Device ID: a 4 digit number, Subsystem ID: an 8 digit number split by a semicolon (":") Reboot your system. The change in BIOS will take effect only after a system reboot. Download the AMD SB850 RAID driver from https://support.hp.com/us-en/document/c07721979. Extract the ZIP file and go to the AMD SB850 RAID driver folder. Navigate to the folder for your version of Windows. For Windows 7, it's C:\Windows\System32\Drivers. Copy the ahcix64s.sys file to the C:\Windows\System32\Drivers folder. At this point, you are ready to reboot your system into Windows. Remember to check the "RAID" option in the BIOS. Reboot into Windows. Go to the Control Panel and open Device Manager. Look for "SATA Controller" and right-click it. Choose "Update Driver". Select the "Browse my computer for driver software" option. navigate to the AMD SB850 RAID driver folder. Browse to the ahcix64s.sys file you copied earlier and click OK. Some devices may require additional steps. For example, on my computer, I needed to disable Intel Rapid Start first. After a while, you should see the RAID controller listed in the Device Manager.

The Facial Action Coding System or FACS is a method used to identify true emotions by observing surface facial movements. The idea for the FACS was conceived in the 1930’s by Gesell, who was attempting to find a measure of cognitive development. Since then, it has been refined and improved upon to become the most widely used system for accurately identifying emotion through facial expressions. FACS has been adopted by research communities in areas such as Cognitive Neuroscience, Psychology, and Biology. This is evident in the number of commercial products that have been developed that utilize FACS (including Kajabi and Primeserve). FACS can assist in accurately identifying real emotions from fake, or hypothesized emotions. The implementation of FACS in a society has significant implications for public safety, insurance, and law. The adoption of FACS would allow 911 to accurately identify the nature of a person’s emotional state at the time of an emergency, which in turn would allow emergency personnel to provide the proper care. Insurance companies could ensure that people are not exaggerating their happiness when filing a claim. Similarly, law enforcement would be able to use FACS to help determine a suspect’s true emotional state during an interrogation. On a more positive note, the use of FACS in Artificial Intelligence could yield highly accurate models that could parlay their successes in identifying real emotions into real talent. The use of machine learning with FACS could yield sophisticated software agents that are highly effective in assisting humans in a variety of activities ranging from health care to criminal investigation to even telling us jokes.

Around sunspots, loops of moving magnetic flux are observed. A popular model to explain the evolution of sunspots connects the formation of these loops with the eponymous U-shaped structure. In this model, the U-loops are observed to be detached from sunspots and to move with the magnetically loaded material around the sun. Velocities of such moving magnetic feature pairs are reported here and are used to support this model. It is found that the horizontal components of these velocities scale with the width of the corresponding features, while the vertical components scale with the square root of the width of the features, consistent with a magnetic flux tube description. Furthermore, the separation of the pair of features is found to increase at a faster rate than their separation in the initial position, also consistent with the U-loop model. This work was performed as part of the NROLS project and was supported by the French National Program for Scientific Research.

Bracket creep refers to the phenomenon where the seed, round one seed in tournament brackets, is adjusted up or down in an organized tournament series in order to ensure the higher seeds reach the final of the tournament. This impairs the chances of lower seeded teams to meet higher seeded teams in the tournament. The change in seed levels occurs because a higher ranked team may have a better record than a lower ranked team but the difference between the two teams is too small to warrant an upgrade. Thus, one of the teams is relegated to a lower seed and advances a lower level of competition. In some cases, a lower ranked team can move up in the seed levels if a higher ranked team loses a game and is eliminated from the tournament. This practice is controversial as some argue it undermines the seed system which is intended to create an approximate measure of tournament strength. Others claim the practice is not widespread enough to warrant concern. Bracket creep has been observed in college basketball, Association Football (soccer), and some formats of the NFL and NHL tournaments. In most sports, the organizers of a tournament have the authority to determine the seed of each team, however in many sports the organizers of a tournament is not the same organization that organizes the tournament for the entire sport. This creates a disconnect where the organizers of a tournament can implement bracket creep without the approval of other tournament organizers throughout the sport. In March 2015, the Division I Men's Basketball Committee discussed changing the rule so that "the seeds in the first round and the final four would be determined by the tournament champions with the top two teams in each conference guaranteed two seeds, and the remaining teams assigned in ranking order other than 1) between the two most distant seed lines; 2) in the case of tie, seed to which team won the game compared to the other team in the same seeding line."

The Electoral College, process is used for presidential elections in the United States. The popular vote does not play a role in determining the outcome of presidential elections. A presidential candidate that receives a majority of votes, but not a majority of states will win the election. This system was established through a constitutional amendment process, and multiple scholars believe this was a good change. The popular vote, process would eliminate the possibility of a candidate not obtaining a majority of the votes in the election. This could create negative ramifications, such as postponing an election or causing candidates to withdraw before the election. Additionally, a popular vote, process could negatively impact the energy required for electioneering. By nature, this process requires negative campaigning. The use of the Electoral College, process in presidential elections is appropriate. There are no apparent flaws in the process. It is important to consider potential implications when proposing change in the electoral college process. There could be negative consequences to reverting back to the popular vote process. For instance, postponing elections could impact the voting schedule. Voter registration would have to accommodate multiple elections with different dates. Campaigns would have to spend additional funds on advertisements. Other examples of negative implications could include unfair negative campaigning. Eliminating this type of campaigning could reduce participation in elections. It is recommended that the Electoral College process be kept. There are no apparent flaws in the current process and eliminating the popular vote, process may have unforeseen negative consequences. The Electoral College, process has worked well in presidential elections. This process needs no adjustments.

Polarizabilities of seven lithium clusters with 143-426 atoms are calculated at the DFT/B3PW91 level of theory with triple-zeta def2-SVP basis set. Size-dependent polarizabilities of these clusters are also discussed. The obtained results show that the polarizability of lithium clusters increases with cluster size. Moreover, the polarizability of lithium clusters increases gradually from 142 to 143 atoms and then increases sharply from 426 to 431 atoms. The maximum polarizabilities of lithium clusters are obtained with lithium clusters with size of 143 atoms. The difference between polarizabilities of lithium clusters with size of 142 and 143 atoms is larger than that of clusters with size of 143 and 144 atoms. The difference between polarizabilities of lithium clusters with size of 142 and 143 atoms is larger than that of lithium clusters with size of 143 and 144 atoms. The difference between polarizabilities of lithium clusters with size of 142 and 143 atoms is larger than that of lithium clusters with size of 143 and 144 atoms. The maximum polarizabilities of lithium clusters are obtained with lithium clusters with size of 143 atoms. This is because two lithium atoms in lithium clusters combine to form a stable closed-shell configuration of lithium ion with [Formula: see text] electron configuration.

Coherence and phase synchronization are a pair of multivariate extensions to the computation of cross-correlation used in time series analysis. In its most basic form, coherence measures the proportion of two signals' in-phase behavior, while the related phase synchronization quantifies the consistency of the signal phase. These metrics have been used to characterize the phase-locking behavior exhibited by systems as diverse as neurons, heart cells, and Josephson junctions. The most widely-applied multivariate extension to coherence and phase synchronization is the computation of delay-embedded correlation. This metric not only quantifies in-phase behavior, but can also be used to estimate the influence that one signal has on another, coupled system. The delay-embedding approach has been successfully applied to spatially-coupled electrochemical systems, coupled financial markets, and coupled network models of pulse-coupled oscillators. While the delay-embedding approach is conceptually simple, its computations are frequently unstable, especially when applied to non-stationary data. To address this, the pair-wise multivariate extension has been introduced. This metric generalizes the computation of delay-embedding correlation to account for cross-correlation between two signals at non-zero time lags. In this manner, it is able to estimate the influence that one signal has on the other, even in the presence of non-stationarity and zero-lag influence. It is important to emphasize that the pair-wise approach can only estimate the influence that one signal has on the other. It is unable to separate the two signals. In contrast, multivariate metrics such as transfer entropy and information dynamics measure the influence that one signal has on both itself and the other. This paper will describe both the pair-wise and multivariate formulations of coherence and phase synchronization, and will provide illustrative examples of their application to synthetic data as well as real-world systems.

The main content of the paper is as follows. Consider a feedforward neural net after random initialization, and assume that the input data is independently and identically distributed (i.i.d.) from a distribution with mean 0 and variance 1. Consider three measures of expressivity. The first is the minimum correlation with any one layer, which is the correlation between the function computed by the net and the function that projects to the 0 vector. The second is the maximum correlation with any one layer, which is the correlation between the function computed by the net and the all-1 vector. The third is the sum of absolute values of differences between the output and the expected value, where the expected value is the dot product with the input of the net’s prediction. For this last measure, we prove that the growth rate of the expressiveness measures is equal to the depth of the network, up to lower-order terms which we bound. We empirically examine the relationship between expressiveness and the depth of the network in two settings. First, we show that the trajectory length (time averaged expression in a moving window) has the same order of growth in depth as the expressiveness measures. Thus, in terms of trajectory length, the expressiveness of a random initialization is bounded by an exponential in the depth of the network, which we prove. This implies that the expressiveness of a well-trained net is also at most an exponential in the depth of the net, as we show in a number of experiments. Secondly, we discuss the interpretation of the trajectory length, and discuss a generalization to networks with non-linearities at layers other than the last.

Richard DeLong (born 1942) is a computer scientist known for his work on languages that parallel everyday human languages, such as English. DeLong began his academic career as an electrical engineer at the University of California, Berkeley, where he helped to create the FIRST circuit design competition in high school. DeLong received his BA in mathematics and physics from Harvard University in 1964, and his PhD in linguistics from Stanford University in 1968 under the supervision of Noam Chomsky. After graduation, DeLong did his post-doctoral work with Kenneth Walbird at the University of California, Santa Barbara. In 1971, DeLong became an assistant professor at the University of Minnesota, where he remained for 12 years. In 1983, DeLong returned to the University of California, Berkeley, where he is now a professor of computer science and a member of the Molecular Computer Science and Engineering and Language Production teams. DeLong was elected a fellow of the Association for Computational Linguistics in 1986 and of the American Academy of Arts and Sciences in 1990. His 1989 book, The Minimalist Program, has been called "one of the most influential papers in the history of NLP". In 1991, DeLong and Peter Sellis published the first joint paper on what would later become called dependency parsing, an approach to analyzing a sentence that computes its syntactic structure. DeLong and Thad Starner published the first successful Synthetic Digitally Generated Intelligible Speech (SDGIS) system in 1993. DeLong was a pioneer in the development of neural networks, beginning with the development of backpropagation inANN in the early 1980s. His 1990 book, Learning with errors: A theoretical analysis of backpropagation was cited by Gradient-Based Training of Neural Netwoks in 1993. DeLong is currently active in the fields of Artificial Life and Evolutionary Computation, where he is a coauthor of several books and a consultant. DeLong is currently an emeritus professor at UC Berkeley and a professor at the University of Minnesota.

Stuart Townend is an English musician, best known as the bassist and second vocalist of The Smiths. Townend joined the band after the previous bassist, Andy Rourke, was fired. The band had originally intended to take a break after their previous album, Strange think, but discovered upon Townend's recruitment that they were already near the end of their schedule and had to leave a note for their record label. Townend is the only Smiths member to have appeared on all of the band's studio albums and live albums, and was the only original member to appear on all of the band's tours. He is the longest standing current member of the band. Townend's songwriting was largely responsible for the band's success, and he was largely responsible for the band's image. He was often mocked by critics and the public for his corpulence, which culminated in a staged photo opportunity during the making of Queer, in which Townend smeared fake blood around his mouth as a gag. The gesture went viral, and Townend became a notable internet meme. Townend was fired by the band in 1987, shortly before the release of The Smiths' fourth and final studio album, simply called The Last of the Home Crowd. He was replaced by Matt Johnson. He has been retired from performing and recording music since. Townend has since had several small cameos in the media as an actor and has published several books about his experiences with The Smiths. He had a cameo in the 2004 biographical film about the band, audio of which was released as a separate track on the deluxe edition of The Smiths – Soundtrack From a Generation.

The author of the article “The Challenge of Exploring Venus” did an a poor job of supporting the idea that studying Venus is worth the risks, including the limitations of current technology, the time and cost involved, and the dangers to human life. The article outlines the difficulties and dangers of living on Venus and the challenges of escaping the limitations of current technology, but it fails to adequately address the risks and costs of living on Venus. The article states that “[t]he challenges of living on Venus may seem intractable in the short term, but I remain hopeful that with enough investment and ingenuity, the hostile environment could be transformed into a thriving settlement.” However, the article fails to provide a clear explanation of how they will ensure that the challenges of living on Venus are intractable, how much investment is needed, or how much ingenuity is required. The article also fails to consider the cost of living on Venus, and instead uses the example of “fuel at triple the cost of Earth, power that is merely two thirds as powerful as Earth, and inflated labor costs of 60 to 70 times what they would be on Earth” as a justification for why the risks and costs of living on Venus are worth it. However, the author does not address why the inflation in costs would not also apply to all of the listed “benefits” of living on Venus. Furthermore, the article fails to consider the time involved and cost of a mission to Venus. According to the article, past attempts to explore Venus have taken “more than 10 years and [have] cost tens of millions of dollars,” and the current estimate for a manned mission is between 75 and 100 million dollars. The article does not explain whether these previous missions carried out by other countries have already developed technology that could be applied to a manned mission to Venus, or whether a new investment of tens of millions of dollars would be required just to study the challenges of living on Venus. Overall, the article provides few reasons to justify why exploring Venus is worth the costs, and instead focuses on the challenges of living on Venus and the risks involved. The article does not acknowledge that the challenges of living on Venus may intractability, but it does not provide an adequate explanation of how they would avoid these risks. The article fails to provide a cost-benefit analysis of Venus exploration, and fails to establish whether the challenges of exploring Venus outweigh the costs.

Understand the five stages of grief. Acknowledge your grief Share your grief with others Seek professional help if you need it Commit to not rushing yourself Take one day at a time Celebrate small victories Let yourself think about something positive Evaluate your love with an honest lens Be honest about the highs and the lows Recognize the parts that may have been damaging for you Don’t dwell on the bad parts Let reconnect with your closest supporters Set boundaries for conversations Set boundaries for interactions Accept invitations to socialize with acquaintances Talk out your emotions Write a letter to the person Write about your feelings in a journal Make a change just for you Live your own life Determine if you’re still in the rebound phase Figure out how much you think of this person Tie memories to new experiences Monitor how you react when someone says your former love’s name Gauge your emotional reaction if you see your former love Follow the instructions above to contribute to this article. If you have contributed to this article, thank you! We welcome comments and suggestions to this article. Our goal is to create the largest collection of wikiHows. If you have a suggestion for a wikiHow article, click the edit button at the top of this page. Thanks,! Letting Go of Someone You Deeply Loved I remember this guy. We were inseparable for three years. I still think about him every now and then. He is not in my life anymore. How do I move on? First, forgive yourself for feeling how you feel. grief is normal and completely normal. If someone was a important part of your life and they broke your heart, it will take time to heal. give yourself time to heal. second, let people around you help you heal. Your friends and family know you best and they want what’s best for you. Listen to them. third, do things that make you feel better. Go to the movies you love. Go for a walk and smell the flowers. finally, put this someone who broke your heart into perspective. consider the times you were with this person and gave it 2.5 stars. Consider the way he/she treated others more than him/herself. Think about how you felt at the time and how you still feel now. Next, consider who this person was to you. Think about what your life was like before and after you started spending time with this person. Remember the good and bad times and think about what your life is like now without this person in it. When you are ready, this person will be in the past. You will be better for it. Letting go of someone you deeply loved and moving on after are extremely common experiences. You should not feel bad for feeling the way you feel. Remembering good times and giving this person 2.5 stars is completely okay. Looking at the past with this clarity will help you focus your energy on moving forward. Keep smiling and remember this person who broke your heart as the time you spent with them made a positive impact on your life. While there are many advantages to online classes, such as the ability to focus on individual learning tasks and take classes at one's own pace, there are also some significant drawbacks. One issue is the lack of personalization of learning, as students in online classes might not be able to establish as strong of a connection with the instructor or other students as they would if they were in a live class. This lack of personalization could lead to a decreased ability to engage in the class and pay attention, as well as a decreased motivation to complete assignments on the syllabus. In addition, the lack of in-person contact means that there is no opportunity for casual chats that might encourage more participation in class, which could reduce the likelihood that students actually completing the assignments, especially if they are important or graded. In terms of improved success rates, the decreased focus on not taking the class seriously could allow for a greater degree of procrastination, as students can often slack off when they know they aren't being graded for their work. This potential for increased procrastination could increase the risk of course failure, as those students who would have otherwise done well in the class may not put in the necessary effort to complete the work, potentially leading to a lower overall success rate than if the class was taught in person. Overall, online classes have the potential to provide a more flexible way to study, allowing students to maintain focus and not procrastinate while also ensuring that the class is not too large such that there is not sufficient accountability for each student, or too small such that there is not enough focus to ensure that all students are able to stay on track. However, as discussed, there are significant drawbacks to these classes that make them less effective at ensuring student success and increasing the likelihood that each student will complete the work, particularly if it is important or graded. Overall, the decision to switch to online classes depends on the particular strengths and weaknesses of each student. For some, the decreased focus and limited access to other students could allow them to procrastinate or slack off; for others, the flexibility might outweigh the drawbacks. But it is important to recognize that switching to online classes would limit the potential for casual interactions that help students balance accountability with focus and success, and the drawbacks of switching to online classes might outweigh the potential benefits. Therefore, although it could be an effective way to teach a class to a wider group of students, switching to online classes should only be done if the instructor can find another method to balance the needs of each student.

Mona Lisa is a painting done by Leonardo Da Vinci, and is currently on display at the Louvre Museum in Paris, France. Based on the facial expressions in the Mona Lisa, a computer program written by researchers at the University of Illinois can identify and display what human emotions the artist was feeling at the time she painted this portrait. This program has the potential to be used in classrooms to help identify students becoming confused or bored, and could be used in research to better understand how to improve learning outcomes. However, this kind of technology also raises some concerns. While this particular painting is priceless and its reproduction could be traced back to the original artist, this same technology could be used to reproduce paintings in a less scrupulous way, for marketing or commercial purposes. In addition, in an educational environment, it could give students the false impression their peers can see how they are feeling, and this knowledge could impact the way they act in front of the class. Finally, with this kind of technology, it would be easy to "stimulate" certain emotions in participants in order to better understand how their brains react under those circumstances. While replicating works of art is forbidden by the law, it is not the case for technological inventions, and the uses of such a technology could be a slippery slope. Overall, this technology can help us understand and empathize with the Mona Lisa, but it should be used with caution and within the ethical framework established by a university.

The gated-attention reader effectively replaced the BERT reader and achieved higher performance on some tasks. The difference in performance between the gated-attention reader and the BERT reader is often statistically significant. The added value of the gated-attention reader is likely the use of "gated memory" to inform the generation of new questions. However, there are several practical concerns: (1) ThePretrainingTexts used to train the gated-attention reader are much smaller than BillionWords, which may increase the burden of reading large volumes of text; (2) the gated-attention reader has a longer training time and is more computationally expensive than BERT. In conclusion, the gated-attention reader is a promising replacement for the BERT reader, but it has limitations that need to be resolved before it can be widely used. The VLT-FLAMES survey of massive stars (VMSS) is aimed at studying the evolution of hot massive stars in the Local Group galaxy the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC). Wind properties and evolution of hot massive stars are key to understand feedback from massive stars in their environment, from the morphological structure of the interstellar medium to the chemical evolution of their host galaxy. Combined high-resolution spectroscopy from VLT-FLAMES allows to determine mass-loss rates from H emission, temperature of the bi-conical winds and thus analyze the evolutionary status of these stars. From the mass-loss rates, Ionic masses can be derived and eventually be used to investigate other properties of the winds, such as ionization degrees and acceleration regions with potential effects on simulations of the feedback into the environment. In this contribution we present the first results on the evolution of hot massive stars in the LMC and the impact of this evolution on the galactic feedback. We confirm previous studies which concluded that the initial mass function (IMF) is top-heavy in the LMC, with respect to the Milky Way. The most massive stars show stronger IMF divergence from the initial mass function (IMF) with increasing age, suggesting that these stars were affected by significant mass-loss episodes. We found more extreme cases of stars with abnormally high nitrogen to carbon (N/C) and nitrogen to oxygen (N/O) abundance ratios, which might be the consequence of a thermohaline mixing process occurring in their winds, an hydrodynamic process taking place in the strong winds of massive stars.

The standard model of fundamental physics suggests that the universe we see around us is composed of matter and radiation, which interact through four fundamental force - the strong and weak nuclear forces, the electromagnetic force, and the gravity. On cosmological scales, these forces appear to be very well aligned - the gravitational pull of the entire universe is sufficient to cause visible galaxies and clusters of galaxies to be attracted towards each other, and the mutual electromagnetic forces between electrons and their negatively charged counterparts, protons, are strong enough to cause atoms to form. Despite this apparent uniformity, we now know that these fundamental constants in the standard model - the strong and weak nuclear coupling constants, the electromagnetic coupling constant, and the gravitational constant - vary in space and time. Through a range of experiments, astrophysics and cosmology, we now understand that these variations are small - the values of these constants have been very similar throughout the history of the universe, with the notable exceptions of the electromagnetic coupling constant and the gravitational constant, whose values have been within 2.2 standard deviations of each other for approximately 12% of the universe's history. This uniformity over time has been referred to as the cosmological principle, and forms the backbone of our current theory of cosmology. But in recent years, we have begun to suspect that these constants may not be constant at all, but may instead vary from place to place in the universe. A compelling explanation has been the putative "dark energy", believed to account for approximately 73% of the universe's energy density, which is known to vary in strength throughout the universe, causing its gravitational pull to vary. This pull, in turn, would cause the various fundamental constants in the universe to change in order to keep track of how much energy is available. While the search for dark energy has continued, new and more precise cosmological data has suggested that the values of these constants may not only vary across space, but may even vary within the same place. For example, in 2013 the PLANCK spacecraft, launched by the European Space Agency, measured anisotropies in the cosmic microwave background radiation, suggesting that the value of the gravitational constant may have changed by 6.5 parts per million (ppm) between the time of recombination, when the universe was smaller than 1 trillionth of its present size, and the present time, caused by the variations in the pull of dark energy. In 2015, the DESI experiment, also launched by the European Space Agency, suggested that the fine structure constant - the value of the electromagnetic coupling constant - may have changed by 6.5 parts per million as well, in addition to small variations in the strong and weak nuclear coupling constants. These observations, if true, could overturn the foundations of modern physics. A possible explanation for these variations is the "Standard Model Extension", which extends the standard model of fundamental physics with additional fields and parameters that describe possible violations of the standard model's local symmetry - specifically, the symmetry of space itself. If Superman were in space and needed to maneuver by moving his cape, the cape would not behave the same as it does on Earth. First, because there is no Earth to pull against, the force of gravity, which is always pulling straight down, would no longer be acting on the cape. Second, because there is no atmosphere to resist the motion, the cape would behave more or less the same way as a bullet shot into space. It would travel in a straight line at approximately 7.5 kilometers per second, or approximately 13,000 miles per hour. For comparison, the speed of Earth's rotation is 25,000 miles per hour. If Superman flew at high speed over a city, the motion of his cape could be seen from the ground. It is possible that, in an emergency, Superman could maneuver by moving his cape in space. For example, if a meteor was heading towards Earth and was about to hit the atmosphere, Superman could use his cape to change the trajectory of the meteor so that it would burn up in the atmosphere and not cause any damage on the ground. However, even in this case, it would likely not be possible for Superman to move his cape fast enough to escape immediate danger.

The paper describes a method of learning low-dimensional embeddings for points on a low-dimensional manifold embedded in a high-dimensional space. This is achieved by training a deep autoencoder on a randomly rotated input point with the goal of reconstructing the original point. The learnt transformation mapping the input to the latent space is interpreted as the optimal linear projection, that is able to capture the local structure of the manifold in a succinct low-dimensional embedding. Experiments conducted on toy examples and real-world data demonstrate the practicality of the proposed method, providing results that are qualitatively and quantitatively close to state-of-the-art alternatives.' One potential drawback of the proposed method is that it requires the number of dimensions in which the manifold is embedded to be known in advance. greetings Hi, I have taken a look at the paper and found it interesting. I do have some comments and suggestions. 1) The description in the paper of the architecture of the autoencoder is incomplete. Specifically, the dimensionality of the input and the dimensionality of the hidden layers are not clearly stated. Furthermore, the number of layers and the number of neurons in each layer are not specified. It is not clear from the text how many transformations (linear projections) the encoder is using and whether the decoder also has the same number of layers and parameters. Without this information, it is not possible to evaluate the efficacy of the approach. 2) The validation set should be used for early stopping. Currently, it looks like it is being used for regular validation, which is not correct. It would be better to do early stopping on the validation set, and then report the loss on the test set. 3) What is the source of the data? The paper does not mention where the data was generated. Was it produced by a machine? Was it mined from a database? Or was it collected using an optical device? This is important because the source of the data could have a large impact on the nature of the data. For example, computer-generated data often has strong patterns that can be detected by the autoencoder. If the data was collected using an optical device, it might have additional geometric properties that can be utilised. In either case, it would be helpful to mention this information in the paper. 4) In general, the performance of the model will be tied to the quality of the data. For example, if all the data points are sitting close to each other, and only a small number of dimensions are needed to capture the local structure, then the model may not need to use all the data to find a good representation. It would be good to mention this concept in the paper. 5) Some of the implementation details could be clearer. For example, the number of units in the fully connected (FC) layers, as well as the weight initialization scheme, should be specified. I would be grateful if you could please consider these comments when preparing a revised version of the paper. Kind regards, Bilal -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- I found these comments useful. Here is a revision of the paper, taking into consideration all the comments. Many thanks for the detailed feedback, we have made revisions in accordance with your suggestions. We have also incorporated your additional suggestions. The validation set should be used for early stopping. We have mentioned the source of data in the paper. We have specified the number of units in the FC layers and the weight initialization scheme. We have also updated the references. Many thanks again for your help! I describe the architecture of the autoencoder. I describe the procedure for using the network to find a low-dimensional representation of a given high-dimensional data set. I present the evaluation methodology. I present and discuss the results. I provide conclusions and future work. Table of Contents before changes. Paper I provide an overview of related work. I highlight the contributions of this work. I provide the details of the data set. I describe the architecture of the autoencoder. I provide conclusions and future work. Table of Contents after changes. Paper I provide an overview of related work. I highlight the contributions of this work. I provide the details of the data set.

The transportation network is a major part of any city or town. It is very beneficial for short-distance travel within a city or town, but it contributes to air pollution and noise pollution when used for long-distance travel. As a result, limiting car usage has many benefits to both the environment and the community. The primary benefit is air quality. In towns and cities with heavy traffic, cars emit both carbon monoxide and nitrogen oxide, which can negatively affect human health. When driving in these environments, people may experience discomfort, headaches, or flu-like symptoms. Limiting car usage forces drivers to use alternatives methods of transportation, such as biking, walking, taking public transportation, or riding a scooter. These forms of transportation all rely on cleaner sources of energy, such as feet or legs. Using a scooter or bicycle for short-distances is probably the best option for environmental and community reasons. Exercising helps lower the blood pressure, and using an electric bike instead of an oil-powered one produces no carbon dioxide emissions. An additional benefit of limiting car usage is improved accessibility. By eliminating cars, the city becomes accessible to people who cannot afford a car, such as students who live in the suburbs or poor people who work in the suburbs. An additional benefit of limiting car usage is improved liveability. The vibe of the city or town becomes friendlier. For example, in Vauban, Germany, a small city on the border with France, people love the small-town friendliness. Bicycling is the preferred form of transportation. There is even a bicycle lane on the main road that goes through the city, making bicycling easier and safer. Other neighborhoods have been designed with biking in mind. They have small roads and narrow alleyways, with flower boxes along the walls. The community intentionally limited car usage, and the result is a local government that cares about the quality of life. The positive effects of limiting car usage are amplified when applied across an entire region. One study has shown that when only a small percentage of the population reduces car usage, the result is reduced air pollution. The study showed that most cars with license plates ending in “9” were registered in only 3% of all households. Making a central mandate to reduce car usage reduces overall vehicle usage and air pollution. Another effect of limiting car usage is improved safety. When people are forced to walk, bike, or use mass transit for short distances, they develop a higher degree of confidence on the public transportation system. They feel more comfortable using the system and are more aware of their surroundings. This increased vigilance on the safety of the commute contributes to a sense of security in the city or town. Overall, limiting car usage improves the health of the environment and the people who live in that environment. It reduces air pollution, noise pollution, and vehicle traffic. It promotes biking and walking as the primary forms of transportation. It also promotes use of public transportation and reducing car usage in high-traffic areas. These policies allow people to live better lives in pleasant communities. The above explanation is a summary of the benefits of limiting car usage. The benefits are numerous and significant, and the benefits of limiting car usage are obvious to anyone who lives in a city with heavy traffic. Vauban, Germany, Paris, France, and Colombia are good examples of towns or cities that have effectively limited car usage. It is possible to improve the environment and make communities better places to live by limiting car usage. It reduces air pollution and carbon monoxide emissions. It also promotes biking and walking as a primary mode of transportation. It promotes public transportation and reduces car usage in high-traffic areas. It is good business for local governments because improved liveability and community security lead to lower crime rates. It promotes liveability and security in pleasant suburbs.

Roy, Montana is a town in the Lincoln County, Montana, United States. It is named after Roy Bean, a local judge. As of the 2010 United States Census, the population of the town was 198, increasing from 146 at the 2000 Census. The town is located on Route 287 between Choteau and Columbus, Montana. Roy was founded in 1902 and lots were sold in 1903. The town was located on the Blackfoot Valley Railroad, which later became the Denver andSalt Lake City Railroad, with tracks running through the center of town. The center of town contains a small park with a plaque dedicated to the railroad. The original townsite is now home to the Roy Cemetery. In 1910, Roy had a population of over 500, with many residents traveling to and from the town by stagecoach. In 1917, a new schoolhouse was built in the center of town. In 1930, the Great Depression hit Roy hard, and the population declined to less than 200. Roy still holds an annual "Pony Express Days" celebration, where residents get together to celebrate the town's heritage and participate in costume-related activities. In 2013, the town was featured on the Travel channel's series, The Best Places to Live as an example of a " gorgeous, small town with big-city amenities". Roy is well known for its annual "Pony Express Days" celebration, where residents of the town come together to celebrate their heritage and participate in costume-related activities.

A search for radiative decays of the Upsilon(1S) meson into eta and eta' has been performed with the full dataset of the L3+00 experiment at the LEP collider. No evidence for these decays has been found, and upper limits at the 90% confidence level of Br(Upsilon(1S)-->eta) < 5.0x10(-8) and Br(Upsilon(1S)-->eta') < 2.5x10(-8) are obtained. These results improve previous ones from the L3 and OPAL experiments. The L3+00 experiment, which was performed at the LEP collider, is described. The data sample consists of 202 x 10(exp - 7) positive mesons, collected from 1996 to 2000, corresponding to an integrated luminosity of 208 pb(s). The results of a search for the radiative decays of the Upsilon(1S) meson are presented. No evidence for these decays has been found, and upper limits at the 90% confidence level of Br(Upsilon(1S)-->eta) < 5.0x10(-8) and Br(Upsilon(1S)-->eta') < 2.5x10(-8) are obtained.

On May 18, 2015, the very high energy (VHE, E > 0.1 TeV) gamma-ray band observed by the Cherenkov Telescope Array (CTA) showed a very bright flare from the direction of PKS 2155-304. Observations in other bands with ground-based gamma- and lower-energy telescopes as well as space-based telescopes (including *Fermi) showed that this was likely due to a gamma-ray flaring blazar, an AGN whose jets are pointed closely toward the Earth. This is the first time that a blazar has been observed in very high energy gamma rays, and the emission was most likely due to accelerated electrons and positrons spiraling in the extremely strong magnetic fields of the blazar jet. This represents the fourth detected TeV blazar flare, all of which were observed by CTA, and the first time that a detection has been made with ground-based telescopes.

Extracurricular activities not only enrich students' educational experiences but also foster a plethora of positive social and emotional benefits. Active students not only achieve better academic results but are also more likely to find employment, form close friendships, and contribute to their local communities. Moreover, students who partake in extracurricular activities report enhanced self-esteem, increased psychological well-being, and higher grades in school. Participation in extracurricular activities not only fosters the above benefits but also promotes student academic performance. Enthusiastic students are more likely to apply themselves to their studies, develop time management skills, and perform better on exams. These students, who by definition participate in a plethora of activities, are therefore more likely to dedicate more time to their studies and consequently perform better. On average, students who partake in extracurricular activities score higher on standardized tests than their counterparts who do not. Experiments in New Zealand found that students who perform well academically are those who attend the fewest extracurricular activities. Therefore, students who participate in these activities tend to underperform relative to their intellectual capabilities. Scientific studies have also linked extracurricular activities with positive effects on mental health. These activities not only improve students' self-esteem but also provide them with opportunities to interact with friends from different backgrounds and acquire new skills that are unrelated to academics. Such interactions promote psychological well-being and avoid isolation, which are factors related to improved mental health. Based on the above, I recommend the following changes to policy: 1) allow schools to provide the academic benefits of extracurricular activities, and 2) promote and fund extracurricular activities for students in need of individual attention.

The question of whether or not students should be required to maintain a certain GPA to participate in extracurriculars is a challenging question that is difficult to answer in a vacuum. On one hand, requiring a minimum GPA to participate in extracurriculars can serve as a useful filter to remove potentially harmful participants who are not serious about their commitments. Extracurriculars, such as sports, require significant time and energy away from other responsibilities such as sleeping and eating, so it is reasonable to expect a minimal level of commitment from the participants. Furthermore, students should be required to uphold certain ethical standards set by their respective organizations, so students who violate these standards can be disqualified from participating without the need for a rigid GPA requirement. However, by removing academic requirements from extracurriculars, students are missing an opportunity to encourage academic rigor and healthy competition. Operating under the assumption that anyone can participate without the need for high standards diminishes the value of the program. Imagine if student government only recruited high school dropouts to participate, or if a team full of beginners defeated a team of seasoned players. By lowering the entry requirements, the elite and challenging aspects of the programs are lost. Overall, while I am sympathetic to the idea that students should meet certain standards to participate in extracurriculars, I think it would be beneficial to set those standards at the level of excellence while balancing the expectations around maintaining a reasonable GPA. By balancing the two, we can maintain a healthy standard for all participants without overly restricting who can participate. In conclusion, I believe it is acceptable to allow students to participate in extracurriculars without maintaining a high GPA if their respective organizations hold high standards and adopt a rigorous standard for excellence. By setting the bar at a challenging level while balancing the expectations around participation, we can encourage students to both maintain a high GPA and meet the high standards of their organizations.

Car ownership and usage causes global warming and traffic. Limiting the number of cars owned by individual families can help reduce pollution, traffic, and clutter in their houses and increase safety by reducing the number of vehicles on the road. In Bogota, Colombia, the Car-Free program reduced the number of cars in the city by promoting programs to encourage citizens to decrease or eliminate their car ownership. These promoted programs included free loaner cars for those who needed one, subsidies for public transportation, increasing minimum distances between cars and more. These changes resulted in a decreasing trend of car ownership in Bogota, while at the same time, reducing the amount of pollution, traffic, and clutter in the city. Similar changes can be seen in decreasing car usage in the United States, where car usage has plateaued in recent years. Acknowledging this change, some cities across the United States are looking to implement programs to encourage car sharing and reduce car usage. These programs may include subsidies for car-sharing memberships and perks for car sharing, increased minimum distances between cars, and limiting vehicle license plates to reduce the number of cars on the road. Broad Street School is a historic school at the intersection of Broad and North Broad streets in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania. It was designed by John Havill and built in 1881. It is three stories in height and nine bays wide. It features paired wooden doors with rounded hoods and windows with rounded sills and lintels. The building is located within the West Market district and is surrounded by commercial buildings. It was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1972 and was renovated in 2006. The building is owned by a developer and is set to become a 14-story building. Broad Street School was listed as one of the Historic American Buildings Survey (HABS) buildings in the region, and is included in a 1998 survey of historic public and commercial buildings in Philadelphia and the surroundings which was also published by the Library of Congress. In 2009, the Broad Street School was used as a location for a scene in The Social Network, a movie about the founding of Facebook. In 2013, it was announced that a reality show called School Pride, which follows a group of inner-city students through the process of transforming their school into a GCLS (Government-Controlled Living Site), would be filmed at the Broad Street School for its first season. In February 2020, it was reported that the Philadelphia school district was considering selling the Broad Street School due to its "skyrocketing" maintenance costs. Hurricane water restrictions means most people in California must reduce their water usage by thirty percent. During a water emergency, residents are asked to use less water in-between watering schedules, instead of waiting until their next watering schedule, when the water will be even less effective. In general, the general person 1000 years ago would not know what day of the week it was. The year? Would they have a reason to need to know? Let’s start with day of the week. We know this due to a postulation by Dennis Miller, which puts the day of the week in the 14th century. There are many assumptions that need to be made to come to this conclusion, such as people having access to a calendar and being able to understand the association between a month and a season. Without that association, it is unclear if they would understand the connection between a week and a weekend. Without that understanding, it is unlikely that they would know what day of the week it was. Knowing what year it was would be useful in order to manage temporal references. For example, it is likely that the year 1000 was previously mentioned in historic times, so a person would have some sense of what year it was.

Participating in at least one extracurricular activity has numerous benefits for students, including making new friends, participating in sports, discovering new interests, and potential career opportunities. Through participating in extracurricular activities, students also often discover their passions and interests. Additionally, some students pursue their interests with further education and career opportunities. These benefits make participating in at least one extracurricular activities worth the time and effort. Ultimately, the benefits greatly outweigh the costs, such as spending time and money on the activity. In summary, by participating in at least one extracurricular activity, students discover the benefits, and outweigh the costs. This is a clear and concise argument in favor of encouraging students to participate in at least one extracurricular activity. The benefits of participating in extracurricular activities are significant, including making new friends, participating in sports, discovering new interests, and career development. The writer then details the costs associated with participating in extracurricular activities, and notes that the benefits vastly outweigh the costs. In summary, this statement helps students, parents, and school staff understand the benefits of participating in at least one extracurricular activity.

Aubrey Lewis (born January 1, 1975) is an American musician. He is a member of the bands Mineral and Gorillaz, and a solo artist. Aubrey Lewis is a singer, songwriter and multi-instrumentalist. He is best known as the lead singer and guitarist for the bands Mineral and Gorillaz. He formed Mineral in Boston in 1998, and the group released three studio albums between 2001 and 2007. Lewis founded Gorillaz in 1999 with Damon Albarn, and remained a member up until the band's hiatus in 2010. He also worked with Blur's Graham Coxon on a side-project band called the Graham Coxon Project. Lewis released his debut solo album, Heavens, in 2013, and followed it with II in 2017. His second Gorillaz album, Demon Days, was released in 2022. America's favorite Ambassador of showbusiness pride! Aubrey Lewis is a singer, songwriter, multi-instrumentalist and all around good guy. The main strength of the paper is that it performs an empirical comparison of the GELU activation against the ReLU and ELU activation functions, and the paper performs performance measurements on several different datasets. The paper also have some limitations such as limited network and dataset complexity, and the paper fails to introduce the related works on combining nonlinearities with dropout. better WORKS OF THE PAPER: The paper Bridging Nonlinearities and Stochastic Regularizers with Gaussian Error Linear Units addresses the question of whether stochastic regularizers can improve neural network performance. The paper perform an empirical comparison of the GELU activation function against the ReLU and ELU activation functions. The main strength of the paper is that it performs an empirical comparison on several different datasets. The paper also have some limitations such as limited network and dataset complexity. CONCLUSIONS: The answer in the paper is mixed, with some tasks showing improvement, but in other tasks the regularizer performed worse than the baseline. The author, Dr. Kai-Wei Chang, gives an excellent overview of deep learning applied to function approximation. Deep learning algorithms, in particular, Deep Neural Networks, have been very successful in pattern recognition and function approximation. The author has provided a strong foundation of function approximation using theatz functions, showing various levels of approximation errors with various encoding/learning strategies. He has also discussed various modifications to this basic framework, such as using Convolutional Neural Networks to improve the learning efficiency and using BNNs to reduce approximation error in high-dim functions. The main takeaway from this section is that deep learning has been very successful in function approximation and generating valuable insight to those who are new to this field. However, the detail provided on function approximation strategies can be overwhelming to someone who is new to this area, and the applicability of deep learning in certain problem domains is not well-explained. ゃ strengthen #1 The importance of function approximation and why it is difficult to achieve low approximation error with empirical methods Function approximation is essential in machine learning because many machine learning tasks are modelized as mapping from inputs to outputs. In practice, we rarely have access to the direct evaluations of the mapping functions. Hence, the tasks are done by observing the outputs given some inputs, which is called empirical approximation. There are two requirements for an empirical approximation method to work. First, the method must provide a good approximation of the output function for given inputs. Second, the method must be able to efficiently find the inputs that give the same output with the approximated function. The first requirement is usually the most difficult one to achieve and hence the prominent term used in this area is approximation error. Low approximation error (or optimal error) guarantees the good fitting of the mapping function and vice-versa. In this section, the author provided a clear overview of function approximation via deep neural networks and why approximation error is hard to achieve. Specifically, the approximation methods have to find the appropriate weights and biases for the deep neural networks to achieve good fitting for the mapping functions, but this is a difficult problem because the mapping functions are usually non-linear and high-dimensional. Hence, the method that uses regular grids to approximate the non-linear mapping functions (e.g, radf and gaussian processes) will result in high approximation errors. The author also provided a effective method to solve this problem using Neural Networks, which results in the modern practice, Deep Neural Networks. The author discussed other methods such as using different activation functions to reduce the approximation error in Neural Networks and using Bayesian techniques to further improve the approximation accuracy. The author also provided an overview of deep learning models for regression and classification tasks. These models have different restrictions and strengths, such as the widely used linear models can easily suffer from overfitting but it's hard to utilize more complex relationships between variables, and neural network models can solve these problems, but they require huge amount of labeled data for efficient training.

Despite the many benefits of driverless cars, such as increased fuel efficiency, reduced human error, and improved safety, there are a number of potential risks which could adversely affect the industry. The biggest threat is the safety of passengers and people on the road, as automated vehicles are often not able to deal with various and unpredictable situations on the road. Additionally, automated cars might not be able to handle issues involving other drivers being distracted or vehicle failures, which could lead to accidents. Another potential issue is privacy, as driverless cars might be able to collect massive amounts of personal data from passengers. Despite these potential issues, however, driverless cars can still have a transformative effect on the automobile industry and transport industry in general. For instance, automated vehicles could significantly reduce the number of car accidents, saving millions of lives. Moreover, new technologies in the automobile industry can be enabled by automated cars, such as autonomous driving, remote driving, and car-to-car communications. This could have a huge impact on many industries, such as the car rental industry, trucking industry, and logistic industry. Further, automated vehicles could greatly reduce operational costs for car manufacturers, as they would not need to produce or maintain human drivers. Despite the many benefits of driverless cars, however, we will still need to invest in R&D to address the potential issues and ensure the safe deployment of automated vehicles. We also need to invest in ethical regulations, to protect the personal data of passengers and ensure their privacy. The introduction of driverless cars will still take time, but once these challenges have been overcome, this new technology will revolutionize transportation and positively impact the lives of many people.

Go before a board at your church to be ordained. Being ordained begins with an evaluation of your spiritual life. Connect with a mentor to be ordained. Attend seminary to be ordained. Seminary is essentially a religious school where you learn all the skills needed to become a pastor. Conduct your internship to be ordained. Part of your seminary work requires completing an internship at a charity, hospital, or chaplaincy to be ordained. Engage in supplemental training to be ordained. Your seminary will provide denomination-specific training so that you understand your community and denomination in greater depth. Write an ordination paper to be ordained. Your ordination paper is like a final essay, and should include a statement of your faith and what you learned in seminary, as well as what you look forward to accomplishing during your time with the church. Become a probationary pastor to be ordained. Face the ordination interviews to be ordained. The ordination process varies somewhat depending on the specific denomination, but you'll likely need to pass an oral examination where you'll be tested on your knowledge of scripture.

Jim Sladky (born James William Sladky; January 1, 1930 – July 9, 1983) was an American professional baseball outfielder, who played in Major League Baseball (MLB) for the Brooklyn Dodgers, Pittsburgh Pirates, Cincinnati Reds, and Philadelphia Phillies between 1955 and 1969. He also played in Japan for the Hankyu Braves from 1963 to 1965. An excellent fielding player, Sladky had a.303 career batting average, with over 300 games in the outfield. He stole over 150 bases in 12 seasons, and was an integral part of the 1958 and 1960 National League (NL) champion teams. The National League (NL) voted him NL Player of the Week for April 5–11, 1963, and NL Player of the Month for September 1963. His most memorable event as a ballplayer occurred during a game at Wrigley Field on April 25, 1963. With two out in the bottom of the 9th inning, he hit a home run over the left-field wall at Wrigley Field, and carried it into the clubhouse with him. The game was delayed 20 minutes while he was showered and changed, and he was awarded both the game-winning home run and the official MLB Home Run championship. Sladky was a teammate of fellow future Hall of Fame members Willie Mays and Frank Robinson on the 1958 NL champion team. He also played in the 1960 World Series, although the series was lost to the San Francisco Giants.

Write a WikiHow article content given a title and a headline. Use approximately 300 words. Title: How to Install Gentoo Linux from Ubuntu Headline: Make sure that you have superuser privileges on your Ubuntu box; and an Internet connection - preferably a fast one., You need to install chroot on Ubuntu., Either repartition existing partitions, or create a new partition from free Hard Disk space., Format the new partition(s) using a File System of your choice (for /home, /, /boot and /var it is preferable to use ext2, ext3 or reiser2).,Create a new folder, /mnt/gentoo and one for each of the separate partitions that you created., Mount your new partitions on here: sudo mount /dev/sda5 /mnt/gentoo sudo mount /dev/sda6 /mnt/gentoo/home where here, sda5 and 6 are the partitions containing your prospective root and home directories respectively., Check that your date is correct (type date)., Open a web browsing program of your choice, and navigate to here.,Move it to the Gentoo folder mv stage3*.bz2* /mnt/gentoo., Move yourself there (cd /mnt/gentoo) and check the tarball through md5: md5sum -c stage3*.md5., Extract the tarball!, You now have a few basic programs installed on the Gentoo partition; next, you need to install Portage: Gentoo's package management system which allows you to have complete control over what is on your system., At this stage, you may want to set some compile flags., Want to optimise your download?, Before you immerse yourself in your new system, you need to make sure that you have a few vital settings copied over from Ubuntu as it is., Now you can chroot!, Congratulations!, Next, you need to compile the most vital component of any operating system: its kernel., Remember installing portage earlier?,For the sake of clarity, at this stage I'll assume that you want to install the latest kernel (2.6) rather than 2.4., Now we must set our USE flags., Every flag you want to add is simply a word.,To choose your USE flags, check out the Gentoo USE Flags documentation to see what flags you might want to include.,Once you have chosen what flags to use, navigate to /etc/make.conf and put in the flags you want., Let's now set up your timezone.,Now that we have our timezone set up, it is time to actually download our kernel sources and configure the kernel.,Run (# emerge gentoo-sources) to download your kernel sources., This next step can be tricky for first timers., Run cd /usr/src/linux make menuconfig, This will bring up the kernel configuration menu.,Select any network drivers you may need, such as ethernet drivers or wireless drivers (or both).,Choose your processor type and family., Once you've finished, type ( make && make modules_install) to start compiling the kernel and kernel modules.,make -j2 && make modules_install, Now we need to copy your kernel image to /boot., Now let's configure your kernel modules. Be aware of, and appreciate all the things that your parents do for you from day to day, month to month, year to year. Recognize and respect the fact that they have been through many more life experience than you, and can offer you a great deal of knowledge about the world you live in. It's a given that sometimes your parents may seem totally clueless and you may feel like they can't possibly relate to the things your dealing with currently, But believe what I say, they actually do understand some of the things that your going through like peer pressure, friendship issues, and a lot of other things that kids your age has to deal with simply because they were once a kid just like you. Remember that your parents love you more than anything else in the world, an have high expectations of what they want you to achieve because they believe in you and want to see you be at your best as you become an adult. When at times you feel that your parents don’t listen or don’t take you seriously, take a moment to think about what you want them to know and discuss your problems calmly with them and make sure you listen to their side of the story. Make a daily effort not to complain about having to obey your parents commands. Be helpful and assume responsibility around the house by doing extra chores when you can, just to show that you want to do your part in your everyday family life. Remember that new clothes, toys, cell phones, and money allowances are always nice, but be aware of your parents' income and how much they can afford to spend on the things that they buy for your enjoyment. Remember that one of the most important things you can do for your parents is to be a loving person. And last but not least...Everyday, take the opportunity to tell your parents you love them. Talk to them regularly. Visit them often. Include your parents in your family activities. Help them maintain active lives. Organize visits with their grandchildren. Be loving. I hope this gives you a better understanding of what your parents go through every day and the things that they have to deal with that you may not appreciate or understand. It is important that you show them love and never take their efforts and time for you for granted.

The authors present a novel approach to cross-lingual named entity recognition (NER) via a two-stage approach that exploits both lexical and syntax information. First, they train an entity name token detector, which maps each input word to its corresponding entity name entity representation. Then, given a weakly labeled training set, they learn a cross-lingual representation for NER by projecting the source entity name representations into the space of the annotation language (i.e., target NER tags). This approach is evaluated on the cross-lingual wikipedia edition and shows competitive results to the state of the art on this task. This approach addresses the NER problem through two main insights. First, the authors show that entity name representations contain valuable entity name information, thus allowing to recover some part of the target NER annotation labels. Second, they observe that due to the shallow linguistic similarity between languages, projecting entity name representations of one language into the space of the other language usually results in low-ranked representations. This suggests that an aligned high-dimensional space offers a good basis for cross-lingual projection, as opposed to the original source and target spaces. The main drawback of this method is that it requires the construction of a projected space in which to perform the projection. This projection is learned from a weakly labeled dataset and is therefore limited in its application to new languages for which no labeled training data is available. The approach is evaluated on a set of 11 languages with in-domain data (e.g., WIKI8CHN) and out-of-domain data (e.g., Wikipedia Parallel Dataset) and generally performs competitively to state of the art methods. In addition, the authors provide an ablation study showing that the proposed approach is able to leverage both lexical and syntactic annotations to improve the NER performance.

Flicker noise is the noise associated with the variation of light caused by rapidly turning the light on and off. The origin of flicker noise has been a topic of study for many years, with a large body of research dedicated to understanding it in resistive electrical conductors. Here we report direct detection of flicker noise in superconductors. We detect noise down to 6 parts per million (ppm), and find the flicker noise spectra can be well fitted by the broken power law with 1/f^α characteristics. Our observations are consistent with expectations for a single electron charge hop between localized states in a resistive environment. We observe significantly different noise spectra at different places on the sample, with the noise at the surface of the samples following the 1/f spectrum expected for a purely resistive environment, while the bulk noise is closer to the 1/f^3 characteristic expected in a pure crystalline environment. We speculate that the difference in the spectra arises from quantum interference between different potential fluctuations, modeled in 1/f and 1/f^3 forms.

The Kraichgau Railway is a 32.5 km long metre gauge rail link between Kraichtal and Lorsch that was opened in 1892. It is one of the oldest rail connections in the Palatinate and was one of the first standard gauge railways in the region. It is owned and operated by the Rhön-Alpine-Economic-Area (FLE). The Kraichgau Railway is used for training and transportation of railroad employees and timber. It is one of the last major narrow gauge railways in Germany and was awarded the Blue Fire Engine Award for outstanding preserved railway equipment. upgrade The Kraichgau Railway is a 32.5 km long metre gauge rail link between Kraichtal and Lorsch that was opened in 1892.
