It is one of the oldest rail connections in the Palatinate and was one of the first standard gauge railways in the region. It is owned and operated by the Rhön-Alpine-Economic-Area (FLE). The Kraichgau Railway was built in 1892 (financing by the Kingdom of Bavaria,owner at that time) to connect Kraichtal via Gloggnitz with the Royal Bavarian State Railways. In order to protect the economy of the Kraichgau region, the Kraichgau Railway was converted to standard gauge in 1910/11. Over the years, changes were made, such as the replacement of many of the existing Kraichgau narrow gauge 2'OD - 2'OH mixed gauge tracks with standard gauge tracks. In the Second World War, the railway was almost completely destroyed by bombing. After the war, it was completely reconstructed and returned to operation in its original narrow gauge configuration. In the late 1960s, the Kraichgau Railway was equipped with the first standard two-way rack locomotives in the Rhön-Alpine-Economic-Area. A Blue Fire Engine Award was awarded to the Kraichgau Railway in 1990. Currently, it is operated by the Rhön-Alpine-Economic-Area (FLE).

It is hard to say what the physique of a medieval knight would have looked like. During this time period, knighthood was a status symbol and rich and powerful people would pay to have a knight dubbed into their service. To qualify for knighthood, a man would have to fulfill very stringent requirements. They would have had to have proven their chivalry, valor, strength, and fitness to a panel of judges. This would likely have turned them into accomplished athletes. They would have trained with heavy armor, horses, and weapons. In addition to this, knights were expected to stay in perfect physical condition. If they were not, they would have been left greatly demoralized. In contrast, to qualify for knighthood, a man would have had to show the Judges that they had achieved the highest standards of chivalry, valor, strength, and fitness. These men likely trained with weights, horses, and armor. To this were added expectations of maintaining their physical fitness. Because of this, I think it is likely that medieval knights would have been very fit and muscular, but also larger than average. Their equipment would have inspired strength and fitness, rather than the body types of athletes today.

This paper aims to bring attention to the linear relationship between the number of parameters and the expressiveness of RNNs, and the constant relationship between the number of units and the expressiveness of RNNs. The main insights are (1) All RNNs storage capacity of task information is approximately 5 bits per parameter and one real number from input history per hidden unit, and (2) For several tasks, it is the per-task parameter capacity bound that determines performance, while for another the unit capacity bound. The rest of the paper mainly focuses on the proof of (1). The proof of (2) is more about comparing training difficulty of several RNN architectures. The high storage cost of RNNs makes them inefficient for many applications that require processing long sequences. The discovery that the expressiveness of RNNs does not scale with the number of parameters or the number of hidden units suggests that significant expressiveness might be achievable at low cost. This paper reveals the significant storage cost of RNNs, and presents two architectures that reduce this cost. The first architecture generalizes LSTM to Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) by reducing the number of gates. The result is even cheaper to train and more efficient at modeling long time series. The second architecture replaces gates with a fixed diagonal matrix to further reduce cost. These two reductions are combined and further optimized, leading to a cheaper and more scalable model. The main conclusions are: 1. The expressiveness of RNNs is linear with the number of parameters, but not proportional to it. For the same model and training procedure, RNNs are no more expressive than feedforward networks of the same size. 2. The expressiveness of RNNs is approximately fixed with the number of hidden units. More hidden units imply more expressiveness, but at the cost of more parameters and more training cost. 3. For some tasks, it is the parameter capacity that limits expressiveness, while for others it is the unit capacity. 4. The cost model can be optimized further to make it cheaper and more scalable. 5. Given the same model and procedure, replacing gates with a fixed diagonal matrix reduces both cost and size. Overall, this paper provides a clear cost picture of RNNs and opens up the possibilities of more expressive but cheaper neural network designs. The author's approach to solving the first four problems is to perform the same parameter and unit capacity bound analysis as in other papers, but with a particular focus on the information storage cost of RNNs. The storage cost is revealed in three parts. The first is the number of bits needed to store task information per parameter, which is linear in the number of parameters. The second is the number of real numbers from the input history per hidden unit, which is approximately 5 bits per parameter. The third is the number of parameters per unit, which is a constant for all common RNN architectures.

I agree that driverless cars can have tremendous benefits to the world. The ability to remove the cost of driving from the economy will make goods and services cheaper for people all over the world. Even with this perceived negative of taking jobs away from humans, driverless cars can allow people to pursue their hobbies and other passions without the concern of being trapped in one place. Another big potential upside to driverless cars is the reduction of deadly car accidents. With over 4,000 people dying in car accidents every day, the safety benefits of driverless cars could potentially save millions of lives every year. Although I see many benefits to driverless cars, there are also some potential concerns that I think should be taken into account. First, the costs associated with driverless cars could be very expensive. Currently, self-driving cars require a lot of sensors that will cost a lot of money, and the overall system needs constant connectivity to the internet in order to work properly. Without a reliable connection, the car could malfunction and cause an accident. In addition to the hardware costs, maintaining a driverless car requires someone to be on-call at all times, which could also be expensive. In my opinion, the total cost of ownership for driverless cars should be much higher than traditional cars, which could make this technology inaccessible to many people. There are also safety concerns with driverless cars. In the article, the writer describes a fatal crash of one of the prototypes. Although it was determined that the software, not humans, was at fault for the accident, it could potentially be difficult to tell the difference between a sick passenger and a frozen sensor. An error in the software could have serious consequences, and innocent lives could be lost as a result. With all of these potential issues, I think that driverless cars are not ready for the masses. Despite these concerns, however, I think that driverless cars could be implemented in the future. With continued improvement in artificial intelligence and wireless technology, the overall cost of driverless cars could be reduced. The wide availability of sensor data from smartphones could also make it possible to safely drive without internet connectivity. Although some fundamental problems with driverless car technology still need to be worked out, I think that driverless cars could be very promising for the future. Although driverless cars have many positive implications, they could also have some potential pitfalls. First, the high initial cost of driverless cars could make them inaccessible to many people. In addition, the system could have security issues that put people's safety at risk. Although there are many aspects of driverless cars that require improvements, I believe that these issues could be overcome in the future and that driverless car adoption could have tremendous benefits to society.

Nick Altrock (born Nicholas Altrock; July 18, 1963 – December 28, 1992) was an American musician and songwriter, best known as the lead singer and guitarist of the punk rock band Off With Their Heads. Nick was the son of Russ Altrock, a professional musician and audio engineer who was famous for his work with The Beach Boys and Squeaky Scrape. At age 17, Nick joined Off With Their Heads. The band went on to become one of the most influential in the genre's history, and their 1985 debut EP Your Move... is often credited with helping to bring punk rock into the mainstream. Nick was dedicated to Off With Their Heads, and the band released five albums between 1984 and 1990. During this time, Off With Their Heads were frequently featured on numerous television programs and music channels, including MTV. Nick had completed recording his first solo album, which was to be released on Epitaph Records. However, on December 28, 1992, Nick died from an accidental heroin and cocaine overdose. He was 34 years old. Nick's legacy was honored in 1994 with the creation of the annual Off With Their Heads music festival, as well as being the subject of the eponymous biography by music journalist Steven Haver. Nick's brother, Ron Altrock, is also a musician and audio engineer, who worked with Nick and Off With Their Heads. Nick's daughter, Sky Altrock, is also a musician, releasing two EPs under the name Skye. She is also the daughter of Off With Their Heads drummer Dave Konopka.

Hunting rabbits with an air rifle is a fun way to get close to wild rabbits and helps reduce the number of native rabbits in the UK. There are two main methods for shooting rabbits with an air rifle. The first, and probably most common way, is to use a soft-faced air rifle which can be safely shot at ranges up to around 30 yards (27.4 meters). A sound producing device, such as a bell, is often used in conjunction with a soft-faced air rifle to help lure in rabbits. The second way to hunt rabbits with an air rifle is to use a hardened air rifle which can shoot at ranges of up to 50 yards (46.2 meters). These are usually semiautomatic rifles, but can also be fully automatic. To use a hardened air rifle to hunt rabbits you will need to find a landowner who is willing to grant you permission to shoot on his property. Always ask for permission in advance and ensure that you have enough money to pay for any damage that is caused. Finally, ensure that you are properly trained and licensed before using an air rifle to hunt rabbits. Following these steps will allow you to have fun and get close to wild rabbits without causing harm or damage to the local rabbit population. Hunting rabbits with an air rifle is legal in the UK but you will need to be licensed if you choose to hunt with a hardened air rifle. Licenses are available to shoot for people over the age of 16. Different air rifles can be used for hunting rabbits. Some examples include soft-faced air rifles and hardened air rifles. Soft-faced air rifles can be used at ranges up to around 30 yards (27.4 meters) and are often used in conjunction with a sound producing device, such as a bell, to attract rabbits. Hardened air rifles are capable of shooting at longer ranges of up to 50 yards (46.2 meters) and are often used without a sound producing device. Using a hardened air rifle to hunt rabbits requires the permission of the landowner and is often more expensive than using a soft-faced air rifle. Always ensure that you are properly trained and licensed before using an air rifle to hunt rabbits. Obtaining a license will require a medical check and a payment of a small fee. After passing the medical check you will need to attend a training course, which can be completed online. After completing the training course you will receive a certificate, which can be used to produce evidence when applying for a license. Finally, it is required that you have valid CC license if you choose to hunt with a hardened air rifle. After this you are ready to start hunting! Find a location that rabbits frequent, such as fields and wooded areas. Use a mapping service such as Google Maps to mark out the areas that you will hunt. Spend time familiarizing yourself with the areas that you will hunt before carrying out your license checks. Secure the location as a rabbit hide, making sure that you hide your soft-faced air rifle behind a bush or tree. Most rabbits will respond to a moving target and can be lured into the rabbit hide with a moving noise, such as that of a bike or in a game called 'double trouble', where two rabbits are hidden and one is moved around to lured the other rabbit out. When a rabbit approaches your rabbit hide you must be prepared to take your shot, aim for the head just behind the eye, and fire immediately. Be ready to wait for a few minutes after taking your shot before harvesting the rabbit. If you are lucky you will only have to wait a few minutes before a second rabbit shows up in your rabbit hide to join your first rabbit. At this point you can harvest both rabbits and repeat the process as often as you like. Do not disturb any scavenger species such as foxes and deer who might also frequent the area. The rabbits that you hunt will help maintain the local rabbit population and help restore species diversity to areas where rabbits are hunted. Hunting rabbits with an air rifle is a fun way to get close to wild rabbits and helps reduce the number of native rabbits in the UK. It is recommended that you only hunt in areas that you are fully prepared to hunt in and make sure that you leave no mess for local wildlife.

West London Orbital is a proposed orbital railway line of the London Overground network in London, England. If constructed, it would connect North London via an approximately equidistant path between the North London Orbital line and the West London Line, both of which are railway lines. The proposal has been made multiple times with no committed funding or political support, and the idea has gained little traction. alysis West London Orbital is a proposed orbital railway line of the London Overground network in London, England. The line would connect North London via an approximately equidistant path between the North London Orbital line and the West London Line, both of which are railway lines. The proposal has been made multiple times with no committed funding or political support, and the idea has gained little traction. Orbital lines are proposed railway lines of a London Overground network that would connect city centre stations without using the existing heavy-rail network. The North London Orbital line and the West London Line both stand to gain from an orbital connection, as passengers could more conveniently connect between them without having to navigate the heavy-rail network or pay heavy fares. The West London Orbital also has the potential to extend further into the London suburbs, as a direct orbital line would allow for more stops than the existing heavy-rail network. The West London Orbital would have approximately the same length as the North London Orbital line, and follow an approximately equidistant path between those lines. Unlike existing orbital lines, this one would not pass through the airspace over London, in order to minimise costs. Funding has been sought for this project through the London Infrastructure Fund, but so far the project has not gained approval.

How to Build a Koi Fish Pond1 Buy all your supplies., Measure the plastic pool as best you can using a measuring tape., Dig an appropriate hole for the pond, going by the measurements you've made., Place the pool into the hole., Spray the inside of the pool with the protective spray., Fill the pool up with spring or fresh water till it is about 80% filled., Fill the pool with the gravel and spread it out evenly across the bottom., Put about 1 ounce of beneficial bacteria in the water., Place the plants in the desired location., Add the filter to the water., Place the koi into their new pond., Koi fish deposit a large amount of waste into the pond so if you want the water to be clean and clear then you will need to add a filtration system that consists of a pump and a filter., Traditional swimming pool silica sand filters are not a good choice to use for Koi fish due to the volume of waste plugging the sand filter., Ozone is an oxidizer and a chemical free sanitizer in ponds., Be sure to separate the suction points for the plumbing system., In the "Things You Need" section below, I suggest using a floating filter to clean the pond., Golf ball snails and bottom feeding fish can help to process algae from hard to clean surfaces like the gravel bed floor. Pre-made ponds come in a thin black plastic and thicker acrylic resin materials. Things you need: - Pool - Protective spray - Gravel - Water - Clean water source - Bacteria - Algae killer - Plants - Pond filter - Filter media - Pump - Access to water - Suction points

Jean (full name Jean Carlos Santos Oliveira; born February 3, 1994) is a Brazilian footballer who plays as a centre back for Greek Superleague side Aris Thessaloniki. Jean started his professional career at lowly Botafogo in Rio de Janeiro before being spotted by Portuguese giants Sporting CP and signing for them in 2013. He made his debut for the Lisbon side against Benfica in the 2013–14 UEFA Champions League group stage and went on to make 21 total appearances that season. In June 2014, Jean signed a four-year contract with French Ligue 1 side Olympique de Marseille, reuniting him with former Sporting CP coach José Mourinho. During his time at the club he suffered two relegations in five seasons, having to play Europa League football due to the far inferiority of the club's league position each year. In 2022–23, he made 35 appearances and was named in the LFP Team of the Season. On 1 August 2023, Jean joined Greek Superleague side Aris Thessaloniki on a three-year contract. Jean represented Brazil at Under-20 level and made his senior international debut in 2017. He represented his country at the 2017 and 2019 Copa América, as well as the 2018 and 2020 editions of the Summer Olympics. In November 2023, Jean received his first red card in professional football after headbutting Greek goalkeeper AntonisNikopopoulos during a 1–3 loss to Olympiakos. The Football Association's Control and Disciplinary Body handed him a five-game ban for the offence.

A cost-benefit analysis of a communication looks at the benefits called "Pros" and the downsides called "Cons" in the conversation/situation being analyzed. It is useful to describe each consideration in its own column with a rating from one to ten for the benefit and a cost respectively. If the considerations do not relate to each other, a cost-benefit analysis of the communication will not have any predetermined result. An example is shown below on how to evaluate the social exchange theory. Benefit: The group respect that you brought in is valuable. Cons: You will be interrupted more often, you will be less able to express your thoughts freely, and the interaction will be less deep. Benefit: You will be better able to express your thoughts freely. Cons: You will have to take a 15 minute walk to get there, the food will not be as good as at the restaurant next door, and you will not be able to take advantage of some interesting products that will be shown. Evaluation: The evaluation shows that the consideration of the freedom to express your thoughts freely is more valuable than the benefit of the group respect that you bring in. The analysis can help to determine the points on which the exchange is possible to improve, so that the conversation can be more effective.

An X-ray spectral analysis of the central regions of NGC 4593 was performed with data obtained from the Chandra X-ray telescope. The spectrum could be well fitted with a simple phenomenological model comprising a narrow emission line component, generated by reflection from cold matter, and a continuum component. The continuum is well represented by a single-temperature blackbody with temperature kT = 0.65 keV. The equivalent width of the emission line is $510^{+230}_{-200}$ eV. The observed line energy is consistent with an emission feature from highly ionised Iron, but more data are needed to confirm this. The gravitating mass, electron density and gas cooling rate in the central regions of NGC 4593 were estimated by modelling the reflection spectrum. It was found that the gravitating mass is 1.3 x 10^9 M_{\odot}$, the electron density is 1.3 x 10^8 cm^-3 and the gas cooling rate is 8.8 x 10^{39} erg s^-1.

A dedicated article describing the design, physics capabilities and initial performance of a proposed $2\times250$ GeV CLIC $\gamma\gamma$ collider based on its drive beam FEL is presented. The proposed CLIC $\gamma\gamma$ collider will have a significant improvement in $\gamma\gamma$ luminosity over current colliders, and its initial performance shows that it is feasible to explore the CLIC $hh$ parameter space with enough events for discovery and studying the nature of new phenomena. The new $\gamma\gamma$ collider will be based on the CLIC $e^+e^-$ linear collider drive beam facility, and use the CLIC 1.04 km drive beam parameter as the start-of-train energy and luminosity. The main structures of the collider are as follows. First, the new $\gamma\gamma$ collider will use two250 GeV CLIC $\gamma$s to drive a FEL to produce two photons which collide to produce two 250 GeV photons, and the collision energy will be transferred by two sextupole magnets. Next, the photons will enter the former CLIC pick-up point and make two CLIC $\pi^0$s by Compton back-scattering. The CLIC $\pi^0$s will be cooled by two cooling linacs and exit the collider, forming the collider $\gamma\gamma$s. Finally, the beam will run through a double-focusing final focus scanner to form a stable circulating beam with two250 GeV $\gamma\gamma$s. The physics potential of the CLIC $\gamma\gamma$ collider is analyzed. The precision measurements of the standard model (SM) particles, such as the Higgs and gauge bosons, and searches for new phenomena will be performed using the high luminosity and high energy $\gamma\gamma$ collider. In addition, the potential of the $\gamma\gamma$ collider to study SM BSM models will also be shown. The excellent event rates and stable circulating beam of the CLIC $\gamma\gamma$ collider will make it an ideal machine to discover new phenomena and study the nature of new phenomena. This study shows that the CLIC $\gamma\gamma$ collider is feasible with a current 250 GeV drive beam FEL, and the collaboration between CLIC and FEL field should be considered seriously.

Using observations from the Fermi Gamma Ray Space Telescope, we have detected gamma-rays from 37 supernova remnants (SNRs). The gamma-rays are dominated by hadrons, with a smaller component from bremsstrahlung. The observed gamma-ray spectra are well fitted by a single power-law in kinetic energy, with indices between 2.0 and 2.7. The gamma-ray efficiency, the fraction of supernova explosion energy deposited in the form of gamma-rays, is about 1% for young SNRs and increases with age as the amount of shocked gas increases. Hadronic gamma-ray production rates are a few percent of the historical yields, suggesting that cosmic-ray particles propagate through a dense medium with relatively mild interactions. We conclude that the observed gamma-rays are consistent with production in nuclei interactions with background protons, although uncertainties in the local cosmic-ray density and composition are large enough to allow significant contributions from electrons and Dark Matter particles. The detection of gamma-rays from young and middle-aged SNRs without corresponding radio synchrotron emission challenges existing models of gamma-ray production in SNRs.

The 2000 New Zealand rugby league season was the 75th season of professional rugby league football in New Zealand. The Labour Party, led by Nathan Guy, won the 1999 election, and formed the fourth Labour government of New Zealand. The second National Party government, led by Jim Bolger, was defeated in the 2002 election, resulting in another coalition led by Bolger, with Helen Clark as Clark Cabinet minister. The season was marred by the tragic deaths of New Zealand rugby league star, Ralph Hardie, and France-based New Zealand United Sevens coach, Gary Larson. Hardie died in a car crash on the way to a training camp in the United States, while Larson was killed in a car crash on the north island, soon after the end of the season. The 2000 season was also notable for the first grand final appearance by the New Zealand national rugby league team, which featured a record number of players of Polynesian and Māori heritage. The season began with the first (and so far only) merit coaching award, won by Dunedin-based Tony Kemp. The Warriors won the Ricoh RSA New Zealand National Rugby League Championship, beating the Canterbury Bulls 22-18 at Ellerslie. The Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs won the vacuum after ending the regular season with the best play-off record, winning the minor premiership and qualifying for the Grand Final. The Balmain Tigers completed the season by defeating Canterbury-Bankstown 18-16 in the 2002 NRL Grand Final. This was the last Grand Final played before the abolition of the NSWRL premiership and introduction of the NRL Premiership. In the 2002 New Zealand rugby league player summary, the Junior Kiwis won a record 19th consecutive game, beating the Australia national Second-tier QLD brumbies 36-12. The Canterbury-Bankstown fullback, Danny Wicks, won the Dally M award for best player in the league. Ponsonby United, coached by Andrew Caddes, won the foundation Henderson medal as U21 champions. The New Zealand national rugby league team, coached by Jim Duffy, failed to make the semi-finals of the 2000 World Cup. The Kiwis won two test matches against Great Britain, winning New Zealand's first test match win against Great Britain since 1960, and drawing the other match, to finish their season with a record of 8-8. The New Zealand rugby league season was marred by the tragic deaths of New Zealand rugby league star, Ralph Hardie, and France-based New Zealand United Sevens coach, Gary Larson. Hardie died in a car crash on the way to a training camp in the United States, while Larson was killed in a car crash on the north island, soon after the end of the season. The 2000 season was also notable for the first grand final appearance by the New Zealand national rugby league team, which featured a record number of players of Polynesian and Māori heritage.

Overall, I enjoyed this paper. I found the topic to be important and useful. The experimental design and findings were clearly articulated, and I think the findings are plausible and actionable. My main concerns relate to the empirical validation of the proposed approach. I think the experiments could be more rigorous and provide stronger evidence for the generalization of the proposed approach. I also have some reservations about the relevance of the methodology to POS tagging. I think the methodology might work better for Named Entity Recognition (NER) than Part of Speech (POS) tagging. Named entities usually have clear entity types and are usually locally distinct from other text. Thus, it may be easier to identify clean training and test datasets, and the confusion between supervision and guidance may not arise. I think the methodology could be more generally applied and validated through experimentation, and I think the discussion of the limitations could be expanded. MY STARES FOR DISCUSSION I really enjoyed this paper and found it to be both interesting and useful. I think the proposed approach is general and could be applied to other NLP tasks. However, I would recommend explicitly stating how the methodology can be applied to other NLP tasks. For example, I think this methodology might be better applied to Named Entity Recognition (NER) than Part of Speech (POS) tagging. Named entities usually have clear entity types (e.g, person, location, organization) and are usually locally distinct from other text. Thus, it might be easier to identify clean training and test datasets, and the confusion between supervision and guidance may not arise. I think the methodology could be more generally applied and validated through experimentation. I also have some reservations about the relevance of the methodology to POS tagging. I think the methodology might work better for Named Entity Typing (NIT) than Part of Speech (POS) tagging. Part of speech tagging often relies on local contexts, whereas Named entity typing might require more global considerations. For example, in the approach, the model assumes that the local entity types (e.g, person, location, organization) are globally consistent (e.g, all people should reside in a location), and this consistency should be inferred from data. I think this assumption might not hold in practice and the validity of the approach should be tested on another NLP task.

Spitzer Space Telescope observations of two early-type galaxies have revealed spatially extended, hot dust emitting at longer wavelengths than those detected in earlier, targeted observations of elliptical galaxies. The apparent locations, morphologies, and spectral energy distributions of these dust components are consistent with a simple model in which the dust was produced by a starburst event between 1 and 10 million years ago and was subsequently heated by a central engine, likely powered by an Active Galactic Nucleus (AGN). The presence of both cold and warm dust phases suggests that the dust has been subject to strong heating and cooling, perhaps caused by both shocks and AGN energy outflows. This new evidence of recent starburst-driven feedback in elliptical galaxies may have important implications for galaxy formation and the co-evolution of galaxies and their central black holes. Infrared Astronomical Satellite (IRAS) observations of these two elliptical galaxies revealed the locations of most of the dust that is now seen at longer (mid-infrared) wavelengths. These galaxies appear to have both cold (T≅20K) and warm (T≅500K) dust components, with an estimated total dust mass of a few hundredths of a solar mass. This warm dust is concentrated toward the centers of the galaxies, where it is associated with a dense network of thin starburst "PAH" features. The most energetically powerful AGN typically exhibit strong mid-infrared emission, but the origin of the cold dust component is not understood. The presence of cold and warm dust in these galaxies has led some to suggest that the star formation is episodic, with strong bursts of activity triggered by major merger events. If so, this may have important implications for the co-evolution of galaxies and their central black holes. Recent analyses of the centers of elliptical galaxies have revealed signatures of both cold and warm dust. Such dust, heated by an active nucleus, was first detected in the nuclei of the Sombrero Galaxy (M104) and of NGC 4486B (the "index" galaxy of the Virgo cluster). Subsequent IRAS and now Spitzer observations have detected dust in several other elliptical galaxies. Similar signatures have also been found in a number of lenticular and spiral galaxies, although with somewhat less certainty. In the case of M104, analysis of IRAS and Spitzer data revealed spatially resolved components consisting of both cold and warm dust, with possible evidence for strong heating and cooling, possibly from both shocks and AGN feedback. M104 is an iconic example of a "rejuvenated" elliptical galaxy (Tran et al. 2003), in which a major merger event between two spiral galaxies is believed to have triggered a massive starburst, heating and expelling dust from the galaxy center (Schweizer 1984; hereafter S84). The dust likely formed within 1-10 million years of the merger event, as evidenced by the similar ages of the newly formed massive stars and the re-radiated

Recent developments in harmonic analysis provide new computational tools for imaging. These tools, termed needlet methods, are flexible representations of multifrequency information and provide a unified approach to a wide variety of imaging techniques. In this work, we introduce two such methods for variational image reconstruction. The first, termed hybrid needlet frame Implementation, combines the two most popular needlet methods, the variational multitaper needlet and the variational wavelet needlet, into a single algorithm. The second method, termed hybrid needlet renormalization, allows the hybrid needlet method to be applied to an almost arbitrary needlet frame. We present numerical results to demonstrate the efficacy of these methods, and suggest potential extensions to volumetric imaging and other inverse problems.' Here is an abstract of a recently published article in the field of variational image reconstruction: Needlet algorithms for estimation in inverse problems Siddharth Koushiappas, Piotr T. Gaining parental trust can be a difficult task for any teenager. This is particularly true for teenagers who live with strong expectations and high standards. However, as a teenager, it's important to build a relationship with your parents by establishing a dialogue that includes your feelings and concerns. It's also important to follow through on your promises, do your chores and homework on time, and to take advantage of opportunities to show your maturity by doing things such as filling your parents' drink or making them breakfast. Above all else, respect your parents by speaking to them with confidence and by listening to what they have to say. If you follow these guidelines, you'll find it easier to build a meaningful relationship with your parents that helps you to feel supported and empowered to make good decisions. The text above is a sample article for a WikiHow article titled "How to Gain Parental Trust As a Teen". The full article is available here: [https://wikihow.com/Gain-Parental-Trust-As-a-Teen](https://wikihow.com/Gain-Parental-Trust-As-a-Teen)

This paper proposes a new deep learning architecture for small and middle scale applications, which is called Marginal Deep Architectures (MDA). The main idea of MDA is to learn layer-wise feature representation with the help of marginal Fisher analysis (MFA) and then to use some deep learning techniques to fine-tune the whole network. Experimental results on 7 small and middle scale real-world datasets show that MDA outperforms both shallow feature learning and state-of-the-art deep learning models. In high school, I did several projects that were student-designed. I have to admit, I wasn't too excited about them at first. I thought the class would be a snoozefest of busywork, but I was wrong. The projects actually gave me a chance to practice teamwork and work with people I didn't know, which were valuable skills for me later on in college. The first project I did was an ethnography. I was assigned to a school in the Dominican Republic, and my job was to observe how students spend their free time. I was surprised to see that many kids spent their free time studying. They had to wake up very early in the morning to take advantage of the school’s computer labs. Many of them had computers in their rooms, which were donated by the government to help the students study. The Dominican Republic is known for a pretty cool public education system. Students receive very little technology at school. However, we had some tablets donated by Apple, and I was able to provide computers and internet to a few schools. My first experience with coding (in a foreign language!) was great experience for me. It gave me a sense of purpose and responsibility. I was able to use what I had learned in high school physics, chemistry, and computer science to create an app that teachers could use to help students learn math. When I got back home, I released it to the public for free. I did some marketing myself and earned some money off of it. My next student-designed project was an interactive fiction (IF) story. We were given a fictional country and a real world problem, and our goal was to create a version of the problem in the IF game. We chose an issue related to pollution, so our IF game was about protecting the environment. To keep things interesting, we were given eight pages of suggestions for story prompts from a template. We had to choose a prompt, write a story with the prompt as a subtext, build a program that would evaluate our story in accordance with our writing prompt, and then submit our story to our teacher in digital form. It was a ton of work, but the end result was worth it: a unique, well-crafted story and a chance to exercise our creativity. The IF game is what really brought it home for me. I was finally able to apply what I had learned about game development, story telling, and how to work with users. After I released it, I got a lot of practice writing and editing text. My writing improved substantially, and I started to understand how to better communicate my ideas. My final student-designed project was a scavenger hunt. It was another team-based activity, and we were given a list of “treasure” items that we could use to track down a secret location. We had to communicate by text message, and we had to use our social skills to find the treasure and present the evidence to our team. It felt a lot like hide and seek, but instead of hiding, we had to organize a party or plan a treasure hunt! The scavenger hunt was my favorite. While we were searching for our treasure, we had to rely on each other to help narrow down the search space. We needed to work together to cover our tracks. Our teammate wasn’t going to believe we had found the treasure if we just blurted out where we were. We had to work together to make it believable. I loved the camaraderie we built during the competition. I think it’s important to learn how to work together, and I learned this in high school through my projects. The final product of all my student-designed projects was exciting to me. For most people my age, personal computers, tablets, and smartphones already feel like parts of their body. My creations will be a part of my legacy. My IF game could have changed the world, IF game has amazing potential for learning and changing minds! My scavenger hunt could have been a treasure hunt for a local child. My student-designed project was not a total waste after all. The only "limitation" of my student-designed projects is that I had to do them in high school. Many kids in the developing world don't have opportunities to use their skills in school or in the workplace, so my projects were a way for them to express their ideas and gain a sense of accomplishment. I wish more student-designed projects could exist for them. My favorite thing about student-designed projects is that they provide a chance to use what you learned in school. In high school, we were assigned projects that had to do with our home culture. Our “product” had to be produced in the United States. This means we were forced to think in terms of the United States. When we presented our work

Examine a second-degree burn for symptoms that call for medical attention. Consider the size of second-degree burns. Seek immediate medical attention for a third-degree burn. A third-degree burn affects both the epidermis and the deep layers of the dermis. Seek medical attention if necessary. First-degree burns and most superficial second-degree burns can be treated at home and heal fairly quickly. Flush chemicals out of eyes to prevent burns. Soak chemical burns in water. Soak thermal burns in cold water. Remember that thermal burns are caused by heat, not chemicals — whether from the sun, steam, or a hot object. Consider applying ice if cold water doesn't work. Do not apply burn ointments for the first 24 hours. Find over-the-counter benzocaine products. Benzocaine is a local anesthetic that numbs nerve endings in the skin, providing relief from burn pain. Take an over-the-counter pain reliever. Spread shaving cream over the burn site. Be aware of the limitations of natural remedies. Apply aloe to minor burns and sunburns. The skincare aisle at any grocery store or pharmacy will have many products that include aloe. Look for St John's wort cream products. Just like the aloe plant, the St. John's wort has anti-inflammatory properties. Use essential oils to treat minor burns. Apply honey to soothe pain and prevent blistering. Brew a calendula tea. Natural healers have been singing the praises of honey for centuries, and modern science agrees. Soothe a burn with raw onion juice. Protect the burned area. HSBC Sports and Social Club (commonly abbreviated to HSBC Sports Club) is a private social club and sports club located at 10 Saint James's Place in the City of Westminster, London. It was founded in 1928 by the Chinese American Hong Kong-based bank of the same name, and was originally located on Scott Street in Belgravia. Since 1986 it has been located on Saint James's Place. The club is best known for being the regular football team and rugby union club of British public school students, and also for hosting large dinner dances and events. It is a member of the Singapore Clubs Association, the London Clubs of London and the International Committee of Sports Clubs. The main football team plays in the Southern Football League, the fifth level of the English football league system. Other sports offered at the club include cricket, rugby, tennis and water polo. The club hosted the 1958 British Empire and Commonwealth Games water polo medal match between England and Australia. In 2010, HSBC agreed to pay £19.5m in settlement of a Serious Fraud Office investigation into allegations that it paid more than £30m in bribes to win business in East Asia between 1999 and 2004. HSBC Sports Club is the former headquarters of the British spy and Czechoslovakian exiles Jan Diala and Ján Kuciak, who uncovered the corruption of company officials and oligarchs at the bank, leading to the release of numerous documents by the International Consortium of Investigative Journalists and others relating to the bank's activities in the country. Kuciak and Diala were murdered in February 2020 by Kleik oscillation, suspected to be due to their work uncovering corruption. In February 2023, HSBC Sports Club moved to 31-37 St James's Street, London, along with other former headquarters of British companies, and buildings of the former Exhibition Road branch of the London County Council.

The store will usually be happy to make the one or two cuts you might need to get the dowels to a suitable length. Back home, lay the dowel on the floor in a rectangle, overlapping the corners a few inches. Lash each of the corners together with twine. Install four hooks in the ceiling approximately where the corners of the enclosure will be. Use some more twine to hang rectangle from the hooks. Install two shower curtains, each extending from the front of the enclosure to the back. Install the shower over the tub spout. To shower, slip between the shower curtains, and use the hand held shower. If you use PVC pipe, you can cut and lash them as described above, or you can also try the following method.

The West Haven station is a disused railway station in West Haven, Connecticut.The single track branchline of the New York and New England Railroad, it was a stop on the infamous “ shackle ” train, which transported Jehovah’s Witnesses from city to city, displaying an illustration and handing out religious materials on the way. The West Haven station was built in 1905 by the New York and New England Railroad at the junction of Boston Avenue and Parsons Hill Road. The land was owned by Townsend Whelen, an investor in the railroad, who provided financial assistance and access to the tracks. Ownership of the railroad was taken over by the New York, New Haven and Hartford Railroad in 1920, and then Penn Central in 1970. Penn Central worked to remove the deteriorated structure in the 1970s, but at a cost of $600,000. It was finally demolished in 1983. The site today is occupied by the New York and New England Railroad grade crossing and a gas station. This historic structure is widely regarded as an example of an underutilized urban rail site, and many have called for its redevelopment. On the other hand, local residents have expressed concern that any redevelopment would bring further crime and decay to the surrounding neighborhood. The West Haven station was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1983. Despite its historical significance, the site has long remained vacant. In the early 2010s, local resident Adam Phillips began advocating for its redevelopment as a public space. He secured grants for the preservation of the building’s trackside fencing and surrounding sidewalk, as well as support from state senator TonyG marten. On August 24, 2015, the West Haven Select Board unanimously approved a 99-year ground lease with Phillips for the property. The terms included Phillips having an unlimited right to assign the lease, with the goal of developing the site as a park. A plan to demolish the structure and replace it with an apartment building was announced in April 2017, but the project faced opposition from neighboring residents. Resident John Kane subsequently formed the non-profit group Friends of West Haven Landmark in opposition to the development. Kane and Phillips had a public dispute over the park’s potential effect on traffic congestion in 2017, but Phillips and Town Selectman Mike Lauriello reached an agreement regarding the traffic concerns in November of that year. At a public meeting in March 2018, a crowd of over 200 people spoke in favor of the redevelopment. Phillips' company, RPS Companies, was awarded the construction contract, and demolition of the building commenced in July 2018. The park opened to the public on June 14, 2019. The West Haven station was added to the New Haven County Register of Historic Places in 1983. It was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1983. The New Haven County Preservation Trust worked with state and local officials to encourage redevelopment of the site as a public space rather than a gas station. Rather than demolishing the former train station, the space was renovated to accommodate a small public park. A bronze statue of a Jehovah's Witness handing out pamphlets on a 1940s edition of the "Shackle" train was installed outside the renovated building in 2019. The park opened to the public on June 14, 2019. Today, the site is a public park which features a statue of Jehovah's Witnesses on the Boston Avenue pedestrian crossing. It is commonly used by local residents for spontaneous gatherings and events, including a monthly yoga class. New York and New England Railroad grade crossing gates on Parsons Hill Road allow the public to access the tracks. A fence on either side of the grade crossing is painted white from graffiti, including tags from high schoolers. Although crime has not been an issue at the park, local residents have expressed concern that any redevelopment would bring further crime. A public park was desired by many, including local resident Adam Phillips, who secured grants for the preservation of the building’s trackside fencing and surrounding sidewalk, as well as support from state senator Tony Marten. Saladin, also known as Salah ad-Din Yusuf ibn Ayyub, was a Christian Lord of Oultrejordain and a statesman of the Third Crusade. In the movie 'Kingdom of Heaven', he is portrayed by Sir Peter O'Toole. The depiction of Saladin in the movie is based on the beliefs of the time period, which depicted Muslim leaders as barbaric. In the era of the Third Crusade, when Saladin lived, it was uncommon for a Muslim leader to pick up a cross and place it on an altar. In many texts, the depiction of Saladin in the movie is shown to be a deviation from the historical truth. The behavior in the movie is more likely to be an artistic license, as the piety of Muslims was distorted in the times of the Crusades. Even though Saladin, a Muslim leader, is shown picking up a cross and placing it on an altar in the movie 'Kingdom of Heaven', based on historical facts, this is rarely if ever seen in the time of the Third Crusade. In the time of the Third Crusade, the Christians had adopted the practice of showing reverence to crosses, by lighting their crosses from the candles held by the priests. The behavior of Saladin in the movie is more likely to be an artistic license, as the piety of Muslims was distorted in the times of the Crusades.

Purchase a quality pair of brake spring pliers at your local automobile supply store. Pull the car onto level, solid ground. Remove hubcaps from the wheels you will be working on. Loosen the lug nuts with a lug wrench (tire iron) or impact wrench. Jack up the car. Remove the wheels. Remove the grease dust cap. Pull out the cotter pin. Remove the retaining nut. Inspect the drum. Attempt remove the drum. Retract the brake shoes. Remove the return springs with your brake spring pliers. Remove the retaining rings that hold the brake shoes in place. Pull the brake shoe off. Remove the emergency brake line. Re-install the emergency brake cable. Remove any parts from the old brake shoes and transfer them to the new brake shoes. Grease the backing plate. Slide the bottom spring onto the new brake shoe. Slide the show into place against the backing plate. Replace the retaining ring for the first shoe. Slide the bottom spring onto the second brake shoe. Put the brake shoe into its proper place against the backing plate. Fasten the adjusting spring onto the second brake shoe. Replace the retaining ring for the second shoe. Use two screwdrivers to set the adjusting screw. Put the drum back on. Replace any bolts that hold the drum to the hub. Reinstall the retaining nut and cotter pin. Put the dust cap back on. Re-install the wheels. Remove the jack stands and lower the vehicle to the ground using the floor jack. Tighten the lugs to the specified torque. Add brake fluid if needed. Test the brake. Thanks for reading and happy auto repairing! More tips for the care and maintenance of your automobile: How to Inflate a tires How to remove and install a speedometer How to change a car battery There are many tips and tricks to maintaining and repairing your vehicle. Follow all of these helpful tutorials to ensure safe and proper operation of your car. Brake systems, or brakes as they're commonly referred to, are essential to the safe operation of your car. Follow this tutorial to learn how to check, maintain, and replace your brake pads. How to check and replace brake pads Jack your car up, and remove the lug nuts using a proper lug wrench, jackstands, or car jacks if applicable. Remove the brake drum and rotor using the steps listed here. Check to see if you have a disc or drum brakes: If your car has disc brakes, you may hear a cracking or popping sound when you apply the brakes. This is normal and indicates that your brake pads are holding onto the disc effectively. If your car has drum brakes, you may feel a vibration when you apply the brakes and may see some light shaking of the vehicle due to the brake rotor. Install the new brake rotor and brake pads as indicated by your make and model. Reinstall the wheel and return the lug nuts to tighten. Check for any pending issues by driving the car and applying the brakes. If you need more help, here are some videos that can help you master the art of brakes: How to change brakes on a car How to install brake pads How to replace brake pads While your car is in for repairs, it's a good idea to have it inspected and tuned by a professional mechanic. Following this guide will teach you how to use your car's diagnostic port, reset the taillights, and replace the brake pads yourself. Diagnostic Port With the key in ACC or Rev Counter mode, or the key fob in remote mode, click the brake pedal. The dials on the instrument cluster will change to show battery voltage, electrical ground, brake and turn signal relays, andvacuum pressure. If you don't have the key fob in remote mode or the key in ACC or Rev counter mode, you can also do this by removing the crankshaft pulley on the engine. You can find this by removing the two brake caliper bolts and the locking washer, or by simply removing the pulley from the engine. Reset the taillights With the key in ACC or Rev Counter mode, or the key fob in remote mode, press and hold the brake pedal for at least 10 seconds. When the lights come back on, the taillights will also flash alternately between low and high beams. Replace brake pads With the key in ACC or Rev Counter mode, or the key fob in remote mode, depress and hold the brake pedal for 5 to 10 seconds. When the brake lights go out, press and hold the brake pedal for at least 5 more seconds. The brake pads need to be installed in the opposite direction that you removed the rotor

This paper presents a new algorithm for learning bilingual word embeddings that rely on mimicking the loss function of a state-of-the-art transformer-based single-task model, but instead using a zero-shot multitask setup, where we use no training data of the other language but only assume a small amount of data of each language is available. This can be an attractive setup for many language pairs for which huge amounts of parallel data is not available (e.g., Italian-English) or too expensive to acquire (e.g., bilingually recorded TV show transcripts). The proposed model, named XMERT, uses the transfer learning mechanism to capture useful information from the auxiliary task, which in this case is a language model (LM) trained on the large amount of data of each language present in the dataset. Extensive experiments show that the proposed model obtains improvements over the strong SOTA model, while using a much simpler setup and using very little data of the other language (less than 1K parallel sentence pairs for English-Japanese). I think this paper has an excellent idea, and presents it in a very well-crafted way. The model presented in this paper can be viewed as an adaptation of the multi-task, transfer learning setup to the task of bilingual word embeddings. Instead of sharing a common task-specific neural network among different language pairs, this model uses a shared code and has a language-specific output layer. The output layers of the different encoders are then used as the inputs of a soft sharing mechanism that adapts the common code to the specific languages in the dataset. This model uses the strengths of the shared code, which are guaranteed to be language-independent, while avoiding the weaknesses of the large amount of data assumption. The model is also simple, since it uses very little data of the other language (only a small amount of extra data is used for the LM, which can also be transferred from a different task). The evaluation of the proposed model is carried out on two datasets, with the same setup as the SOTA model. In all cases, the paper reports large improvements over the strong SOTA model, and in some cases (particularly for the smaller French-English dataset) the improvement is extremely significant. I recommend publication of this paper, even though I have some minor comments to address. First, there is some redundancy in the XMERT name and the "zero shot multitask" description. I suggest name one of these XM (pronounced "X-mer", as in "cross-mod") and leave the other name as is. Second, it would be good to provide a bit more discussion of how the language model (LM) is trained on the small amount of data of the other language (ELM). The language model is shown to have near-optimal performance when the only available training data for the other language is a small amount of data (e.g., 1K parallel sentence pairs). How is the small amount of data from the other language generated? Is it some kind of crowdsourced/bootstrapped dataset, or did the authors find a way to use large amounts of data from other domains (e.g., BooksCorpus) to train the language model? Or is it simply not possible to train a good language model on so little data? I've added a paragraph to the Discussion addressing these points. Finally, in the last paragraph of the Results, the authors mention that the small amount of data from the other language did not help when combined with the shared code part of the model. This is actually quite surprising, and I don't think this would be obvious to a reader without being told in the text that the XM model was also tested with a shared code setup. I suggest adding an extra paragraph to this section to clarify this. I've updated the Results section to mention the shared code setup. Finally, I also suggest including an additional small table that presents the results on the two small datasets. The table can be limited to just the top few methods, as there are only two entries. I've added this table. Overall, I think this is a very nice paper and I look forward to reading the source code to this model when it's published. I appreciate the feedback, and thank you again for recommending this paper for publication. ---!downs jdb_commenter: approve: true commenter: author date: 2020-05-20 17:04:28.000000000 +0000 UTC publication: None text: "XMERT: Multi-task Transfer for Bilingual Word Embeddings Without Parallel Data" uid: "Fz8Hd5Uxp9s" usage:

Slide-o-Cam (SOC) is a next-generation virtual slide microscopy system that generates high-fidelity virtual slides from microscopic images of microscope slides. The slide image capture, transmission, and processing are conducted in real-time with no mismatch in the illumination, color, contrast, and exposure between the virtual and real slides. The virtual slides are viewable on modern web browsers and can be annotated with rich graphics and measurements. Since virtual slides are generated from real slides, the labor and reagent costs are eliminated, and the process can be performed in high-throughput mode. Given the extraordinary demand for virtual slides, we describe a slide transmission strategy applicable to virtual slide microscopy. Slide shaving is the process of thinning the glass coverslip attached to a glass slide using either sharp-edged tungsten or carbide plungers or sandpaper, which often damages theslide surface. The SOC uses a diamond-impregnated rubber plunger to create a slide that is suitable for light microscopy, avoiding damage to the surface and allowing the capture of high-resolution images with good signal-to-noise ratio. Slide-shaving is only needed to create a usable slide for transmission; thus, the cost of generating virtual slides is substantially reduced. The SOC slide transmission is quantitatively compared with bright-field microscopy, and similar lateral resolution and signal-to-noise ratio are achieved with lower hardware costs and less effort. Since the SOC uses real microscopes and glass slides, the method can be easily extended to other virtual slide microscopy systems.

Dan Vasser is a technology entrepreneur, investor, and co-founder and CEO ofdatapad.com. He was named one of 50 Top Global Digital Leaders by Masters in Digital Marketing and Social Media at the London digital marketing festival, and one of 10 Influential People in IoT by The Internet of Things Industry. He's been a regular contributor to TechCrunch and IEEE Spectrum, and his work has been read by millions through blogs and social media. He's also a TED Senior Fellow, an advocate for Adopting technology, innovation, and leadership to achieve social change, and a longtime USA Today contributor. ABrand new high energy X-ray transient IGR J11321-5311 was discovered by the INTErnational Gamma-Ray Astrophysics Laboratory (INTEGRAL) on February 11, 2010. Subsequent rapid follow-up observations with other space and ground-based telescopes revealed that IGR J11321-5311 is a highly obscured OB supergiant, located in the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC). The distance to the source was estimated to be approximately 50 kpc (which is about 20 times closer than the Andromeda galaxy, M31). IGR J11321-5311 is the second example of a supergiant fast X-ray transient (sfxt), an uncommon class of X-ray transients that are accreting binaries hosting a highly obscured massive star. To date, six such systems have been identified. This is the first report of its kind for the LMC. The available data do not allow to firmly establish the nature of the compact object, if a neutron star or a black hole. The large column density and high spin period of the pulsar (inferred via periodicities in the quiescent X-ray flux) point to a neutron star. However, the high inferred mass transfer rate and peculiar properties of the pulsar (such as an unusually slow pulse period and a high absorption column density) could also be explained via the acquisition of material via stellar wind from a highly magnetized companion. Moreover, the source lies in a globular cluster which, given the highly old age of these objects, might also point to a black hole accretor. Finally, based on the large column density and peculiar X-ray variability, a possible explanation for this source could be that IGR J11321-5311 hosts a new subclass of ultraluminous X-ray sources (ULXs), dubbed “hyper-compact binaries”, with a compact star orbiting a neutron star or a black hole in a very tight orbit (less than a few tens of solar radii) and being significantly more obscured than classical ULXs.' The fast nature of the X-ray transient and the high luminosity (assuming a distance of 50 kpc) make this source a promising candidate to investigate the process of mass transfer from a companion on the highly-accreting regime. Finally, the upcoming sensitive monitoring with the currently operational modules of INTEGRAL (IBIS and JEM-X) as well as with other X-ray instruments (e.g. the proposed LOFT mission) is expected to provide a more detailed characterization of the physical phenomena taking place in this system, and to provide a definitive identification of the compact object.

If we put a male baby into a community that only consists of men and never show him what women look like until he is 20, he would likely be attracted to women's bodies and physical features, not their features or appearances. This is because during childhood and adolescence, males typically focus more on physical features and less on a woman's overall appearance. The only exception to this would be if the man were born with a congenitalchromosome disorder or disorder of sex development that significantly altered his physical appearance; however, this is unlikely as genetic disorders between the sexes are exceedingly rare. Putting a male baby into a community that only consists of men and never showing him what women look like until he is 20 would allow him to focus on women's bodies and physical features rather than their features or appearances. This would allow him to be physically attracted to women without initially having preconceived notions or prejudgments about women based on their appearances. Note, this answer assumes that the man is not born with a genetic disorder or disorder of sex development that significantly alters his physical appearance. It also assumes that the community in which he grows up does not show him women's pictures or tell him women's names until he is 20. The answer does not take into account the possibility that the community might show him women's pictures or tell him women's names before he is 20.

In today's world, using a cellphone while driving can be dangerous. In Georgia, the government takes this issue seriously and has made it illegal to use a cellphone while driving. This law has had a positive effect in decreasing the number of people who use cellphones while driving, because it prevents violators from being able to receive and respond to text messages and emails, which are the two primary uses of cellphones while driving. However, I believe this law could be improved by also prohibiting Google glass use while driving. Google glass is capable of video calling, which is one of the primary uses of using a cellphone. While this law would not prevent violators from using Google glass while driving, it would at least prevent them from receiving information through text messages and emails. my opinion on laws regarding cellphone use while driving my opinion on laws regarding cellphone use while driving should be strengthened to prohibit all phone usage. While laws regarding driving specifically ban using the phone for texting and calling, drivers often have fewer inhibitions picking up a phone and reading or sending messages or emails. An emphasis on readability could perhaps save lives by reducing distractions. as of January 1st, 2022, Georgia made it illegal to use a cellphone without being within 10 feet of the vehicle. However, it did not apply that rule to using a wearable computer like Google Glass or a computer screen like an iPad. This means that people who use these devices like Google Glass or iPad in driveways or other non-public places, such as coffee shops or restaurants, are not held legally accountable. This is a missed opportunity to prevent people who use these devices from being distracted when they are in public and vulnerable to being struck by a vehicle. in 2022, Georgia made it illegal to use a wearable computer like Google Glass or a computer screen like an iPad in a vehicle. This is a step in the right direction towards eliminating distractions while driving, which can lead to devastating consequences.

Yuka Tsujiyoko (tumor promoter) is a professional wrestling referee from Japan. She started her wrestling career at the age of 15 as a student nurse in her school's wrestling club. She trained at the Amazon Wrestling Academy, and was the youngest member of the main roster at 20 years old. She has worked for both New Japan Pro-Wrestling (NJPW) and Pro Wrestling EVE. In 2011, she was diagnosed with nasopharyngeal cancer, and given 2 years to live. She inspired the "Yuka TSUCHIYA" train campaign, where she was able to raise over 600,000 yen[1] for her medical fees, which was donated by the train company she worked for at the time, Gotetsu. In 2013, she beat the cancer, and continue to work as a referee. She is currently married to fellow professional wrestler Yuji Yasui, and have two children. She is one of the most beloved professional wrestling referee in the industry, and has been called "Wrestle-bellen" (German for "Wrestle bell") by the German press, as she frequently is the first person wrestlers ring the bell for their entrance. In June 2019, she received an Special Award from the Wrestling Observer Newsletter award "Wrestler of the Year", as well as the "Fans' Award". She is a highly popular character among wrestling fans, often being referenced or parodied by other professional wrestling celebrities. CM Punk, the former WWE wrestler, stated he wanted to bring her into the WWE, prior to his firing in 2012. She has also received offers to work in Japan's big three pro wrestling companies, NJPW, Pro Wrestling Noah, and New Japan Ace, but turned them all down, as she does not want to put her family through a long distance relocation, and the more far-away companies did not afford her the opportunity to perform at major wrestling shows in front of her family. She is the older sister of fellow professional wrestler Hikaru Tsujiyoko. She currently works for All Japan Pro Wrestling (AJPW), where she is the lead referee, and performs at most of their major shows. She is currently working on a book and documentary about her cancer experiences, titled The Education of a Wrestler, and her family's experience with the Japanese healthcare system. Her ring name, "Yuka TSUCHIYA", is written with the characters, which can be read as "Cancer Destroyer". She has been referred to as the "Japanese Chyna" (a reference to the female WWE wrestler Jacqueline, who worked under the ring name, Traci Brooks) or the "Japanese Tamina Benjamin" (a parody of the "Pomp and Circumstance" singer, "Tamina Benjamin"). On April 2020, she announced that she was pregnant with her second child, following a tryst with fellow referee Shigeo Harada, who was previously her boyfriend. She will return to the ring in November of that year for the first time in nearly five years, to work for NJPW's 100th anniversary show, but only as a referee, as she is still recovering from childbirth. She is the most memorable pro wrestling referee, and her wrestling career meant a lot to many people, even outside of the wrestling industry.

In this paper we examine the possibility of stable vector bundles over higher-dimensional spacetimes without gravity by considering the local structure of the gauge group on the fiber. In four dimensions, the necessary and sufficient condition for the existence of stable vector bundles with rank greater than or equal to four is the absence of global holomorphic vector bundles with similar characteristics. In higher dimensions, we generalize this condition using the Hodge number which governs the local topological properties of the gauge group. We find several higher-dimensional examples of stable bundles over a sphere and over a hyperbolic plane, including some with both extra spacetime and gauge symmetries. This solves several stability problems in supergravities and string theories with large extra dimensions. In particular, we consider a simple four-dimensional example with general covariance, general coordinate invariance, and with the gauge group structure $SU(2) \times SU(2)$ local on the fiber over a four-sphere. For this bundle, the necessary and sufficient condition for the existence of stable vector bundles with rank greater than or equal to four is the absence of global holomorphic vector bundles with similar characteristics. We further show that this bundle can be defined over a complex four-dimensional manifold, with the holomorphic structure induced by the gauge group structure. We also discuss several higher-dimensional examples. For a simple point particle in eleven dimensions we find a stable vector bundle with the gauge group $SO(5) \times SO(5)$, local on the fiber over a five-sphere. The necessary and sufficient condition for the existence of such a stable bundle is the absence of global holomorphic vector bundles with similar characteristics. We also consider the case of a five-dimensional torus which is a product of two four-dimensional spheres. In this case, we find that the necessary and sufficient condition for the stability of a vector bundle is that there are no holomorphic vector bundles of the appropriate rank and second Chern class.

In this paper, we present a large-scale life-long memory module for use in deep learning. The module exploits fast nearest-neighbor algorithms for efficiency and thus scales to large memory sizes. Except for the nearest-neighbor query, the module is fully differentiable and trained end-to-end with no extra supervision. It operates in a life-long manner, i.e., without the need to reset it during training. Our memory module can be easily added to any part of a supervised neural network. To show its versatility we add it to a number of networks, from simple convolutional ones tested on image classification to deep sequence-to-sequence and recurrent-convolutional models. In all cases, the enhanced network gains the ability to remember and do life-long one-shot learning. We set new state-of-the-art for one-shot learning on the Omniglot dataset and demonstrate, for the first time, life-long one-shot learning in recurrent neural networks on a large-scale machine translation task. This is a significant result, which shows the power of using a memory module to do one-shot learning, which as a strong generalisation ability. The paper also describes some of the limitations of the memory module, e.g, the need to reset it during training, and the requirement for it to be fully differentiable. These limitations may restrict the applications of the model. Overall this is a useful and thought-provoking paper, which may contribute to the future work on one-shot learning.

Quark-Gluon Plasma (QGP), the state of matter conjectured by particle physicists several decades ago, is the densest form of matter known to humans. It exhibits many remarkable properties, like color charge and net-baryon number fluctuations, that can in principle be measured in the LHC. While most of the properties of the QGP were confirmed in the LHC Run 1, the observation of the novel phenomenon of photon emission from the QGP, reported by the ALICE collaboration, remains a great mystery. In this work, using the kinetic theory of heat and angular momentum transfer, we show that the QGP can emit virtual photons with an intensity that depends only on the temperature and the properties of the plasma, obviating the need for a dynamical calculation within the standard model of particle physics. The virtuality of the emitted photons, defined as the square of the momentum scale at which the induced propagator vanishes, is determined by the temperature, and hence, the emitted photons may possess the energy and virtuality required to explain the ALICE anomaly. The proposed mechanism is not constrained by Lorentz invariance, and therefore it can operate in any QGP-like system that exhibits large momentum scales and non-trivial thermodynamics. In order to explore this mechanism, we make use of the uniqueness and robustness of the QGP thermodynamics to perform predictions for photon emission from strongly coupled plasma, which we confront against available data from lattice QCD. We find that the predicted photon rates are sufficient to resolve the ALICE anomaly, should it persist upon the completion of the Run 2 of the LHC. The ALICE collaboration has recently reported photon emission from the QGP in Pb-Pb collisions at the CERN LHC [1]. This observation has remained a mystery to the scientific community for over three decades, as most of the expected QGP signatures were observed in the first run of the LHC. However, the observation of photon emission, with an energy of approximately 9 GeV, and at an average rate of 1010 GeV-1 s-1, has remained a mystery. The emission of real photons is a Quantum Electrodynamics (QED) process, whereas the emission of virtual photons is not constrained by the symmetries of the standard model of particle physics, and may occur via a myriad of different processes. The paper Verb Physics: Relative Physical Knowledge of Actions and Objects describes an associative memory model for learning physical relationships between objects and actions. The model is designed to leverage the commonly used natural language description of an action and paired images of the action. The model is trained on a large dataset of image-verb pairs and tested on how well it can generalize new image-action pairs. The model shows promise in being able to generalize new image-action pairs by extracting general physical relationships between objects involved in the action. Here is a link to the paper: https://www.pnas.org/cgi/doi/10.1073/pnas.1904709116 Strength: The model presents an interesting combination of learning physical relationships between objects using paired images and natural language description, potentially a useful application in the robotics space. Weakness: The paper only shows results on a small dataset and provides limited experiments and results on how well the model generalizes to physical relationships between new actions and objects. Overall, the paper presents a potentially useful application of a machine learning model that can learn physical relationships between objects from paired images and natural language descriptions. However, the model only shows results on a small dataset and the generalization to physical relationships between new actions and objects is not thoroughly tested and does not appear in the paper. Overall, this is a potentially useful application of a machine learning model that can learn physical relationships between objects from paired images and natural language descriptions. However, the model only shows results on a small dataset and the generalization to physical relationships between new actions and objects is not thoroughly tested and does not appear in the paper.
