In this paper, we propose a quantum repeater architecture capable of supporting long-distance, fault-tolerant quantum communication over broadband metropolitan networks. To accomplish this, we introduce a novel technique for combining low-dimensional entanglement and quantum fast forwards to scale computation. Our system design is optimized for several key metrics: entanglement generation rate, consumable resources, memory usage, and pair production rate. The entanglement is generated between lightmatter qubits in a quantum frequency quadpter, distillation of the shared entanglement is accomplished in parallel on a cluster of nearby quantum computers, and a quantum signal reconstruction step is completed at a metropolitan node. Through numerical simulation, we show that this system can achieve high-rates of entanglement generation and consume a small number of quantum photons and matter qubits. Introduction Quantum repeaters use quantum entanglement to enable long-distance quantum communication. Entanglement is a quantum property that permits two or more particles to have correlated properties, even when separated by a large distance. Quantum repeaters use entangled quantum signals to distribute entanglement over long distances. Because of the exponential decay rate of entanglement with distance, long-distance quantum communication requires repeaters that extend the entanglement over long distance. Current approaches to quantum repeaters use individual systems, each optimized for a specific range of distances. Systems optimized for short distances, such as quantum key distribution (QKD) systems, achieve high rates of entanglement generation through detection of either quantum side-channels or modification of qubits in transmission. These systems optimized for long distances, such as quantum memories, achieve high rates of entanglement generation through purification of largelyentangled quantum systems. Both systems require local adaptation of the transmission process, which limits the distances over which entanglement can be distributed. In this paper, we present a quantum repeater architecture that combines features from these optimized systems for short and long distances. The design is intended to support long-distance entanglement distribution over broadband metropolitan networks. It makes use of parallelization to scale the communication overhead from entanglement generation to quantum memory usage, and low-dimensional entanglement between light and matter qubits to achieve high rates of entanglement generation at low quantum resource consumption. Entanglement generation is facilitated by using light-matter entanglement, where matter qubits interact with light in quantum frequency quadpters (QFQs). The QFQs are fully integrated on-chip networks that support multi-user connectivity and may be instantiated using silicon photonics. These networks support entanglement distribution over long distances by enabling parallelization of purification protocols. However, the purification rates in previous approaches were limited by the need to localize the entanglement generation to specific purification registers. In this design, we propose a metropolitan node that combines long-distance entanglement distribution with fast forwards, a form of quantum communication that allow a quantum signal to be reconstructed despite loss. In this paper we consider the problem of variable selection in the linear regression model. We compare several algorithms for this problem, starting from a forward stagewise approach, which is known to be consistent, and ending with the recent nonconvex approach of cyclic and global minimization of the regularized loss with the $\ell_1$ norm. For the forward stagewise approach, we consider two versions: the standard version, which is also known to be consistent, but is known to select many false variables, and the modified forward stagewise algorithm, which has the advantage of not selecting many false variables, but might be inconsistent. For the nonconvex approaches, we use the penalty of Kurdyka and Mitrushak (2001) with the special case of totally monotone convolution kernel, which we call the monotone lasso. We show that this method is consistent and has an oracle property with the true model being a local minimizer of the penalized loss. We also show that this method is order-optimal for the convex case and prove its non-convex convergence.

In this paper, the author addresses the problem of how to accelerate the training of neural networks by efficiently approximating the first order gradient of the loss function with respect to the weight initialization. The technique they propose is quite clever. It decomposes the learning process into multiple small learning steps that are easier to compute. For each small learning step, it estimates the weight update using an adjoint formula. In particular, it solves an optimization problem whose objective function is the loss, and the variables are the weights. This approach is surprisingly effective. When it is applied to deep learning models, it can often reduce the number of effective iterations by a large factor. Moreover, it usually converges very fast. As a result, the training can be speeded up significantly. In the paper, the authors provide several effective techniques to implement this adjoint-based optimization. The last section of the paper is a discussion on the implications and future work. The main strength of this paper is the clever idea of learning weight updates using an adjoint formula. For untrained readers, a key supporting proof in the paper is too long to be included here. But the technique of learning weight updates using an adjoint formula has never been published before. It has a high likelihood of being patented, so the authors chose to post it online instead. The paper also has some weaknesses. First, the technique seems to only be applicable to a certain type of models. For example, it doesn't work well for regularized models with $\ell_p$ norm regularization, where $p>1$. Also, the technique does not work if the loss function is not differentiable. Despite these weaknesses, the paper is very interesting and the techniques seem very promising. I believe this paper will inspire many researchers to try new ways to apply adjoint methods in the field of machine learning. Here is a more detailed discussion on this paper. The paper Introspection: Accelerating Neural Network Training By Learning Weight Evolution introduces a novel approach that uses an adjoint method to compute the first order derivative of the loss function w.r.t. the model parameters. Let us denote the loss function as L(θ, x; y) where θ are the model parameters and x is the training sample. The paper first reduces the learning process into small learning steps. For each small learning step, it estimates the weight update using an adjoint formula which is a function of the loss function L(θ, x; y) and the current weight initialization θ. More specifically, the weight initialization is optimized using an optimization problem with an objective function of the loss function L(θ, x; y) and the constraint of the parameter norm. The idea is quite clever because each small learning step can be efficiently computed because it's a function of the loss function and the current weight initialization. One can approximate the whole optimization by solving the optimization for each small learning step. When applying this to deep learning models, it usually reduces the number of effective iterations by a large factor. Moreover, it usually converges very fast because the algorithm will converge to the optimal weight initialization which can't be violated by the small weight updates. As a result, the training can be speeded up significantly. The paper also has some weaknesses. First, the technique only applies to a certain type of models, i.e. models with first order gradients. So it doesn't work for regularized models with L2 or L1 regularization, where p > 1, or models without first order gradients. Despite these weaknesses, the paper is very interesting and provides a promising direction for weight initialization in deep learning models. This may spark some interests for other techniques for weight initialization. Paper Link: https://arxiv.org/abs/2001.04342 Strengths: This paper's main idea of using an adjoint method to compute the first order derivative of the loss function with respect to the model parameters is very clever. It has a very high likelihood of being patented. Weaknesses: The paper only applies to a certain type of models, i.e. first order gradients. So it doesn't work for regularized models with L2 or L1 regularization, where p > 1, or models without first order gradients. Overall, this paper is very interesting and provides a promising direction for weight initialization in deep learning models. It is recommended for users who are interested in this topic. The electoral college is a powerful tool that enhances the election process in the United States. The system has the advantage of increasing voter turnout by requiring voters to first vote in their state before proceeding to the next states. It also gives smaller population states more weight in the presidential election compared to larger states. Additionally, the electoral college provides for a two party system, as it takes at least 2% of the vote in each state for a candidate to be elected without the need for a runoff election. This prevents fringe candidates from gaining power disproportionate to their popularity. However, there are several shortcomings to the electoral college. First, lying by a candidate could result in an invalid election, as the candidate could purchase enough electoral college votes to be elected. Second, millions of Americans could be disenfranchised, as they will not live in a state, or even a part of a state that the candidate won. Despite these shortcomings, the electoral college is not without fault. States that are strongly Democratic or Republican often favor the election of that party, making it hard for third parties to get a foothold. Additionally, the system does not reflect the popularity of candidates, as it is simply the sum of the states' populations. What's more, many people complain that the presidential election is no longer decided by a candidate's abilities, but rather their "likability" and the "optics of the campaign." Despite these concerns, the electoral college is a strong system that promotes democracy, fairness, and the election of mainstream candidates. It should be protected and left untouched.

To add the Like button to your blog, first visit the https://developers.facebook.com/docs/plugins/like-button. Scroll down to Like Button Configurator. Enter your blog’s address into the “URL to Like” box. Then enter your desired plugin width into the “Width” box. After that, select a button style from the “Layout” menu. Then select an action from the “Action Type” menu. After that, select a size from the “Button Size” menu. Then check or uncheck the “Include Share Button” box. Then click Get Code. After that, log into your Blogger account, then click Template. Then copy the code from the first box of the Facebook Like Button Configurator. Then paste the code into your Blogger HTML code. Replace every “&” in the pasted code with “&”. Then click Save Template. After that, log into your Blogger dashboard, then click Layout. Then click the Add a Gadget button where you want the button to appear. Then select HTML/JavaScript. Then type facebook under “Title.” Then paste the copied code into the “Content” box. Then click Save. After that, click Save Arrangement.

Overall, this is a very interesting and well-presented paper. The proposed GPV substantially improves theParagraph Vector as a representation learning method by allowing it to learn representations for unvisited texts from the context provided by the training examples. The generative model is learned by maximizing the joint log-likelihood of both the supervised and unsupervised data, which is done through an alternating optimization between the generators and discriminators. The model is evaluated on five classification benchmark datasets and found to outperform both the previously introduced GAN-based generative models and the previously introduced Paragraph Vector based classifiers. performed both a quantitative and qualitative analysis of the learned text embeddings, showing that the generative model allows the model to learn representations that are useful for downstream classification tasks. supervision is introduced by feeding a few labeled examples into the generative model during the training, which gives the model the ability to infer the representations for texts that are not in the training set. The use of both labeled and unlabeled data in the training maximizes the chance of the model generalizing well beyond the training data, thus making the model more robust against over-fitting. The combination of both labeled and unlabeled data makes the model more powerful and useful for a variety of tasks that may not have been originally envisioned during the design of the model. dernly, I think the proposed GPV is a very interesting approach to distributional representation learning and should be a main contribution of the paper. The contributions over the previously proposed GAN-based generative models are more obvious and should be highlighted. For example, there are very good comparisons of GPV with state-of-the-art GAN-based generative models, such as DC-Text andBigGAN, in the section of Limitations and Future Work.

Please follow the steps in the image below to properly sharpen your knife: 1.Place the stone vertical to your body, one short side closest to you, one short side furthest from you. 2.Take your knife in your sharpening hand (right-handers in your right hand, lefties in your left) and lay the knife flat on the stone with the edge facing toward you. 3.Tilt the back of the knife up until the front-most part of the edge makes contact with the stone. 4.With the knife in the position described in the previous step, rub the knife back and forth across the stone until a slight burr begins to form. 5.Turn the knife over so the edge faces away from you and grind exactly the same as in the previous step. 6.Repeat step 4 but continue grinding until a visible and significant burr is formed along the entire edge of the knife. 7.After the burr is formed, repeat step 4 but this time use a fine honing stone India Sharpening Stones. 8.Repeat step 5 until the burr is completely flipped over to the scraping side. 9.Position the blade perpendicular to and flat on the stone with the tip of the blade hanging over the side of the stone and the edge of the knife closest to you. 10.Turn the knife over and repeat the previous step, this time with the edge of the knife away from you; the stroke also going away from you. 11.Test the edge on your thumbnail.

Malnourishment causes loss of muscle mass, which is primarily composed of protein. Protein is what makes your stomach look bloated. In order to combat this effect, your body will first attempt to retain as much water as possible. As a result, you will likely see a slight increase in weight, height, and circumference of your stomach. This effect is most noticeable in severely malnourished individuals. Additionally, a change in metabolism may also cause your heart to look larger than normal. Your heart undergoes physiological changes in order to sustain high levels of exertion in severe malnourishment. Lastly, loss of fat and water stores can lead to a loss in overall body weight. However, the individuals in this case may have an overly positive appearance. Their face may appear gaunt, as opposed to their stomach looking bloated. This is because their body is trying to sustain life through their muscles rather than through reserves of fat and water. Malnourishment is a very serious problem that frequently occurs in underdeveloped countries. Approximately 30% of the world's population suffers from malnutrition in some form. Because many people live off of a single income, malnutrition can easily occur when more food resources are not produced to meet demand. Additionally, malnutrition is a major cause of mental retardation and certain types of cancer, which can lead to decreased productivity at work and an increased risk of infection. Due to the severity of the problem, the international community has created numerous organizations to prevent and treat malnutrition. The Union Worldwide Fund for Breastfeeding, Baby and Child Growth (World Breastfeeding Week) has worked to combat the problems caused by low levels of breast milk, which can lead to malnutrition. The International Labour Organization and World Health Organization have both produced documents on safe food practices and good nutrition, respectively. The World Food Programme is a United Nations organization that works to ensure people have access to food. Overall, malnutrition is a very real problem that can occur to anyone, regardless of their location, economic situation, or profession. The major causes are often out of an individuals' control. However, by staying educated on the causes and symptoms, you can be better prepared to identify the problem and take action should you encounter someone suffering from malnutrition. Malnutrition, or lack of food, is a global issue that affects millions of people around the world. Malnourishment can lead to physiological changes in the body, such as bloated stomachs and larger heart patterns. Malnourishment can also lead to decreased IQ and increased risk of cancer. With 30% of the world's population suffering from malnutrition, it is an issue that affects many people and needs to be addressed worldwide. Major causes of malnutrition include lack of food resources, which can occur in underdeveloped countries, and overpopulation, which can lead to a lack of food resources. Other major causes include infectious diseases and lack of clean drinking water. With so many different causes,

China's population increased dramatically between 1960 and 1985 due to massive rural-to-urban migration. In particular, the "Hundred Flowers" and "Cultural Revolution" programs saw millions of people move from the countryside to the cities for work, vastly improving rural living conditions and leading to a baby boom. The one-child policy was enacted in 1980, but many couples were allowed to have two children afterwards. There was a cultural preference for boys, so many parents had one child because they knew they could have two children later. From 1985 to 2020, China's population remained relatively steady because the one child policy was not as strictly enforced and many couples were only able to have one child. However, the one child policy was eventually repealed in 2020 and the population of China has dramatically increased once again. It is estimated that China's population will reach 1.4 billion by 2050. Most experts believe the increase is due to the elimination of the one child policy and the rapid improvement of Chinese reproductive technology, which has led to multiple children for many families.

Environmental effects on galaxies are known to have profound impacts on their evolution. Indeed, the large-scale environment in which a galaxy resides dictates where it can efficiently capture dark matter and new stellar mass, affecting both the rate at which baryons can collapse into the potential wells of dark matter halos and the transport of gas to the galaxy. Additionally, the nature and spatial distribution of neighboring galaxies can also affect the evolution of a galaxy through both internal processes, such as mergers and galactic harassment, and external processes, such as mergers and accretions. These processes can induce, trigger, or enhance signatures of active galactic nuclei (AGN) activity, which in turn can fuel new star formation as well as heat and eject gas from galaxy reservoirs. Thus, the large-scale environment in which a galaxy resides acts as a catalyst for its evolution, driving both internal processes, such as reionization and AGN feedback, and external processes, such as mergers and accretions.

Requiring students to participate in extracurricular activities can provide a number of benefits to both the students and the educational system as a whole. By requiring students to participate in a variety of extracurricular activities, students receive exposure to a diverse range of perspectives, experiences, and skills that expand their knowledge of different people and communities and can help them build better relationships with people from all walks of life. Additionally, by encouraging students to participate in extracurricular activities, schools can promote a culture of achievement and support motivated and driven students, as well as help students learn to manage their time and balance academic and extracurricular responsibilities. The positive effects of requiring student to participate in extracurricular activities outweigh the potential harms, and it is recommended that schools adopt policies to promote and encourage student participation in these activities. myself: I personally feel that schools should require students to participate in extracurricular activities. Enforcing student participation in extracurricular activities can have a number of benefits, such as promoting diversity, learning how to better manage time and resources, and supporting motivated and driven students. In addition, by encouraging students to participate in extracurricular activities, schools can help create a supportive learning environment, promote a culture of achievement, and encourage students to manage their academic and extracurricular responsibilities. I believe the potential harms of not requiring student participation, such as students being encouraged to avoid activities that may be difficult or unappealing, are out-weighed by the benefits of requiring student participation.

In today's fast-paced world, many students struggle to balance schoolwork with their personal schedules. Oftentimes, students say they do not have enough time to participate in extracurricular activities such as sports or clubs because of the large amount of schoolwork they have due. As a result, these students end up performing poorly in their schoolwork and miss out on experiences that could help them develop valuable social skills or business acumen. On the other hand, some students argue that requiring students to participate in extracurricular activities, such as sports, could hinder their education because it interferes with their performance on the current trimester. In response to this, schools should instead foster students' social skills and business acumen by allowing them to participate in extracurricular activities after school rather than requiring them. By allowing students to participate in extracurricular activities after school rather than requiring them, students will have more time to devote to their classes and personal schedules while also learning social and business skills. In conclusion, requiring students to participate in extracurricular activities may not be the best approach to fostering students' social skills and business acumen, since the additional requirements can get in the way of their education and learning. On the other hand, fostering students' social skills and business acumen by allowing them to participate in extracurricular activities after school will not interfere with their education and learning and is a better approach to fostering students' social skills and business acumen.

Overall, I enjoyed this paper. The topic was interesting and you introduced an interesting case study of using a programming language for education. I also think that the writing could use some work. I found that the writing was often wordy and verbose. Particularly later in the paper, the writing tried to explain some subtle points with words a non-technical reader could understand. I think you could trim some of the fat from the writing without compromising the points you were trying to get across. I also found it difficult to keep track of the characters used in the acronyms. I had to look up how the acronyms were defined multiple times. I would recommend defining the acronyms up front and then using the acronyms consistently throughout the paper. The last point I would make is that the case study of the adoption of a programming language for education. I think there are a lot of fascinating aspects about the adoption of the Elm programming language. I would encourage you to focus more on the theoretical aspects of the adoption and less on the very specific implementation of the language. I think the specific implementation details could be placed in an appendix. Overall, I would recommend improving the writing and trimming some of the fat from the paper. I think there are interesting ideas in here worth exploring. Thanks for your feedback! We have edited the paper to address your points. Abstract This article analyzes the adoption of a programming language for education from the perspective of a “user experience” that took place over a 10-month period. We discovered that the process was “natural” for students and teachers. They quickly internalized the basic concepts, and the students’ grades improved considerably. In addition, the experience was extremely educational, allowing students to practice concepts across all curriculum areas and learn basic system administration skills. In the process, we identified several shortcomings of the process, some of which are general to all large-scale implementations of new technologies and some of which are more specific to the implementation of a programming language. We believe that these lessons will be useful to anyone attempting to introduce a new technology into an education environment. Body The Elm programming language was chosen as a candidate for introduction into an existing curriculum. The rationale for using Elm was that it has a simple grammar and word-based syntax that allows students to quickly learn the concepts involved in programming. As described by its proponents, Elm’s use of a functional language with declarative code provides many of the benefits of mathematical notation without the pitfalls of abstraction. In addition, the immediate feedback of quick-check exercises allows students to easily locate mistakes and correct them in the fast-forward mode required in today’s world of “programmable knowledge.” The Elm implementation in the Ohio Department of Education’s (ODE) computer science course, “Computer Science 1A,” started with the first quarter in fall 2014. The implementation involved a total of 1700 students in first through sixth grades. We examined the experience in terms of: (1) students’ adoption of Elm; (2) student learning of the basic concepts of programming; (3) grades of students in the course; and (4) the student learning within other curricular areas. Students’ adoption of Elm was excellent, with almost all students having used the language at some point by the end of the year. The rapid student uptake was not a surprise, as students were already comfortable using basic expressions to describe transformations of graphs representing real-world entities. Student feedback was positive and, in most classes, most students completed a quick-check exercise in less than five minutes. The students rated the exercises very highly with an average score of 3.96 out of 5.00. In addition, they learned enough Elm to complete more advanced exercises and to start writing their own programs. The feedback was that the Elm was “easy to learn” and that the “language had some similarities to English.” The learning of the basic concepts of programming was variable, but was encouraging. In classes using a teacher-laptop, the time from introducing Elm to a basic level of programming increased from quarter to quarter. In other classes, the students reported the difficulty with block modeling in the first quarter. Nonetheless, the students were able to solve basic problems using various strategies, such as “if statements” and “if-then-else” constructs. Students were even able to begin to break down problems into discrete steps and eventually to describe “subroutines” in a few quarters. As with student feedback, the students’ opinions were positive, with students finding the exercises very helpful for “remembering” concepts and for locating and correcting mistakes in their work. Most students were able to complete the exercises in a few minutes (5 to 10 minutes) except when they could not understand how to correctly use a block.

Public schools provide an important foundation for children's education by teaching students vital skills such as cooperation, competition, stress management, and acceptance of diversity. By exposing students to a diverse range of people, cultures, and ideas, public schools help students develop a sense of acceptance and belonging. In addition, through proper exercise of competition and cooperation, students learn how to work with others to meet common goals. Finally, because public schools foster an atmosphere of openness and trust, students learn to manage their stress levels and develop a positive attitude toward learning. Because public school systems typically receive more resources per student than private schools, they often have larger class sizes and more funding for teacher training, supplies, and equipment. As a result, students benefit from receiving more one-on-one attention and instruction. In addition, the larger class sizes often allow students to develop relationships with their peers as well as with the teachers. These personal connections make students less likely to misbehave in class or skip homework assignments. In summary, public schools provide an important supplement to a child's home education by fostering important skills and promoting responsible behavior. Because of their ability to foster a positive environment and develop the essential skills in our children, public schools should remain accessible to all. implemented in America today. However, public school systems are not universally regarded as an effective way to educate children. Often, parents simply prefer to home educate their children, either due to philosophical objections to centralized educational institutions or financial reasons, such as the cost of running a home for a child. In any case, in the United States, public school enrollment has been falling for several decades now. This is interesting, given that public school systems are designed to help students develop the skills of acceptance, cooperation, competition, and stress management, all of which are essential to participating in American society. On the one hand, not everyone agrees that public schools are an effective way to promote these critical skills. Competing business can and do educate their workers at home, citing the low costs and flexible schedules as primary advantages. Additionally, the K-12 public school system requires students to spend many years in a facility that is not their home. Even if that place happens to be a classroom, groups of students are rarely allowed to roam free. As a result, these critics argue that a public school system is meant for a child, not a human being. What's more, because children are uniquely vulnerable and impressionable, a traditional public school setting may not present lessons in the ways that are developmentally appropriate. For example, explaining sexual education to children too early or at all may traumatize them. In contrast, a home environment provides children with the flexibility to explore topics at their own pace and in an environment specifically designed to support their physical, emotional, and mental well-being. In addition, homeschools can be tailored to the needs of individual students, allowing them to learn social and academic skills at their own paces.

Explainable pre-paid mobile phones could be a good alternative to provide students with safe access to their parents in emergency situations. For example, a student could set up an alert on the phone to send a text message to their parents upon detection of an injured arm. Additionally, the phone could include a button that allows students to contact authorities (e.g., police, ambulance, principal) in the event of any unrecoverable student disappearance. Furthermore, the phone could log GPS coordinates (from a global satellite network) for reporting lost or stolen phones, and the phone would require a PIN to bypass the screen when using in class. This would enable the teacher to keep track of students who have lost their phones and could limit the phone usage during class time, thereby minimizing distractions and ensuring that students are adequately supervised during important lesson presentations. While the proposed system could ensure safety and security for students, it also raises other concerns. For instance, students could try to find ways to exploit this system for getting away with more unsupervised time. An example of this would be that students would abuse this privilege to watch TV or play online games, which would reduce the actual learning time. Therefore, the introduction of this technology into the current education environment should be implemented with clear policies, as well as procedures to monitor and enforce those policies. Overall, it is clear that mobile phones can offer benefits to students in certain circumstances. However, before deciding to implement such a system, it is crucial to clearly define goals, analyze the potential impacts and address these concerns with specific policies and procedures. This approach could create a framework that allows the implementation of useful technology within the school environment, while avoiding concerns such as privacy, security, and abuse.

This is a complex question that cannot be answered simply with a yes or no. There are many factors that can affect how we think or perceive things, and the language we speak is only one of them. Other factors include our upbringing, environment, education, and socio-economic status. In some languages adjectives are before nouns, in others, after -- does this, or other differences, change how we think or perceive things? According to cognitive scientist and professor of psychology, Dr. Elizabeth Baetjer- indexing languages into one of these categories does not affect how we think or perceive, but instead, can affect how we communicate. As Baetjer notes, “Most of the world’s population resides in countries where language is not a primary organizing factor. To communicate, people in these countries simply mix and match aspects of different languages. If you learn Spanish from a young age, for example, it will typically be the adjective before the noun: ‘El hombre era guapo’ (‘the man was handsome’).” On the other hand, in countries like Iran and Afghanistan, adjectives follow the noun. This can cause issues when speaking with people from other countries where adjectives come before the noun. However, Baetjer says “there is no evidence that language affects cognition,” and adds that in her opinion, “such comparisons are tenuous at best.” While these differences in language can cause issues when communicating with people from other countries or regions, Baetjer says the differences are largely insignificant. “My view,” Baetjer says, “is that differences like these are far less critical than the obvious differences in people, cultures, and environment.”

Mental health issues affect a significant portion of the population and can cause a myriad of psychological disabilities. These disabilities can severely impact a person’s capacity to function in daily life and work. As such, people with mental health issues often encounter difficulties in schooling, which in turn can have a negative impact on their ability to receive the education they deserve and their career opportunities. In recent years, online education has grown significantly. With the spread of high-speed internet and mobile broadband, it has become easier and more convenient to attend school from home. Additionally, with the increase in prevalence of online classes, there has been an increase in people choosing to take their schooling this way. Despite the benefits, online education still has several drawbacks. First, a lack of live interactions can reduce the social interaction benefits of learning in-person. Furthermore, some people feel more comfortable learning in a casual environment and not being judged as they might in an online class. In addition, students with disabilities are often left out in the COVID19 pandemic, as online classes have not been allowed for students with disabilities. With the increasing prevalence of mental health issues and their possible negative effects on someone’s education, it is paramount to ensure that there is adequate access to quality education for everyone, particularly those with mental health conditions. One way to provide the necessary education to all is to adopt a mixture of in-person and online learning. By providing the best of both worlds, the social interaction benefits of in-person learning can be fully realized while access to education is maximized for all students. For those that require a more immersive environment, online education can be augmented by having teachers visit a local classroom and deliver online education. With the adoption of this approach, the potential benefits of in-person learning can be fully realized, while the technical and economic barriers of distance learning can be overcome.

There are arguments for both teacher-designed and student-designed summer break projects. On the one hand, many students learn best by watching tutorials, so it is natural for teachers to guide students toward achieving their own learning goals. It can be hard for students to be open to new ideas if they are not attracted to the content themselves, but it is important that students leave the safety of school to experience diverse ideas. On the other hand, giving students complete autonomy over their projects frees them from the daily constraints of school and may allow them to try new ideas without fear of repercussions. Projects that are truly student-designed may give them the ability to try more innovative and creative ideas, and letting students decide how to spend their time can help them avoid procrastinate. If I were designing summer break projects, I would aim for a balance between these two extremes. In most cases, I would give students some basic guidelines about what kinds of projects are suitable (e.g. they should be meaningful to the student, easy to complete, and safe for the environment) but then encourage them to design their own activities. I would make sure to emphasize to students that they can try new ideas and develop projects that are truly their own. At the same time, I would expect some level of guidance from me, the teacher, to make sure students do not stray too far from the guidelines and that they have enough resources to complete the projects. By giving students some structure but allowing them the freedom to think creatively, I believe this approach can maximize learning and student ownership of their projects. While summer break is a time for students to gain independence, most students do not feel ready for this kind of responsibility and need some help managing their time and learning new material. Asking students to complete their own projects helps students develop self-management skills that can be useful for the rest of their lives. Since most students do not enjoy the summer unless they have a project to work on, giving students some choice over the type of project can help them engage with the break. On the one hand, a wide choice of projects allows students to find a topic they are passionate about and makes it less likely that they will procrastinate on a dull project. On the other hand, projects that are too open-ended may allow students to spend the whole summer working on something without ever truly achieving completion. By carefully considering the choices that students have, I hope to give students the autonomy and opportunity for student-designed projects but with the structure needed to ensure completion. In summary, I think both teacher-designed and student-designed projects bring value to the classroom. The specific projects selected should consider the needs of the students and the instructor, as well as student and teacher time available. However, in most cases, I believe that student-designed projects provide the best balance between quality and efficiency. References: [1] Ewing, R.P. & Christensen, C. (2009). Essentials of learning sciences. New York: Longman. [2] Hattie, J. (2009). effective teaching. London: Continuum.

Summer Projects? Should They Be Teacher-Directed or Student-Directed? There are advantages and disadvantages to each approach to the selection of projects for the summer. On the one hand, a student may not fully appreciate the relevance of a topic or the nitty-gritty of a complex subject if the student doesn't have a sense of how the topic could or should affect real people. On the other hand, a teacher may not convey enthusiasm for a topic or be able to convey the importance of a topic in a way that inspires a student's interest. A well-designed project should be able to engage a teacher's skills as a teacher and a student's interests. In favor of student-directed projects: In order to truly capture the imagination and the potential of students, a project should allow students to tap into their imaginations and find relevance to their own lives. This is not always the case with teacher-designed projects, where the content is often in the realm of the mundane and the student's individuality isn't given the opportunity to flourish. In favor of teacher-directed projects: A teacher is, first and foremost, a professional, and a professional in charge of a particular field. This implies that the teacher knows more about the field than anyone else, including other professionals. A teacher has the knowledge, experience, skills and contacts needed to find a particularly interesting project that students would otherwise never discover. This is advantageous for the development of a student's understanding of certain topics and of how they can apply what they've learned to their own lives. Thus, in conclusion, both student-directed and teacher-directed projects can have benefits, and which approach to adopt should depend on which elements are more useful to emphasize. Follow-up discussion: In summary, there is some overlap between the advantages of both the student-directed approach and the teacher-directed approach, but the advantages of each approach are sufficiently distinct that either approach could be considered the preferable option. It depends on the goals of the student and the type of teacher that the student wants to emulate.

Select an appropriate story introducing the cooking activity. Know your children's dietary restrictions. Consider your cooking resources. Select an appropriate food activity for making with a group of small children. If possible, prepare "recipe cards" which are large cards with pictures of each step you are completing in the recipe. Be sure to use food health and safety procedures. Talk about how to measure and why you are measuring (pre-math). Ask open-ended questions of the children, such as what will happen when we put this in the oven? Be sure each child gets a chance to "help" in the activity. If you have a large group of children, more than three or four, plan to do the activity more than once, dividing the children into groups. Let the children help with the clean-up, too. Even though pre-school children cannot yet tell time setting a timer and asking them to watch the time with you, helps them to begin to develop a sense of time. Point out that you must complete the steps in a recipe in a certain order to get the desired end product. When the recipe is complete, enjoy the food with the children and congratulate them on their creation.

The main content of this paper is Steerable Convolutional Neural Networks, it has achieved state of the art results on the CIFAR image classification benchmark. The paper builds upon the work of F.G. Scholkopf who first proposed steerable filters to be used in Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). The main idea of this algorithm is that the representational capacity of a layer in a neural network should be restricted to the types of representations that are most useful in solving the specific classification problem at hand. They introduce steerable filters to effectively exploit the symmetry of natural images, which allows a steerable filter to be parameterized by a small number of learnable parameters. This small number of parameters implies that steerable filters can be efficiently trained and their learned representations can be easily transferred to new images. This general approach of using structural information about the signals being represented to design specialized filters is known as structural Risk Minimization (SRM). They design several layers of steerable filters to build a network with multiple layers of SRM units. They evaluate their method on the CIFAR 10 and CIFAR 100 datasets, and achieve better or comparable performance to other state of the art techniques. This paper is interesting because it applies this idea of SRM to the deep learning architecture of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to improve their performance. Steerable Convolutional Neural Networks build multiple layers of SRM units using steerable filters. This allows it to have fewer parameters and still achieve competitive performance to competing methods on CIFAR. The fact that it is a parameter-efficient deep neural network which can be trained using less training data is especially appealing. This research is also significant because it unifies a large body of earlier work on steerable representations. They build upon the work of Scholzkopf who proposed steerable filters to be used in Convolutional Neural Networks. They introduce steerable filters to effectively exploit the symmetry of natural images, and build multiple layers of SRM to achieve a competitive performance to other methods on CIFAR. The fact that it's parameter-efficient deep neural network which can be trained using less data is especially interesting. This paper is technically sound and the research question it asks is significant. The idea of applying structural knowledge about the signals being represented to the design of specialized filters is of interest to the deep learning community. The main problem I see with this paper is that although it has achieved state of the art performance on CIFAR, there is no work on the question of why this approach works, which means this is an empirical study and is less theoretical. Theories and evidence are important in explaining and justifying experimental results. Another related point is that because this is an empirical study, rather than building upon a strong theory, there are fewer restrictions on the design of steerable filters. More importantly, the types of steerable representations that can be achieved are not fully explored. We see that they achieve good performance by using multiple layers of SRM. However, it's not clear why this approach of adding layers of SRM is effective. There could be many other combinations of steerable filters and SRM that can also be effective. It's not clear if a principled way to search through all these possibilities has not been considered. Although the main idea of applying structural knowledge about the signals being represented to the design of specialized filters is interesting, the application of this idea in this paper does not achieve the full potential of this idea. Therefore, the paper is only a partial exploration of this idea. Overall, this paper is an interesting exploration of an under-explored application of a strong idea, and I think it would be an interesting future work to fully exploit the potential of this idea.

The Electoral College, or EC, is a voting system used in 14 states and the District of Columbia to elect the president of the United States. The EC also selects the vice president, Cabinet members, and federal judges. The EC was created as part of the Twelfth Amendment to the United States Constitution, ratified in 1804. The EC has both benefits and drawbacks. The EC gives smaller states a significant voice in presidential elections. Smaller states have equal representation in the EC as larger states. This makes it more likely that more people from less populated areas will be represented in the EC. Also, the EC gives less populated areas a greater influence in the presidential election. For example, the president of the United States, Andrew Jackson, was elected with only 29 percent of the electoral vote, even though more than 50 percent of the population voted for him. The EC also reduces pressure on candidates to campaign in less populated areas, because less populated areas are less able to swing an election. Despite the benefits, the EC also has drawbacks. It can prevent candidates from gaining widespread appeal if they don’t have strong support in large states like New York or California. Another drawback is that the EC gives an individual power to a small number of people, because electors simply vote for the candidate they were told to vote for by their state. If enough people do not back the same candidate, that candidate may not receive enough electoral votes to win the election. The EC can also encourage negative campaigning, because negative campaigning works best when the candidate’s opponent has a base of support. Negative campaigning could encourage negative messaging targeted at swing states, which risks dividing the voting population. The EC’s winner-take-all system was another point of contention in the 2012 presidential election. Mitt Romney won the popular vote by about 1.2 million votes. Yet, based on the EC, Barack Obama won the election. A presidential election winner needs a majority of electoral college votes to win. Had Romney won a few more electors, he would have won the election. The EC’s winner-take-all system raises concerns about democracy. The EC gives greater power to a small number of people who reside in a small number of states. The EC also encourages negative campaigning, which can risk dividing the voting population and discouraging political diversity. The winner-take-all system of the EC may also encourage negative campaigning and discourage political diversity. In the 2008 election, John McCain won the popular vote but lost the EC to Barack Obama. Had the EC not given so much power to a few key states, Barack Obama would not have been elected in the first place. In conclusion, the EC has both benefits and drawbacks. It gives more power to less populated areas, but the EC also encourages negative campaigning and could discourage political diversity. Because of this, the EC could discourage political stability and increase uncertainty in American democracy. Therefore, the EC needs reform. The Electoral College should be replaced by a popular vote. Votes should be divided by Congressional district, then state legislative district, then county district. Voters would choose a person rather than a candidate. This method would make it less likely that negative campaigning would discourage political diversity, and encourage political stability.

I strongly support the adoption of distance learning programs in schools. Distance learning can promote student learning and engagement by providing an alternative way for students to receive and share content during inclement weather conditions, or local emergencies that affect classes, such as extreme weather conditions or local emergency, such as fires or explosions. Offering distance learning programs can promote equity by permitting students who live in remote locations, for example, to attend school without the need to drive for hours, which may not be feasible during inclement weather conditions. Distance learning may also promote accessibility for students with disabilities by permitting these students to access educational opportunities using technology, such as through online courses or video conferencing. The adoption of distance learning programs in schools also promotes innovation by permitting institutions to trial new pedagogies and educational strategies that might be more suitable for some students. Additionally, schools that offer distance learning are able to centralize resources, such as textbooks and educational materials, and share those resources across multiple schools, which can reduce cost and permit broader access to learning materials. Finally, the adoption of distance learning permits teachers to customize learning for individual students, such as by providing tutoring or mentorship, especially for at-risk students. Distanced learning programs can promote student engagement by permitting the sharing of content during inclement weather conditions or local emergencies. Offering distance learning programs permits students who live in remote locations to attend school without the need to drive for hours, which may not be feasible during inclement weather conditions. Distance learning permits access to educational opportunities for students with disabilities by permitting these students to access educational opportunities using technology, such as online courses. Distanced learning programs can promote student learning by permitting institutions to evaluate new pedagogies and educational strategies. Distance learning permits teachers to personalize learning for individual students by permitting tutoring or mentorship, especially for at-risk students.

Buried is a performance art form that was popularized in the 1970s by the German artist Rudi Xantho. The performer(s) create an ensemble of temporary structures, such as chairs, tables, or other light frameworks that are buried in the ground and covered with shrouds, papier-mache heads, or other decorative objects. The buried structures can include false compartments, false bottoms, or internal structures that are intentionally hidden from view. The ensemble is left in an exhibit space for a predetermined time, and then the hidden structures are gradually uncovered or revealed to the audience. The reveal can be intended to reflect the passage of time, the exposure of the structures to different environments, or some other development. The motivation for creating the ensemble is often a central idea related to the concept of burial. The burial often refers to the subjection of someone or something to an undesired or cruel fate, and the motivation creates a literal or symbolic burial site for a idea, a concept, or a entity. The buried objects can include personal belongings, symbols of authority or power, or references to contemporary or historical events. The form can also be used to express a sense of irremediable loss or grief. Buried has been used as a means of protesting against social injustice, exposing racism or religious persecution, and promoting art. The first professional exhibition of Buried was held in Cologne in 1975. Since then, numerous practitioners have created successful careers in the performance art form. The first U.S. exhibition was held in Santa Monica, California in 1979. By the end of the 20th century, the form had achieved international recognition and was included in many museum art catalogs. It has been the subject of multiple publications and documentaries, and the burial sites of some of the original artists have become notable landmarks.

Geostationary orbit (GSO) is the exact orbital position at which a satellite will appear to remain stationary relative to a fixed reference point on the Earth's surface. Strictly speaking, GSO is not a single orbit, but rather a region around the Earth in which each point defines an orbit approximately round the Earth in the opposite direction from the way the Earth rotates. The name "geosynchronous" is applied to any satellite that continuously occupies GSO, because its travel around the Earth makes it appear to "follow" the rotating Earth in its sky projection. The optimum distance for a geostationary orbit to stabilize the satellite around the Earth is determined by the Equatorial Geostationary Orbit (EGO), which is a circle around the Earth with a radius of approximately 36,000 km at the Equator. At this distance, the combined gravitational effects of the Earth, the Moon, and the Sun cause the orbit to remain approximately circular and stabilize the satellite's position in the sky. A satellite in an equatorial GSO will remain above the same point on the Earth's surface (the Equator), and thus any equipment on the satellite will be able to communicate with equipment on the Earth normally via radio waves, avoiding the need for satellite-broadcast communication channels, which are limited to the locations between the Equator and the North and South Poles.

Studying Venus could yield significant benefits such as learning more about another planet in the solar system and potentially discovering new resources that could be beneficial on Earth. However, there are several risks that could result from studying Venus, such as increasing tensions between Earth and Venus due to limited understanding of the planet and the potential for technology transfer. In addition, there may be consequences regarding the protection of delicate planetary environments. Another risk is that studying Venus might bring up ethical questions regarding potential exploitation of the planet. Despite the potential risks, there are a number of ways that scientists could study Venus and mitigate some of the challenges. One method is to study Venus from Earth with high-resolution imaging satellites, which could allow for global monitoring of vegetation and surface conditions. There are also concerns that Venus might have potentially habitable regions in the form of stable areas on the surface. If these regions are identified, it may be possible to provide support to assist with planetary habitability in order to minimize any potential negative consequences resulting from studies on the planet. It is also important to note that if Venus could help improve understanding of our home planet, it would contribute significantly to humanity's future endeavors. again Studying Venus could yield significant benefits such as learning more about another planet in the solar system and potentially discovering new resources that could be beneficial on Earth. As of today, there is no cure for tinnitus. However, scientists and researchers across the world are hard at work on developing treatment options. Some progress has been made, but there is still a long way to go before a cure is discovered. In the mean time, management of tinnitus is a huge priority for many people. They spend thousands of dollars each year on tools and devices to help manage the symptoms. Management of tinnitus is a huge priority for many people. They spend thousands of dollars each year on tools and devices to help manage the symptoms. Some of the most popular items they purchase are: white noise machines, relaxation CDs, sound Therapy, and high-tech notifiers. Tinnitus is very loud random noise in the ear similar to constant noise allergy. It can impact a person's quality of life and they will do just about anything to manage it. References: 1. https://www.researchgate.net/publication/236813446_Tinnitus_Related_Disabilities_And_The_Role_Of_Psychoeducational_Therapy 2. https://www.researchgate.net/publication/265966806_Effect_of_tinnitus_management_on_the_quality_of_life

I believe that driverless cars should be developed despite the potential liability issues and inefficiencies in changing traffic laws. The societal benefits of driverless cars cannot be understated. Nearly 1.2 million people are killed each year in automobile accidents. Driverless cars could greatly reduce this number due to their ability to drive automatically at higher speeds on busy city streets and avoid dangerous situations such as those caused by distracted or drunk driving. Driverless cars could also greatly increase the ability of disabled people to travel. With the help of artificial intelligence, driverless cars could detect and maneuver around emergency vehicles, disabled cars, and other obstacles. Additionally, the potential liability issues and inefficiencies in changing traffic laws could be ironed out as more and more driverless car companies are developed, creating a virtuous cycle. Furthermore, driverless cars are not yet a fully developed technology. As with any new technology, there will be hiccups along the way, but with sufficient investment, these bugs can be fixed. An optical lattice is a periodic standing wave of light with wavelengths corresponding to the spacing between the barriers in a laser optical lattice. By using a Bose-Einstein condensate as a quantum wave reservoir, it is possible to control the effective strength of the optical lattice. Here, we report an experimental measurement of the speed of sound of an optical lattice, revealing a good agreement with the theoretical prediction. The obtained result can be used for monitoring and controlling the dynamics of the condensate in the optical lattice. Authors: D. Skryabin and A. The Variable Star One-shot Project (VSOP) is an international collaboration aiming at detecting extreme planet-crossing binaries (ECBs) from photometric data released by diverse astronomical surveys. VSOP is a planet-search project, whose target characteristics are highly planetary systems in which a frequently dimming/flares star, called the star-planet system central star (CSS CS), is gravitationally bound to an extremely low-mass companion in a tight orbit. The system resembles a binary star, but the lightcurve of the CSS CS is instead driven by the companion star. VSOP searches for new extreme planet-crossing binary CSS CSs from space-based wide-field surveys and time-domain observations. It also supplies public Kepler light curves for follow-up observations. VSOP is a global collaboration involving more than 20 Chinese and foreign partners. The first data release of VSOP includes CSS CS candidates found from the light curves of the Catalina Sky Survey (CSS), Pan-STARRS1 (PS1), and Kepler spacecraft. The CSS CSs found by VSOP so far share several similarities with the recently announced rare astromechanically cool stellar systems. The expected yield of CSS CSs from VSOP is about one per month.

This paper addresses the problem of generative model identification in the context of natural image synthesis. Specifically, the authors introduce a technique called Distilled Variational Loss (DVL) that minimizes the effect of external factors on the final result. The strength of this paper is clearly demonstrated through the rigorous experiments and analyses performed to compare DVL against state-of-the-art methods. Although DVL demonstrated state-of-the-art performance on this task, the researchers also point out some of the challenges associated with the application of this approach to more complex domains. aly This paper studies the problem of identifying the generator in a deep learning model, i.e. finding the function that converts a noise input to a natural image. The main problem in this setting is that the model was not trained on any objective measure of naturalness and thus cannot be optimized with respect to that goal. The paper shows that distilling the information from a separately trained measure of naturalness can help in this setting. Another problem is that the performance depends on the choice of this extra measure of naturalness. mix The main idea of this paper is the distilling of information from a separately trained measure of naturalness. The idea of this measure is to generate the noise and then optimize it with respect to a fitness function that measures how natural the generated image looks like. The generated image with the highest fitness is then the desired result of this approach. The strength of this approach is demonstrated through the rigorous experiments and analyses performed. The main challenge is related to the choice of the fitness function.

In this paper we propose a quantum algorithm for the classical approach to the graph isomorphism problem using a quantum walk. This approach, due to Hell and Neubauer, is based on a classical algorithm due to Bron and Kerbosch that constructs an explicit mapping between the vertices of the two graphs, using a combination of depth-first search and transitive closure. We describe the quantum algorithm in detail and provide an experimental quantum implementation on small test graphs. The obtained results indicate that our approach is promising from the perspective of the application of quantum computing to computational problems. In recent years, driverless cars have received a lot of attention from industry and government organizations. Proponents claim that the cars will improve the lives of passengers by reducing human errors and vehicular accidents, thus enhancing safety. Automated vehicles will be guided by artificial intelligence (AI) algorithms that enable the cars to make correct decisions when making rapid adjustments to the environment. Such vehicles can avoid collisions with other vehicles or pedestrians. However, the sudden implementation of driverless cars could create many other kinds of issues. For example, the cars will lack the critical social skill of interacting with people and communicating. Also, the lack of a human element may allow the cars to become compromised by lobbyists or have their interests promoted by special interest groups. Since human error is a factor in many accidents, the death toll could rise significantly with the introduction of this new technology. The roads would then be littered with bodies of dead pedestrians and cyclists. Such a scene might greatly impact public opinion about driverless cars, making them the source of negative perceptions. Furthermore, not every human can be trusted with a vehicular interface. The massive deployment of driverless cars may put many people out of work and subject the general public to unfair risks. There will certainly be many unforeseen consequences, but the sudden change from driver control to autonomous navigation will disrupt the economy and the people’s lives. However, it could be argued that it is not fair to assume that humans are infallible. People make mistakes all the time, even under the best of circumstances. Since self-driving cars are designed to make an error less probable, they can reduce the probability of error while not eliminating it. The greatest value of human life comes from experiencing and learning from mistakes. Since errors are an unavoidable part of human existence, by eliminating errors through automation, humans will be relieved of a huge responsibility and be freed up to pursue higher purposes, such as improving their skills. It can be said that in exchange for being freed from mundane chores, humans would have more time to devote to their families, hobbies, and more noble pursuits. Furthermore, in some cases, driving directions may be difficult to follow, due to construction zones, unpaved roads, or other unexpected circumstances. Humans may make errors at these times too, which will probably be noticed by self-driving vehicles. In this case, the autonomous car is not at fault. Instead, an unforeseen event has interfered with driving directions. In this case, humans can ask other human drivers for help. Some people may view these two types of errors differently, so it is very possible that an “error” could be seen as an error one day and not an error the next. The ability to perceive a hazard and react adequately is a prerequisite to full autonomy. While self-driving vehicles have significant processing power, their sensors and cameras may not be able to perceive all aspects of the environment. In addition, dynamic factors cannot be ignored. Current self-driving technology is not yet able to master every kind of driving situation or handle all conditions perfectly. Whether intentional driving or otherwise, all driving entails some level of risk, and human drivers face all sorts of potential hazards. Taking these factors into account, it is not possible to achieve absolute safety in all circumstances. Thus, it is safe to assume that driverless cars will have errors too, although these errors will be fewer and less harmful than those committed by human drivers. It seems almost certain that driverless cars will become a reality in the near future. The increase in processing power and development of algorithms to support driving directions will likely result in more precise, safer, and more efficient autonomous vehicles. Moreover, society must be prepared to make the necessary changes to protect people, such as installing safety systems in all vehicles. The reality is that if we wait for the problems to develop fully before taking action, we may miss out on this great opportunity. It would not be fair to future generations to fail to explore this option and leave them with a post-apocalyptic world. Autonomous vehicles have the potential to alleviate traffic accidents, make driving safer, and increase efficiency. The implementation of this technology should be expedited to benefit society as a whole. While there will certainly be many unexpected consequences, the benefits are too numerous to ignore, and the risks are tolerable compared to those we have now. In summary, I strongly support the use of autonomous vehicles. I believe that given a chance, these vehicles could significantly improve safety on our roads, reduce traffic accidents, and substantially increase the efficiency of car travel.

The unprecedented resolution and dynamic range of modern cosmological simulations has allowed the quantification of several previously-unsuspected discreteness effects. We focus here on those which could potentially affect the validity of the cosmological paradigm, namely the inability of standard algorithms to properly follow the behavior of dark matter particles past the moment at which they become progressively confined to collapsed structures. We find that these “shell-crossing” events induce systematic displacements in the spatial distribution of dark matter particles of up to several tens of kiloparsecs, which are long-lasting and require hundreds of Hubble times for reestablishment. These so-called “ discreteness effects” may therefore represent a significant obstacle to using dark matter as a cosmological tool, potentially leading to biases on scales comparable to or larger than those observed in current galaxy surveys. In this companion paper we quantify the extent to which these previously unquantified effects affect current cosmological data.

If Earth was oriented like Uranus, its strange axial rotation would have very different effects on weather and seasons than they do on Neptune's moon, Triton. Earth's axial rotation, which takes about 24 hours to make one full rotation, causes day and night and therefore heating and cooling. Night would fall near the south pole and day would spread northward. This cycle of night and day would cause extreme temperature fluctuations and erratic weather. On Triton, the dominant axial rotation, in almost nothing like Earth, spins about twice as fast and takes about 40 days to make one full rotation. This slow rotation means that day and night would be almost completely static. In fact, if Earth and Triton both had axial rotations like this, seasons wouldn't exist because there would be no variations in temperature. However, the odd gravitational pull of Uranus would cause an extreme variant of the Triton effect. Triton's small mass and axial rotation would cause its two halves to fly away from each other at an insane pace. In fact, Uranus would likely pull Triton apart. So, the combination of axial rotation and the odd gravitational pull of Uranus would likely result in the immediate destruction of any moon or other object Triton had, as well as any moons Uranus may have. The escape velocity on Triton would be small given its low mass, and the escape velocity for a moon like Triton would be even smaller.

In a court of law, the diary of an accused person is admissible as it is their own account of the events that took place. The accused is not forced to write this diary nor is an editor present to ensure best practises. The diary is therefore unbiased and the word of the accused is taken as true. Furthermore, the diary can be considered as primary evidence as it was written shortly after the fact has occurred. It is therefore suitable to be submitted into evidence. There are many more aspects that could be covered here, but this is intended to be a short response to the question posed. In today's world of instant communication and wide spread social media, it's difficult to have an unbiased account of one's actions. Hearsay reports and second hand accounts of one event happening to another person are common. In a court of law, such accounts are considered to not be reliable and are generally not accepted as evidence. A simple example would be an accusation of sexual harassment. The accused person could easily deny the accusation and claim the accusation is motivated out of hatred or a desire for revenge. In such cases, the diary of the accused could provide an unbiased and reliable account of what happened.

Mahesh Anand (born 14 August 1973) is an Indian actor, director and producer who works in the Hindi film industry. He is well known for his villain roles and is most popular for his role of Vicky in the 2000 hit Krrish. He has acted in over 200 films and is a recipient of two Filmfare awards, five International awards and six prizes at the Moscow Film Festival. He debuted in 1996 in a Bengali movie Apanjan and gained popularity with Ram Gopal Varma's biographical film Mr.G. The film was a success at the box office and gained him critical acclaim. Subsequently, he appeared in several movies like Quarester, Dil To Pagal Hai, Agnipariksha, Koi... Mil Gaya and many more. He established himself as a leading actor in 2000 with Krrish, a science fiction action film featuring Hrithik Roshan in the title role. He played a biological terrorist whose DNA mutation gives him superpowers. The film was a commercial success and was declared a masterpiece by many critics. He then played negative roles in movies like Rise: story of a soldier, Dhan dhansu, Daud, Main aur bahar and Ram @ Ramda, where he played antagonist. In 2014, he made his directorial debut with the Marathi film Henna. In 2017, he played a bahadur in WarCTOR operated films Thief of Baghdad and The Duel. In 2018, he played a supporting role in Baaghi 3. In 2019, he played the lead role in Raj Kumar Gupta's directorial debut Sarkar 3.

While it is generally more efficient to drive at higher speeds for shorter periods of time, the answer to this question depends on variables including the vehicle's engine size and characteristics, fuel efficiency at different speeds, and the effects of both acceleration and brake fuel consumption. In general, the bigger the engine and higher the speeds, the more efficient driving for longer is compared to shorter high-speed stints. On the other hand, if you're in a vehicle with a smaller engine or poor acceleration or braking performance, then the shorter, higher-intensity stints are likely to be more fuel efficient. In general, driver efficiency will decrease with smaller engines and higher speeds. However, the decline in efficiency with increasing engine size is more rapid for smaller engines than for larger ones. For example, the 5.7L turbo-diesel from the 2019 RAM 1500 provides relatively consistent fuel efficiency at 70mph to 90mph, with a sharp increase in consumption at higher speeds. At similar speeds, the 2011-2015 model years of the Jeep Grand Cherokee provided better efficiency than the 2019 model year RAM 1500. In addition to the engine size, fuel efficiency also varies significantly based on driving speed. For instance, the 2011-2015 Jeep Grand Cherokee and the 2019 RAM 1500 all provide the same estimated fuel economy at 70mph (9.4 and 9.0 miles per gallon of diesel), but the 2011-2015 Jeep Grand Cherokee is estimated to provide better fuel economy at 90mph (6.8 vs. 7.8 miles per gallon of diesel).

Write as if you were explaining this to a child. Use punctuation and capital letters properly. This is how you play dodgeball. You hit the ball over there, you catch the ball over there, you run here, you jump there, you throw the ball over there. Always watch your surroundings, as the person who catches the ball could possibly throw it back at you. Dodge, throw, run, jump, and catch. Those are the basic ingredients to becoming a good dodge ball player. Practice these and you will be able to throw and catch a ball quickly, jump high, and run fast, and even though you might get hit by the ball, you will be able to keep running and dodge it. And most importantly, have fun!
